    Types --> BaseObj[BaseObjectType]
    Types --> SensorType[SensorType<br/>Properties + Methods]
    BaseObj --> SensorType
    Motor --> SpeedCtrl[Method: SetSpeed rpm]

    classDef obj fill:#e7f5ff,stroke:#1971c2
    classDef var fill:#fff9db,stroke:#f08c00
    classDef mtd fill:#f3d9fa,stroke:#862e9c

    class Root,Objects,Types,Views,Reactor,Motor,BaseObj,SensorType obj
    class Temp,Pres,Alarm var
    class SpeedCtrl mtd
```

**Explanation:** OPC UA's address space is a **graph of nodes**. Each node has attributes (BrowseName, DisplayName, Value, DataType, etc.). Variables carry the data, Methods expose callable functions (e.g., `SetSpeed(rpm)`), and Types define reusable semantics. This rich **information modeling** is the cornerstone of OPC UA's dominance in Industry 4.0.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram: MQTT QoS-2 Exactly-Once Handshake State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> P_Idle
    P_Idle --> P_WaitPubRec: PUBLISH sent (DUP=0)
    P_WaitPubRec --> P_WaitPubRel: PUBREC received
    P_WaitPubRel --> P_Idle: PUBCOMP received
    P_WaitPubRec --> P_WaitPubRec: Timeout вҶ’ retransmit PUBLISH (DUP=1)

    state B {
        [*] --> B_Idle
        B_Idle --> B_Received: PUBLISH received
        B_Received --> B_Committed: PUBREL received
        B_Committed --> [*]: PUBCOMP sent
    }
```

**Explanation:** The publisher's state machine (left) and the broker's state machine (right) are coupled by the 4 control packets. The DUP=1 flag on retransmissions, combined with the in-flight set, ensures **at-most-once application-level processing** of each message.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ June 2024]
**"Differentiate between MQTT and HTTP as application layer protocols for IoT. Mention any three points."**

**Model Answer (3 marks вҖ” 1 mark per point):**

1. **Architecture:** MQTT follows a **Publish/Subscribe model** with a central broker, whereas HTTP follows a **Client/Server Request/Response model**. (1 mark)
2. **Transport & Header Overhead:** MQTT uses a **2-byte fixed header** over TCP (port 1883/8883), while HTTP carries **variable, large text headers** (often > 200 bytes) over TCP (port 80/443). MQTT is therefore far more bandwidth-efficient. (1 mark)
3. **Push Capability & Power:** MQTT natively supports **server-to-client push** (broker вҶ’ subscriber) which is critical for real-time alerts, whereas HTTP requires workarounds like long-polling or WebSockets; this makes MQTT more **power-efficient** for battery-operated IoT devices. (1 mark)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023]
**"What is the role of a Broker in the MQTT protocol? List the three Quality of Service (QoS) levels in MQTT."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Broker** is the central server in the MQTT architecture that decouples publishers and subscribers. (ВҪ mark) It receives all published messages, **filters them by topic**, and **forwards** them to all clients that have subscribed to matching topics. The broker does not interpret the payload; it only handles message routing, session persistence, and QoS handshake bookkeeping. (1 mark)

The three QoS levels are: (1ВҪ marks вҖ” ВҪ each)
- **QoS 0 вҖ“ At most once** (fire-and-forget, no ACK)
- **QoS 1 вҖ“ At least once** (PUBACK possible duplicates)
- **QoS 2 вҖ“ Exactly once** (4-step handshake, no duplicates)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each вҖ” Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** In the 2024 Scheme ESE, Module-3 questions typically carry **14 marks** with two sub-parts (a) and (b) of **7 marks each**. The student is expected to write at least 3вҖ“4 pages with diagrams and tables.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) вҖ” **MQTT and CoAP Deep Dive**

**(a)** Explain the **MQTT protocol architecture** in detail with a neat diagram. Describe the **three QoS levels** with their message exchange sequences. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (3 marks):**
MQTT follows a **Publish/Subscribe messaging pattern** built around a central **Broker** (server). The key components are:
- **Publisher:** IoT device/sensor that produces data and sends it to the broker tagged with a hierarchical **Topic** (e.g., `factory/line3/motor1/vibration`).
- **Subscriber:** Application or device that registers interest in one or more topics with the broker.
- **Broker:** Central server (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ, AWS IoT Core) that performs **topic-based filtering** and forwards messages from publishers to all interested subscribers.
- **Topic:** A UTF-8 string with `/` as the hierarchical separator. Wildcards `+` (single-level) and `#` (multi-level) allow flexible subscription patterns.

```
+--------+         +---------+         +----------+
|Sensor  | PUBLISH | MQTT    | PUBLISH |Dashboard |
|Publisher|---->   | Broker  | ------> |Subscriber|
+--------+ topic=X +---------+ topic=X+----------+
                          |
                          | PUBLISH
                          v
                    +----------+
                    |  PLC     |
                    |Subscriber|
                    +----------+
```

**QoS Levels (4 marks):**

**QoS 0 вҖ” At Most Once:** No acknowledgment. Publisher sends PUBLISH packet and forgets. Lowest overhead (2-byte header + payload). Suitable for non-critical telemetry like ambient temperature logging.

**QoS 1 вҖ” At Least Once:** Publisher sends PUBLISH; Broker responds with PUBACK. If the publisher doesn't receive the PUBACK within a timeout, it retransmits with DUP=1. **Guaranteed delivery, but duplicates possible** (the receiver should be idempotent).

**QoS 2 вҖ” Exactly Once:** A 4-step handshake ensures the message is delivered to the application layer **exactly one time**, with no duplicates:
1. PUBLISH (with Packet ID)
2. PUBREC (acknowledgment of receipt)
3. PUBREL (release/commit)
4. PUBCOMP (completion)

This is the highest-overhead mode, used for **billing, alarms, safety-critical** events.

---

**(b)** Explain the **CoAP protocol** with its **message format**. How does the **Observe option** transform CoAP into a Pub/Sub-like protocol? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**CoAP Overview (2 marks):**
CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol, IETF RFC 7252) is a specialized **RESTful** application layer protocol for **constrained nodes and lossy networks (LLNs)**. It is designed to translate the HTTP model (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) to a UDP-based, low-overhead equivalent suitable for 8/16-bit MCUs and battery-powered sensors.

Key features:
- UDP-based (port 5683) with optional **DTLS** for security.
- Built-in **reliable unicast** via Confirmable (CON) messages + ACK with exponential backoff.
- **URI support** (e.g., `coap://[2001:db8::1]/sensors/temp`).
- **Resource discovery** via well-known URI `/.well-known/core`.

**Message Format (3 marks):**

| Field | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Version (V) | 2 bits | Always 01 (current version) |
| Type (T) | 2 bits | CON / NON / ACK / RST |
| Token Length (TKL) | 4 bits | 0 to 8 bytes |
| Code | 8 bits | Class.Detail (e.g., 0.01=GET, 2.05=Content) |
| Message ID | 16 bits | Reliability + ACK pairing |
| Token | 0вҖ“8 bytes | Async response correlation |
| Options | variable | URI, Content-Format, Observe, etc. |
| Payload | variable | CoAP payload (often CBOR/JSON) |

**Observe Option (2 marks):**
RFC 7641 introduces the **Observe** option. When a client sends a `CON GET /sensors/temp Observe:0` request, the server responds with the current value and **registers the client** in its observer table. Subsequently, **whenever the resource state changes**, the server pushes a CON response with `Observe:<sequence_number>` to the client. The client returns an ACK, and updates its local cache. This effectively transforms CoAP's request/response paradigm into a **server-push, Pub/Sub-like stream** вҖ” a feature HTTP lacks natively.

**Advantage:** Achieves near real-time data delivery with **UDP-level efficiency** and **built-in reliability** (CON + ACK + retransmit), making it ideal for battery-powered sensor networks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” **OPC UA, DDS, and Industrial Protocol Selection**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **OPC UA information model** and its **address space**. Why is OPC UA considered the backbone protocol of **Industry 4.0 / RAMI 4.0**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**OPC UA Information Model (4 marks):**
OPC UA (IEC 62541) replaces the older OPC Classic (Windows-only, COM/DCOM) with a **platform-independent, service-oriented architecture**. Its core abstraction is the **Address Space**, a graph of **Nodes** (each with attributes like `BrowseName`, `DisplayName`, `NodeId`, `DataType`, `Value`, `AccessLevel`).

Node types include:
- **Object Nodes:** Represent components/systems (e.g., `Reactor2`, `Motor1`).
- **Variable Nodes:** Carry real-time data (e.g., `Temperature`, `Pressure`).
- **Method Nodes:** Callable functions (e.g., `SetSpeed(rpm)`).
- **Reference Nodes:** Define relationships (`HasComponent`, `HasProperty`).
- **Type Nodes:** Define reusable templates (`SensorType`, `MotorType`).

```
Root
в”ңв”Җв”Җ Objects
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ Reactor2
в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ Temperature (Variable, K)
в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ Pressure (Variable, bar)
в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ AlarmStatus (Variable, Enum)
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ Motor1
в”Ӯ       в””в”Җв”Җ SetSpeed (Method, rpm)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ Types
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ SensorType
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ MotorType
в””в”Җв”Җ Views
    в””в”Җв”Җ OperatorView
```

**Why OPC UA is the Industry 4.0 Backbone (3 marks):**
1. **Semantic Interoperability:** Unlike MQTT/Modbus that carry raw numbers, OPC UA's information model carries **self-describing semantic data** вҖ” units, alarm limits, engineering context вҖ” eliminating the "raw-data" problem.
2. **Security by Design:** Built-in **authentication, authorization, encryption, signing, and audit logging** (X.509 certificates). It satisfies IEC 62443 industrial security standards.
3. **Vertical & Horizontal Integration:** Bridges **field (PLCs/sensors)** to **SCADA/MES/ERP** on a single, consistent model вҖ” exactly the gap RAMI 4.0 aims to close.
4. **Transport Flexibility:** Runs over TCP binary, HTTPS, and emerging **Pub/Sub over UDP/MQTT/AMQP** via companion specifications.

---

**(b)** Compare **DDS and MQTT** as Pub/Sub protocols. In which IIoT scenario would you choose DDS over MQTT, and why? Discuss the **QoS parameters** of both. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | MQTT | DDS |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Centralized Broker | **Decentralized, brokerless** |
| Transport | TCP (mostly) | UDP / TCP / Shared Memory |
| Discovery | Static (configure broker) | **Built-in (participant discovery)** |
| QoS | 3 levels (0/1/2) | **22+ fine-grained parameters** |
| Latency | ~10вҖ“100 ms typical | **Ојs to low ms (real-time)** |
| Scalability | Millions of devices | Thousands of high-rate publishers |
| Use case | Cloud telemetry, asset tracking | **Robotics, autonomous vehicles, defense** |

**DDS over MQTT in IIoT (2 marks):**
DDS is preferred for **real-time, mission-critical, multi-vendor interoperability** scenarios such as:
- **Multi-robot coordination in a smart factory** where six-axis arms from different vendors must synchronize motion with sub-millisecond precision.
- **Autonomous guided vehicles (AGVs)** exchanging high-frequency telemetry (position, velocity, intent) without going through a centralized broker (which would be a single point of failure).
- **Aerospace/defense** where guaranteed delivery deadlines (DDS `DEADLINE` QoS) and `LIVELINESS` checks are safety-critical.

**QoS Discussion (2 marks):**
- **MQTT QoS** is a single integer (0, 1, 2) controlling *reliability only*. It cannot express time, durability, or history constraints.
- **DDS QoS** is a **set of policies** applied per DataReader/Writer: `RELIABILITY` (best-effort/reliable), `DEADLINE` (max inter-arrival time), `LATENCY_BUDGET`, `DURABILITY` (volatile/transient/persistent), `LIVELINESS` (alive check interval), `HISTORY` (depth of cached samples), `RESOURCE_LIMITS`, etc. This **fine-grained, composable** QoS is what makes DDS suitable for real-time industrial control.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls вҖ” Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Confusing MQTT QoS with DDS QoS.** They are fundamentally different; MQTT has 3 discrete levels; DDS has 22+ composable policies. Examiners deduct marks for treating them as equivalent.
> 2. **Forgetting the port numbers.** MQTT (1883/8883), CoAP (5683/5684), AMQP (5672/5671), Modbus (502), HTTPS (443). Examiners love asking "default port for [protocol]."
> 3. **Drawing HTTP for IoT unconditionally.** HTTP is rarely the right answer for constrained sensors; examiners expect a justified trade-off discussion.
> 4. **Omitting diagrams in 14-mark answers.** A 14-mark answer without a labeled diagram or comparison table typically loses 1вҖ“2 marks even if the prose is correct.
> 5. **Ignoring security.** A 2024+ KTU answer that doesn't mention TLS/DTLS/X.509 in a protocol question is considered incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this in your exam-prep notebook):**

- вҳҗ **MQTT** = Pub/Sub, **2-byte header**, TCP, **3 QoS levels** (0/1/2), ports **1883/8883**, broker-centric.
- вҳҗ **MQTT QoS 2** = **4-step handshake** (PUBLISH вҶ’ PUBREC вҶ’ PUBREL вҶ’ PUBCOMP) for exactly-once delivery.
- вҳҗ **CoAP** = RESTful (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) over **UDP** with **DTLS**, **4-byte base header**, port **5683/5684**.
- вҳҗ **CoAP Observe (RFC 7641)** turns Req/Resp into server-push pub/sub.
- вҳҗ **HTTP** is heavyweight for IoT; use only at the cloud/dashboard tier.
- вҳҗ **AMQP** has **Exchanges** (Direct/Fanout/Topic/Headers) + **Queues** + **Durable persistence**; ideal for enterprise middleware, not constrained devices.
- вҳҗ **XMPP** uses **XML streams**, federated like email, good for **presence** and chat-style IoT control.
- вҳҗ **DDS** = **brokerless, decentralized**, ~22 QoS policies, **microsecond latency**, real-time + mission-critical.
- вҳҗ **OPC UA** = **information modeling + semantic interoperability + built-in security (X.509)**; the **Industry 4.0 / RAMI 4.0 backbone**.
- вҳҗ **Modbus** = **Master/Slave**, function codes (1/2/3/4/5/6/15/16), 4 data tables (Coils, DI, HR, IR), **no security**, port **502**.
- вҳҗ **Default ports to memorize:** MQTT 1883, MQTT+TLS 8883, CoAP 5683, CoAP+DTLS 5684, AMQP 5672, AMQP+TLS 5671, Modbus 502, HTTPS 443, OPC UA 4840.
- вҳҗ **Transport choice rule of thumb:** TCP for reliability (MQTT/AMQP/OPC UA); UDP for efficiency (CoAP/DDS).
- вҳҗ **Pub/Sub vs. Req/Resp:** Pub/Sub decouples sender and receiver in time and space; Req/Resp needs both to be online.
- вҳҗ **Security always ask:** Does it support TLS/DTLS? Mutual auth? Encryption? (OPC UA вң“ built-in; Modbus вң— none; CoAP requires DTLS).
- вҳҗ **Information modeling** is what differentiates modern protocols (OPC UA) from legacy numeric ones (Modbus).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy for this Module:**
> 1. Always start a 14-mark answer with a **labeled block diagram** (1 mark reserved).
> 2. Follow with a **comparison table** comparing 2вҖ“3 protocols on вүҘ 5 parameters (2вҖ“3 marks reserved).
> 3. Discuss **architectural pattern, transport, header size, QoS, and security** for each protocol (4вҖ“6 marks).
> 4. Conclude with a **real-world Industry 4.0 use case** (e.g., "This protocol is used in BMW's Spartanburg plant for ...") to demonstrate applied knowledge (1вҖ“2 marks).
> 5. Always **underline or bold** key terms (Pub/Sub, QoS 2, DTLS, etc.) вҖ” examiners scan for these.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT525 - INDUSTRY 4.0 AND INDUSTRIAL INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** MQTT and Other IIoT Communication Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 вҖ” Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is MQTT?

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is a lightweight, publish/subscribe-based messaging protocol designed for resource-constrained devices and unreliable networks. It was originally developed by **Andy Stanford-Clark (IBM)** and **Arlen Nipper (Eurotech)** in **1999** for monitoring oil pipelines over satellite links. Today, MQTT is an **OASIS Standard** and an **ISO/IEC 20922** standard, and it forms the de-facto messaging backbone of most Industrial IoT (IIoT) and Industry 4.0 deployments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *MQTT is an application-layer messaging protocol built on top of TCP/IP that uses a publish/subscribe paradigm, a central broker, and topic-based filtering to enable low-bandwidth, low-power, asynchronous machine-to-machine (M2M) communication in IIoT systems.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The Newspaper Stand

Imagine you are interested in cricket news. Instead of going to every news agency and asking for updates (this is how **HTTP/Request-Response** works), you simply **subscribe** to the "Cricket" topic at your local newspaper stand. Whenever a new article is published on that topic, the stand (acting as the **Broker**) prints and delivers it to your doorstep. Publishers (newspapers) don't know who you are; you don't know who the publishers are вҖ” the broker matches supply and demand.

- **Publisher** = Newspaper journalist (sensor / PLC)
- **Subscriber** = Reader (dashboard / cloud analytics)
- **Broker** = Newspaper stand (e.g., **Mosquitto**, **HiveMQ**, **EMQX**)
- **Topic** = Newspaper section (e.g., `factory/floor1/temperature`)
- **QoS Level** = Subscription tier (free, premium, archive-grade)

## 1.3 Other Companion IIoT Protocols (CoAP, AMQP, DDS, OPC UA)

KTU Module 3 typically groups MQTT with the following protocols:

| Protocol | Full Name | Transport | Pattern | Best Suited For |
|----------|-----------|-----------|---------|-----------------|
| **MQTT** | Message Queuing Telemetry Transport | TCP (port **1883** / TLS **8883**) | Publish/Subscribe | Low-power telemetry, mobile, IIoT cloud |
| **CoAP** | Constrained Application Protocol | UDP (port **5683**) | Request/Response | Constrained nodes, RESTful interaction |
| **AMQP** | Advanced Message Queuing Protocol | TCP (port **5672**) | Pub/Sub + Queues | Enterprise messaging, banking |
| **DDS** | Data Distribution Service | UDP/RTPS | Pub/Sub with QoS | Real-time, high-performance (defence, robotics) |
| **OPC UA** | Open Platform Communications Unified Architecture | TCP | Client/Server + Pub/Sub | Industrial interoperability (PLC-to-PLC) |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus places strong emphasis on **MQTT**, **CoAP**, and **OPC UA**, since these three represent the dominant IIoT communication paradigms: lightweight pub/sub, constrained REST, and industrial interoperability respectively.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Default MQTT Port (non-TLS):** `1883`
- **Default MQTT Port (TLS):** `8883`
- **CoAP Default Port:** `5683` (DTLS: `5684`)
- **AMQP Default Port:** `5672`
- **MQTT-SN Default Port:** `1885` (UDP-based)
- **Maximum Topic Length (MQTT 5.0):** **65,535 bytes**
- **Maximum Payload (implementation-dependent):** up to **256 MB**, but practical deployments use **вүӨ 1 KB** for IIoT.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 вҖ” Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 MQTT Architecture вҖ” Components & Flow

### 2.1.1 Core Components

1. **Publisher (Client)** вҖ” A device or application that produces data and sends messages to the broker tagged with a topic.
2. **Subscriber (Client)** вҖ” A device or application that expresses interest in one or more topics via topic filters.
3. **Broker (Server)** вҖ” The central message dispatcher. It receives all messages, filters them, and dispatches them to interested subscribers. Examples: **Mosquitto**, **HiveMQ**, **EMQX**, **AWS IoT Core**, **Azure IoT Hub**.
4. **Topic** вҖ” A UTF-8 string used as a routing key, structured hierarchically using `/` as a separator (e.g., `factory/line1/motor/temperature`).
5. **Wildcards** вҖ”
   - `+` matches **one** level (single-level wildcard)
   - `#` matches **multiple** levels but must be the **last character** (multi-level wildcard)

### 2.1.2 MQTT Control Packet Types (14 in total)

| Packet | Direction | Purpose |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| `CONNECT` | Client вҶ’ Broker | Initiate connection |
| `CONNACK` | Broker вҶ’ Client | Acknowledge connection |
| `PUBLISH` | Bi-directional | Deliver application message |
| `PUBACK` / `PUBREC` / `PUBREL` / `PUBCOMP` | Bi-directional | QoS 1 & 2 handshakes |
| `SUBSCRIBE` | Client вҶ’ Broker | Register interest |
| `SUBACK` | Broker вҶ’ Client | Acknowledge subscription |
| `UNSUBSCRIBE` | Client вҶ’ Broker | Cancel interest |
| `UNSUBACK` | Broker вҶ’ Client | Acknowledge |
| `PINGREQ` / `PINGRESP` | Bi-directional | Keep-alive |
| `DISCONNECT` | Client вҶ’ Broker | Clean termination |
| `AUTH` | Bi-directional | Enhanced authentication (MQTT 5.0) |

## 2.2 Quality of Service (QoS) Levels

| QoS | Name | Guarantee | Mechanism | Use Case |
|-----|------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **QoS 0** | At most once | None вҖ” fire and forget | Single PUBLISH | Sensor telemetry where loss is acceptable |
| **QoS 1** | At least once | Possible duplicates | PUBLISH вҶ’ PUBACK | Standard control signals |
| **QoS 2** | Exactly once | No loss, no duplicates | 4-step handshake (PUBLISH вҶ’ PUBREC вҶ’ PUBREL вҶ’ PUBCOMP) | Billing, safety-critical commands |

### QoS 2 вҖ” The 4-Step Handshake (Exhaustive)

1. **Publisher вҶ’ Broker:** `PUBLISH` (DUP=0)
2. **Broker вҶ’ Publisher:** `PUBREC` (acknowledges receipt)
3. **Publisher вҶ’ Broker:** `PUBREL` (releases the message)
4. **Broker вҶ’ Publisher:** `PUBCOMP` (confirms completion)

## 2.3 MQTT Message Lifecycle States

When a client subscribes with QoS 2, a message transitions through these states:

1. **Unacknowledged** вҖ” Initial state after PUBLISH.
2. **Acknowledged** вҖ” After PUBREC received.
3. **Released** вҖ” After PUBREL received.
4. **Completed** вҖ” After PUBCOMP received.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all the formulae, constraints, and quantitative relationships required for solving numerical/analytical problems on this topic.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| MQTT Keep-Alive Timeout | $T_{\text{keepalive}} = 1.5 \times T_{\text{interval}}$ | If no packet in 1.5Г— interval, broker disconnects |
| Total Bandwidth per Message | $B_{\text{msg}} = H_{\text{fixed}} + L_{\text{topic}} + L_{\text{payload}}$ | Fixed header (2 bytes) + variable header + payload |
| Fixed Header Size | $H_{\text{fixed}} = 2 \text{ bytes (minimum)}$ | First byte = control type; second byte = remaining length |
| Remaining Length Encoding | $R = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (b_i \cdot 128^i)$ | Variable-length encoding, max 4 bytes = up to **268,435,455 bytes** |
| QoS Delivery Probability (independent retries) | $P_{\text{deliver}} = 1 - (1 - p)^{n}$ | Where $p$ = single-attempt success, $n$ = retries |
| CoAP Message Overhead | $O_{\text{CoAP}} \approx 4 \text{ bytes (UDP header only)}$ | vs MQTT's TCP overhead of **20+ bytes** |
| Topic Wildcard Cardinality | For topic depth $d$ with `+`, possible matches $= \prod_{i=1}^{d} N_i$ | $N_i$ = number of children at level $i$ |
| Mean Publish Latency (MQTT over TCP) | $\bar{L}_{\text{MQTT}} = RTT + T_{\text{broker}} + T_{\text{deserialize}}$ | Round-trip + broker processing + client processing |
| Mean Latency (CoAP over UDP) | $\bar{L}_{\text{CoAP}} = T_{\text{ack}} + T_{\text{process}}$ | Lower than MQTT due to UDP + no handshake |

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Smart Manufacturing:** MQTT bridges PLCs (e.g., Siemens S7) to **AWS IoT Core** or **Azure IoT Hub** for predictive maintenance.
- **Connected Vehicles:** Tesla, BMW, and Volkswagen use MQTT over cellular for telemetry and OTA updates.
- **Energy & Utilities:** Smart grids use MQTT for SCADA telemetry because of low bandwidth (< 100 kbps per device).
- **Healthcare Wearables:** Continuous patient monitoring with QoS 1 for reliable vitals.
- **Logistics & Supply Chain:** MQTT-SN extends MQTT over ZigBee / BLE for asset tracking in warehouses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 вҖ” Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation вҖ” QoS Delivery Probability vs Retries

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *вҖңIf a single packet transmission has success probability $p$, and the system retries up to $n$ times, derive the probability of at least one successful delivery.вҖқ*

**Step 1 вҖ” Define failure probability of a single attempt**

The probability that a single transmission **fails** is:

$$P_{\text{fail, single}} = 1 - p$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Compute probability that ALL $n$ attempts fail**

Assuming independent attempts (a standard assumption in KTU problems), the joint failure probability is the product of individual failures:

$$P_{\text{fail, all}} = (1 - p)^{n}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Compute probability of at least one success**

Using the complement rule, $P(\text{at least one success}) = 1 - P(\text{all fail})$:

$$P_{\text{deliver}} = 1 - (1 - p)^{n}$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Numerical Example for KTU Exam**

Let $p = 0.8$ (single attempt success), $n = 3$ retries.

**Compute $1 - p$:**

$$1 - p = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2$$

**Raise to power $n$:**

$$(0.2)^{3} = 0.008$$

**Final delivery probability:**

$$P_{\text{deliver}} = 1 - 0.008 = 0.992$$

**Interpretation:**

$$P_{\text{deliver}} = 99.2\%$$

This is why QoS 1 in MQTT, with even a few retries, achieves near-perfect reliability at modest bandwidth cost.

---

## 3.2 Derivation вҖ” MQTT Bandwidth Estimation

> [!NOTE]
> KTU numerical: *вҖңA sensor publishes a temperature value every 10 seconds with a 4-byte payload and a 20-byte topic. Compute the hourly bandwidth in bytes for 100 such sensors.вҖқ*

**Step 1 вҖ” Total message size per publish**

$$B_{\text{msg}} = H_{\text{fixed}} + L_{\text{topic}} + L_{\text{payload}}$$

**Substitute values (fixed header = 2 bytes):**

$$B_{\text{msg}} = 2 + 20 + 4 = 26 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Number of publishes per hour per sensor**

$$\text{Publishes/hour} = \frac{3600}{10} = 360$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Bandwidth per sensor per hour**

$$B_{\text{sensor,hr}} = 26 \times 360 = 9360 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Bandwidth for 100 sensors per hour**

$$B_{\text{total,hr}} = 9360 \times 100 = 936{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$

**Convert to kilobytes:**

$$B_{\text{total,hr}} = \frac{936{,}000}{1024} \approx 914.06 \text{ KB/hour}$$

**Convert to MB per day (Г— 24 hours):**

$$B_{\text{total,day}} \approx 914.06 \times 24 \approx 21{,}937.5 \text{ KB/day}$$

$$B_{\text{total,day}} \approx \frac{21{,}937.5}{1024} \approx 21.42 \text{ MB/day}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix вҖ” MQTT vs CoAP vs AMQP

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP | AMQP |
|-----------|------|------|------|
| **Standard Body** | OASIS / ISO/IEC 20922 | IETF RFC 7252 | OASIS / ISO/IEC 19464 |
| **Transport** | TCP | UDP | TCP |
| **Header Size** | 2 bytes fixed (min) | 4 bytes | 8 bytes (frame header) |
| **Pattern** | Pub/Sub | Request/Response | Pub/Sub + Queues |
| **QoS Levels** | 3 (0, 1, 2) | 2 (CON/NON messages) | 4 delivery guarantees |
| **Security** | TLS / Username-Pwd | DTLS / PSK | TLS / SASL |
| **Power Consumption** | Medium | Very Low | High |
| **Latency** | Medium | Lowest | Medium-High |
| **Suitable For** | Cloud telemetry | Sensor REST calls | Enterprise messaging |
| **Broker Needed?** | Yes | No (uses caching proxy optionally) | Yes |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step вҖ” CoAP 4-Way Message Exchange

CoAP uses confirmable (CON) and non-confirmable (NON) messages:

1. **Client вҶ’ Server:** `CON` request (with token, type = CON)
2. **Server вҶ’ Client:** `ACK` (acknowledges receipt, contains the response)
3. *(Optional)* If piggyback response is not possible, a separate `CON` response is sent.
4. **Client вҶ’ Server:** `ACK` for the response.

For NON messages (analogous to MQTT QoS 0), no acknowledgement is expected.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 вҖ” Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MQTT Publish/Subscribe Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Pub["Publishers (Sensors / PLCs)"]
        P1["TempSensor"]
        P2["PressureSensor"]
        P3["VibrationSensor"]
    end

    subgraph Brk["MQTT Broker (Mosquitto / HiveMQ)"]
        T1["Topic: factory/floor1/temp"]
        T2["Topic: factory/floor1/pressure"]
        T3["Topic: factory/floor1/vibration"]
    end

    subgraph Sub["Subscribers (Apps / Cloud)"]
        S1["SCADA Dashboard"]
        S2["Cloud Analytics"]
        S3["Alert Mobile App"]
    end

    P1 -- "PUBLISH QoS1" --> T1
    P2 -- "PUBLISH QoS1" --> T2
    P3 -- "PUBLISH QoS2" --> T3

    T1 -- "Subscribe factory/floor1/+" --> S1
    T2 -- "Subscribe factory/floor1/#" --> S2
    T3 -- "Subscribe factory/floor1/vibration" --> S3
```

## 4.2 QoS Handshake Sequence Diagrams

### 4.2.1 QoS 0 (At Most Once)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Pub as Publisher
    participant Brk as Broker
    participant Sub as Subscriber
    Pub->>Brk: PUBLISH (no ack)
    Brk->>Sub: Forward message
    Note over Pub,Sub: No acknowledgement; loss possible
```

### 4.2.2 QoS 1 (At Least Once)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Pub as Publisher
    participant Brk as Broker
    Pub->>Brk: PUBLISH (DUP=0)
    Brk->>Pub: PUBACK
    Note over Pub,Brk: Duplicate possible
```

### 4.2.3 QoS 2 (Exactly Once)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Pub as Publisher
    participant Brk as Broker
    Pub->>Brk: PUBLISH
    Brk->>Pub: PUBREC
    Pub->>Brk: PUBREL
    Brk->>Pub: PUBCOMP
    Note over Pub,Brk: No loss, no duplicates
```

## 4.3 IIoT Protocol Stack Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AppLayer["Application Layer"]
        A1["MQTT - Pub/Sub"]
        A2["CoAP - REST"]
        A3["AMQP - Queues"]
        A4["OPC UA - Industrial"]
        A5["DDS - Real-Time"]
    end

    subgraph TransLayer["Transport Layer"]
        T1["TCP"]
        T2["UDP"]
    end

    subgraph NetLayer["Network Layer"]
        N1["IPv4 / IPv6"]
    end

    subgraph PhysLayer["Physical / Link"]
        L1["WiFi / Ethernet / Cellular / 6LoWPAN"]
    end

    A1 --> T1
    A2 --> T2
    A3 --> T1
    A4 --> T1
    A5 --> T2
    T1 --> N1
    T2 --> N1
    N1 --> L1
```

## 4.4 End-to-End MQTT Data Flow in Smart Factory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Sensor Layer: Temperature Pressure Humidity"] --> B["Edge Gateway: Mosquitto Broker"]
    B --> C["Local Historian: Time-Series DB"]
    B --> D["Cloud: AWS IoT Core"]
    B --> E["Mobile App: Flutter Dashboard"]
    D --> F["Analytics: ML Predictive Maintenance"]
    F --> G["Alert Engine"]
    G --> H["Email / SMS Notification"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 вҖ” KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of an MQTT broker with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**
An MQTT broker is the central server in the publish/subscribe model. It receives all messages from publishers, filters them based on topic, and dispatches them to subscribers that have subscribed to matching topic filters. It does not store messages for non-subscribed topics (unless using retained messages feature).

*Example:* In a smart factory, 100 temperature sensors publish to topic `factory/line1/temp`. The Mosquitto broker running on a Raspberry Pi receives these and forwards them to the SCADA dashboard, the cloud analytics platform, and the engineerвҖҷs mobile app вҖ” all based on their subscriptions.

*(Marks breakdown: Definition 1, Filtering 1, Example 1)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between QoS 0, QoS 1, and QoS 2 in MQTT.**

**Model Answer:**

- **QoS 0 (At most once):** Fire-and-forget; no acknowledgement. Lowest overhead; used for non-critical telemetry.
- **QoS 1 (At least once):** Uses PUBLISHвҖ“PUBACK handshake; duplicates possible; used for standard control.
- **QoS 2 (Exactly once):** Four-step handshake (PUBLISH, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP); no loss and no duplicates; used for billing, safety-critical commands.

*(Marks breakdown: One line each = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each вҖ” Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the MQTT protocol architecture in detail. Discuss the role of brokers, topics, wildcards, and retained messages. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare MQTT with CoAP and AMQP across at least 6 parameters with a comparison table. Discuss one industrial use-case where each is most suitable. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution вҖ” Part (a)

**Architecture Components:**

1. **Client:** Any device that runs the MQTT library (Eclipse Paho, etc.). A client can be a publisher, a subscriber, or both.
2. **Broker:** The heart of MQTT. Examples include **Eclipse Mosquitto**, **HiveMQ**, **EMQX**, and **AWS IoT Core**. The broker is responsible for:
   - Accepting client connections
   - Decoding control packets
   - Maintaining session state
   - Filtering messages against topic subscriptions
   - Dispatching messages to subscribers
   - Enforcing QoS handshakes
   - Handling authentication via username/password, certificates, or OAuth

3. **Topics:** Hierarchical UTF-8 strings used for routing. Example: `factory/line3/motorB/temperature`.

4. **Wildcards:**
   - `+` (single-level): `factory/+/temperature` matches `factory/line1/temperature`, `factory/line2/temperature` but **not** `factory/line1/motorB/temperature`.
   - `#` (multi-level): `factory/#` matches all topics under `factory`.

5. **Retained Messages:** A publisher can flag a message as "retained." The broker stores the last retained message for that topic and immediately delivers it to any new subscriber.

6. **Last Will and Testament (LWT):** A client can register a "will" message that the broker automatically publishes if the client disconnects abnormally.

**Diagrammatic Explanation:**

Publisher вҶ’ Broker вҶ’ Subscribers (with topic filtering and QoS negotiation).

*(Marks distribution: Broker 2, Topics/Wildcards 2, Retained/LWT 2, Architecture Diagram 1)*

#### Model Solution вҖ” Part (b)

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP | AMQP |
|-----------|------|------|------|
| Transport | TCP | UDP | TCP |
| Pattern | Pub/Sub | Req/Resp | Pub/Sub + Queues |
| Header Size | 2 bytes min | 4 bytes | 8 bytes |
| Power | Medium | Lowest | High |
| QoS Levels | 3 | 2 | 4 |
| Security | TLS | DTLS | TLS + SASL |
| Standardization | OASIS | IETF | OASIS |

**Industrial Use Cases:**

- **MQTT вҶ’ Smart Metering:** Lightweight telemetry from smart electricity meters to cloud.
- **CoAP вҶ’ Constrained Wireless Sensors:** Battery-powered environmental sensors using 6LoWPAN.
- **AMQP вҶ’ Banking / Enterprise:** Transactional messaging in financial systems.

*(Marks distribution: Table 4, Use cases 3)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the MQTT QoS mechanism in detail. For QoS 2, explain the four-step handshake with a sequence diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A wireless sensor node publishes 4-byte temperature readings every 5 seconds over MQTT. The topic is `home/room1/temperature` (15 bytes). Compute the bandwidth consumed per hour and per day in KB and MB, considering the 2-byte fixed header. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution вҖ” Part (a)

**QoS Mechanism:**

The Quality of Service (QoS) level is an agreement between sender and receiver about the guarantee of message delivery. MQTT supports three QoS levels:

**QoS 0 вҖ” At most once:**

The sender transmits the PUBLISH packet once. There is no acknowledgement. The message may be lost if the network is unreliable. This is the lowest-overhead option.

**QoS 1 вҖ” At least once:**

The sender transmits PUBLISH and waits for PUBACK. If no PUBACK is received within a timeout, the PUBLISH is retransmitted with the DUP flag set. Duplicate messages may be delivered.

**QoS 2 вҖ” Exactly once:**

The four-step handshake ensures the message is delivered exactly once.

**QoS 2 Sequence (already shown in Section 4.2.3):**

1. **PUBLISH** (Publisher вҶ’ Broker)
2. **PUBREC** (Broker вҶ’ Publisher, acknowledges receipt)
3. **PUBREL** (Publisher вҶ’ Broker, releases the message for delivery)
4. **PUBCOMP** (Broker вҶ’ Publisher, confirms completion)

This handshake uses packet identifiers (16-bit) to ensure correct ordering and prevents duplicates via state tracking.

*(Marks distribution: QoS 0 & 1 description 2, QoS 2 explanation 3, Diagram/sequence 2)*

#### Model Solution вҖ” Part (b)

**Given:**

- Payload size = 4 bytes
- Topic size = 15 bytes
- Fixed header = 2 bytes
- Publish interval = 5 seconds

**Step 1 вҖ” Total message size per publish**

$$B_{\text{msg}} = H_{\text{fixed}} + L_{\text{topic}} + L_{\text{payload}}$$

$$B_{\text{msg}} = 2 + 15 + 4 = 21 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Number of publishes per hour**

$$\text{Publishes/hour} = \frac{3600}{5} = 720$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Bandwidth per hour**

$$B_{\text{hr}} = 21 \times 720 = 15{,}120 \text{ bytes}$$

**Convert to KB:**

$$B_{\text{hr}} = \frac{15{,}120}{1024} \approx 14.77 \text{ KB/hour}$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Bandwidth per day**

$$B_{\text{day}} = 14.77 \times 24 \approx 354.49 \text{ KB/day}$$

**Convert to MB:**

$$B_{\text{day}} = \frac{354.49}{1024} \approx 0.346 \text{ MB/day}$$

*(Marks distribution: Formula 2, Substitution 2, Hourly calc 2, Daily conversion 1)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls in MQTT Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing QoS 1 and QoS 2:** Students often write "QoS 2 is at least once" вҖ” wrong. QoS 1 is at least once; QoS 2 is **exactly once**.
> 2. **Forgetting fixed header in bandwidth calculations:** Many students calculate only `payload + topic` and forget the 2-byte fixed header. This costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Wildcard misuse:** Using `#` in the middle of a topic (e.g., `factory/#/temperature`) is invalid; `#` must be the **last** character.
> 4. **Confusing MQTT with HTTP:** MQTT is **not** request/response вҖ” it is **publish/subscribe**. Listing it as "lightweight HTTP" loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the broker:** When asked "MQTT architecture", failing to mention the broker explicitly loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Bandwidth unit conversion:** Mixing up 1 KB = 1000 bytes vs 1024 bytes вҖ” KTU expects **1024** (binary).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- MQTT is a **publish/subscribe**, TCP-based, lightweight messaging protocol standardized as **ISO/IEC 20922**.
- It uses a **central broker** (Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX) for message routing.
- **Topics** are hierarchical (`/`-separated) and support `+` (single-level) and `#` (multi-level) wildcards.
- Three **QoS levels**: QoS 0 (at most once), QoS 1 (at least once, PUBACK), QoS 2 (exactly once, 4-step handshake).
- **Default port:** `1883` (non-TLS), `8883` (TLS).
- **Retained messages** allow new subscribers to immediately receive the last published value.
- **Last Will and Testament (LWT)** enables automatic notifications on abnormal disconnection.
- **MQTT-SN** extends MQTT to UDP/ZigBee/BLE for sensor networks.
- **CoAP** is UDP-based, REST-like, ideal for constrained devices (header 4 bytes).
- **AMQP** is TCP-based, supports pub/sub + queues, used in enterprise systems.
- **OPC UA** is the industrial interoperability standard for PLC-to-PLC communication.
- **DDS** is real-time, brokerless pub/sub used in robotics and defence.
- Delivery probability after $n$ retries: $P_{\text{deliver}} = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$.
- Total message size: $B_{\text{msg}} = H_{\text{fixed}} + L_{\text{topic}} + L_{\text{payload}}$.
- MQTT 5.0 introduces **shared subscriptions**, **message expiry**, **topic aliases**, and **response topics**.
- For KTU 2024 exams: focus on architecture diagrams, QoS handshakes, and bandwidth/latency numericals.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Cloud Storage Models and Communication APIs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT525 - INDUSTRY 4.0 AND INDUSTRIAL INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Cloud Storage Models and Communication APIs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 вҖ” Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cloud Storage Models вҖ” Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cloud Storage Model** is a structured paradigm that defines **how digital data is logically organized, addressed, accessed, and managed across distributed, virtualized, and remotely-hosted storage infrastructure** delivered to end-users as an on-demand, scalable, and metered service over a network (typically the Internet).

In the context of **Industry 4.0 and IIoT**, cloud storage models form the **persistent data backbone** of any smart factory, predictive maintenance system, or digital twin platform. They abstract physical hardware (HDDs, SSDs, SANs, NAS) behind virtualization layers and expose them through standardized service APIs.

### The Three Foundational Cloud Storage Architectures

| # | Architecture | KTU Definition | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Public Cloud Storage** | Storage provisioned over the public Internet by third-party providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) | Multi-tenant, elastic, pay-per-use |
| 2 | **Private Cloud Storage** | Storage operated exclusively for a single organization, on-premises or hosted | Single-tenant, high governance |
| 3 | **Hybrid Cloud Storage** | Orchestrated integration of public + private with intelligent data tiering | Burst-out capability, data sovereignty |

> [!NOTE]
> **SLA (Service Level Agreement)** is the contractual guarantee from a cloud provider specifying **availability (%)**, **durability (e.g., 99.999999999% вҖ” "eleven nines")**, and **latency (ms)**. KTU expects students to remember that AWS S3, for instance, claims a durability of **99.999999999%** (eleven 9s).

---

## 1.2 Communication APIs вҖ” Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Communication API (Application Programming Interface)** in IIoT is a **contractually defined set of rules, endpoints, message formats, and protocols** that enables heterogeneous machines, sensors, gateways, cloud services, and applications to exchange structured data over a network in a deterministic, secure, and machine-parsable manner.

For Industry 4.0, Communication APIs are the **"nervous system"** that connects the **Operational Technology (OT)** layer (PLCs, SCADA, sensors) with the **Information Technology (IT)** layer (cloud, analytics, ERP).

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy вҖ” The Restaurant Menu:**
> Think of a **Communication API** exactly like a **restaurant menu**:
> - The **menu items** are the **endpoints** (e.g., `/getTemperature`, `/storeData`)
> - The **kitchen** is the **server/cloud backend**
> - The **waiter** is the **protocol** (HTTP, MQTT, CoAP) that carries your request and brings back the response
> - The **menu language** is the **data format** (JSON, XML, CBOR)
> - The **waiter rules** are the **REST/SOAP constraints**
>
> Without a standardized menu, you cannot order reliably; without an API, devices cannot communicate reliably.

---

## 1.3 Cloud Storage as a Service вҖ” High-Level Classification

Cloud storage is delivered under three primary **XaaS (Anything-as-a-Service)** models:

| Service Model | Storage Provider Manages | Customer Manages | IIoT Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service)** | Hardware, virtualization, raw storage | OS, applications, data | Amazon EBS, Azure Disk |
| **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service)** | Hardware, OS, middleware, runtime | Applications and data | Google App Engine Storage |
| **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service)** | Everything except user data | Only user data and access | Dropbox, OneDrive |

---

## 1.4 Communication API Layers in IIoT Stack

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the **layered model** of IIoT communication. From bottom to top:
>
> 1. **Perception Layer** вҖ” Sensors/actuators (physical signals)
> 2. **Network Layer** вҖ” Transport (Wi-Fi, 5G, LoRa, Ethernet)
> 3. **Middleware/Communication API Layer** вҖ” REST, SOAP, MQTT, CoAP, AMQP
> 4. **Application Layer** вҖ” Dashboards, analytics, control logic
> 5. **Business Layer** вҖ” ERP, billing, decision support

The Communication API sits in **Layer 3** and acts as the translation bridge between raw device data and enterprise applications.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 вҖ” Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Logical Data Storage Models in the Cloud

Although cloud providers expose many branded services, **all map logically to one of four storage abstractions**:

### 2.1.1 File Storage (NAS-style)
- Data is stored as **hierarchical files inside directories** (folders).
- Accessed via standard file protocols: **NFS (Network File System)** and **SMB (Server Message Block)**.
- Use case: Shared document repositories, configuration files, log archives.
- Latency: **Low-to-medium** for small files; degrades with millions of files.

### 2.1.2 Block Storage (SAN-style)
- Data is split into **equally-sized blocks** (typically **4 KB to 16 KB** each).
- Each block has a unique address; no inherent hierarchy.
- Accessed via **iSCSI, Fibre Channel, or cloud-native APIs** (e.g., AWS EBS).
- Use case: Databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL), VM boot disks, transactional workloads.
- **Highest performance, lowest latency** (sub-millisecond in premium tiers).

### 2.1.3 Object Storage
- Data is stored as **discrete objects** inside a **flat namespace** called a *bucket*.
- Each object contains: **payload (bytes), metadata (key-value pairs), and a unique ID**.
- Accessed via **HTTP REST APIs** (e.g., `PUT /bucket/object`).
- Use case: Unstructured data вҖ” images, videos, IoT sensor dumps, backups.
- **Practically unlimited scalability**; **eleven-9s durability** (e.g., AWS S3).

### 2.1.4 Database Storage (Structured)
- **SQL (Relational):** MySQL, PostgreSQL вҖ” tabular schema, ACID guarantees.
- **NoSQL (Non-relational):**
   - **Document:** MongoDB (JSON-like)
   - **Key-Value:** Redis, DynamoDB
   - **Column-Family:** Cassandra, HBase
   - **Graph:** Neo4j
- Use case: Time-series sensor data, device telemetry, asset registries.

---

## 2.2 Communication APIs вҖ” Detailed Taxonomy

### 2.2.1 Classification by Architectural Style

| API Style | KTU Definition | Transport | Payload Format | IIoT Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **REST (REpresentational State Transfer)** | Stateless, resource-oriented, HTTP-based | HTTP/HTTPS | JSON / XML | Cloud dashboards, webhooks |
| **SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol)** | XML-based, stateful or stateless, WSDL-defined | HTTP, SMTP, TCP | XML only | Enterprise (SAP, ERP) |
| **GraphQL** | Query-language-based, single-endpoint | HTTP | JSON | Custom dashboards |
| **gRPC** | High-performance, contract-first | HTTP/2 | Protocol Buffers | Edge-to-cloud microservices |
| **WebSocket** | Full-duplex, persistent connection | TCP | JSON / Binary | Real-time control loops |

### 2.2.2 Classification by IoT Protocol Purpose

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 3 specifically emphasizes **IIoT-grade protocols**. Memorize these:

| Protocol | Full Name | Layer | Header | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MQTT** | Message Queuing Telemetry Transport | App (TCP) | **2 bytes fixed** | Constrained sensor telemetry |
| **CoAP** | Constrained Application Protocol | App (UDP) | **4 bytes base** | Low-power devices |
| **AMQP** | Advanced Message Queuing Protocol | App (TCP) | **8 bytes** | Enterprise messaging |
| **DDS** | Data Distribution Service | App (UDP/TCP) | Variable | Real-time distributed systems |
| **HTTP/HTTPS** | HyperText Transfer Protocol (Secure) | App (TCP) | Variable | Web, REST APIs |
| **XMPP** | Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol | App (TCP) | XML-based | Chat, presence |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Comparison Matrices

### 2.3.1 REST API Maturity Model (Richardson Maturity Model)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"What level of REST maturity does an API achieve?"* вҖ” Refer to this **4-level ladder**:

| Level | Compliance | KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|
| **Level 0** | HTTP as transport only, single URI | "The Swamp of POX (Plain Old XML)" |
| **Level 1** | Multiple URIs, one per resource | Resources introduced |
| **Level 2** | URIs + HTTP verbs (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) | **True REST** вҖ” most common |
| **Level 3** | Level 2 + **HATEOAS** (hypermedia links) | Hypermedia controls |

### 2.3.2 MQTT QoS Levels

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality of Service (QoS)** in MQTT defines delivery guarantees:

| QoS Level | Name | Guarantee | Packet Flow |
|---|---|---|---|
| **QoS 0** | At most once | Fire-and-forget | PUBLISH only |
| **QoS 1** | At least once | Possible duplicates | PUBLISH вҶ’ PUBACK |
| **QoS 2** | Exactly once | No duplicates | PUBLISH вҶ’ PUBREC вҶ’ PUBREL вҶ’ PUBCOMP |

### 2.3.3 Cloud Storage SLA Metrics (Cheat Sheet)

| Metric | Formula / Standard | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Durability** | $\text{Durability} = 1 - P(\text{data loss over time})$ | $99.999999999\%$ |
| **Availability** | $\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}}$ | $99.9\%$ to $99.99\%$ |
| **Latency** | $\text{Latency} = T_{\text{request}} - T_{\text{response}}$ | $< 100\text{ ms}$ |
| **Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Data Transferred}}{\text{Time}}$ | MB/s or IOPS |
| **IOPS** | I/O Operations Per Second | SSD: $10^4$вҖ“$10^6$ |
| **Replication Factor** | $R = \text{number of stored copies}$ | $R = 3$ typical |

### 2.3.4 HTTP Status Codes (KTU Hot-Favorite)

| Range | Class | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **1xx** | Informational | 100 Continue |
| **2xx** | Success | **200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content** |
| **3xx** | Redirection | 301 Moved Permanently |
| **4xx** | Client Error | **400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 404 Not Found** |
| **5xx** | Server Error | **500 Internal Server Error, 503 Service Unavailable** |

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind API Design in IIoT

1. **Interoperability** вҖ” A Siemens PLC, a Schneider sensor, and an AWS Lambda must all speak the same "language".
2. **Scalability** вҖ” REST's statelessness lets cloud load balancers horizontally scale to millions of devices.
3. **Security** вҖ” APIs enforce authentication (OAuth 2.0, API Keys, JWT) and authorization (RBAC) at a single chokepoint.
4. **Decoupling** вҖ” Producers (sensors) and consumers (analytics) evolve independently.
5. **Observability** вҖ” Standardized APIs allow uniform logging, tracing, and SLA monitoring.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Cloud Storage Used | API Used |
|---|---|---|
| Smart Factory (Siemens) | AWS S3 + Azure Blob | MQTT + REST |
| Connected Vehicles (Tesla) | Custom Kafka + S3 | gRPC + MQTT |
| Smart Grid (GE) | Azure Time Series Insights | AMQP + REST |
| Healthcare IoT | HIPAA-compliant blob storage | HTTPS REST |
| Agriculture IoT | Google Cloud Storage | LoRaWAN вҶ’ MQTT вҶ’ REST |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 вҖ” Step-by-Step Derivations, Constructions & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: REST API Request-Response Lifecycle

The REST communication cycle can be expressed as a **deterministic state machine**. Let us derive it step-by-step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:**
> - Client: IoT sensor gateway
> - Server: Cloud REST API endpoint
> - Resource URI: `/api/v1/devices/{deviceId}/telemetry`
> - Method: `POST`

### Step 1 вҖ” Client Constructs Request

A REST request = **Request Line + Headers + Body**

$$
\text{Request} = \underbrace{M \; U \; H}_{\text{Start Line}} \; + \; \underbrace{H_1, H_2, \dots, H_n}_{\text{Headers}} \; + \; \underbrace{B}_{\text{Body}}
$$

where $M$ = HTTP method, $U$ = URI, $H$ = HTTP version.

**Concrete Example:**

```
POST /api/v1/devices/D042/telemetry HTTP/1.1
Host: api.iiot-cloud.example.com
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 87
```

### Step 2 вҖ” JSON Payload Construction

The body uses **JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)** вҖ” a key-value structure:

```json
{
  "deviceId": "D042",
  "timestamp": "2026-01-21T14:33:12Z",
  "temperature": 78.4,
  "vibration": 0.023,
  "status": "NOMINAL"
}
```

### Step 3 вҖ” TLS Handshake (for HTTPS)

Since REST over HTTPS is standard in IIoT, a **TLS 1.3 handshake** occurs before any payload exchange:

$$
\text{TLS Handshake} = \text{ClientHello} \rightarrow \text{ServerHello} \rightarrow \text{Key Exchange} \rightarrow \text{Finished}
$$

Total round-trips before payload: **0-RTT (TLS 1.3)** versus **2-RTT (TLS 1.2)**.

### Step 4 вҖ” Server Processing

The cloud API server:

1. **Parses** JSON into internal objects.
2. **Authenticates** the JWT token (validates signature against issuer's public key).
3. **Authorizes** (checks RBAC: does this device have `telemetry:write` permission?).
4. **Validates** payload against schema.
5. **Persists** data to chosen storage (e.g., DynamoDB for hot data, S3 for cold archive).
6. **Returns** HTTP response with status code.

### Step 5 вҖ” Response Construction

$$
\text{Response} = \underbrace{S \; R \; H}_{\text{Status Line}} \; + \; \underbrace{H_1, H_2, \dots, H_n}_{\text{Headers}} \; + \; \underbrace{B}_{\text{Body}}
$$

**Concrete Example:**

```
HTTP/1.1 201 Created
Location: /api/v1/devices/D042/telemetry/evt_87a2c1
Content-Type: application/json
```

```json
{
  "eventId": "evt_87a2c1",
  "storedAt": "2026-01-21T14:33:13Z",
  "ttl": 7776000
}
```

### Step 6 вҖ” Latency Computation

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{DNS}} + T_{\text{TLS}} + T_{\text{request}} + T_{\text{server}} + T_{\text{response}} + T_{\text{network}}
$$

For a regional cloud endpoint: $T_{\text{total}} \approx 50$вҖ“$200$ ms.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: MQTT Publish-Subscribe Lifecycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> MQTT uses a **broker-mediated pub/sub model**. It is the **dominant IIoT protocol** and a KTU high-yield topic.

### Step 1 вҖ” Connect

$$
\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{CONNECT (clientId, keepAlive)}} \text{Broker}
$$

Broker replies:

$$
\text{Broker} \xrightarrow{\text{CONNACK (returnCode, sessionPresent)}} \text{Client}
$$

Return codes: `0` = Accepted, `1` = Refused (protocol version), `2` = Refused (identifier rejected), etc.

### Step 2 вҖ” Subscribe

$$
\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{SUBSCRIBE (packetId, topic, QoS)}} \text{Broker}
$$

Example: `SUBSCRIBE "factory/lineA/sensor/+/temperature", QoS=1`

The `+` is a **single-level wildcard**; `#` is a **multi-level wildcard**.

### Step 3 вҖ” Publish

$$
\text{Publisher} \xrightarrow{\text{PUBLISH (topic, payload, QoS, retain)}} \text{Broker}
$$

### Step 4 вҖ” QoS 2 Handshake (Exactly-Once Derivation)

The four-step handshake guarantees no duplication:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Publisher} &\xrightarrow{\text{PUBLISH}} \text{Broker} \\
\text{Broker} &\xrightarrow{\text{PUBREC}} \text{Publisher} \\
\text{Publisher} &\xrightarrow{\text{PUBREL}} \text{Broker} \\
\text{Broker} &\xrightarrow{\text{PUBCOMP}} \text{Publisher}
\end{aligned}
$$

This guarantees **exactly-once** delivery through a two-phase commit-like exchange.

### Step 5 вҖ” Topic Tree Resolution

MQTT topics are **UTF-8 strings** with hierarchical `/` separators:

$$
\text{Topic} = \text{Segment}_1 / \text{Segment}_2 / \dots / \text{Segment}_n
$$

Example: `factory/kerala/cochin/lineA/press01/temperature`

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right API Protocol

> [!NOTE]
> The decision matrix below is a **KTU favorite** for descriptive 14-mark questions.

| Criterion | REST/HTTP | MQTT | CoAP | SOAP | AMQP |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP | TCP | UDP | TCP/HTTP | TCP |
| **Payload Size** | Large (JSON/XML) | Tiny (2-byte header) | Tiny (4-byte header) | Large (XML only) | Medium |
| **Power Consumption** | High | Low | Very Low | High | Medium |
| **Latency** | 50вҖ“500 ms | 10вҖ“100 ms | 5вҖ“50 ms | 100вҖ“1000 ms | 20вҖ“200 ms |
| **Security** | TLS, OAuth | TLS, username/pwd | DTLS | WS-Security | TLS, SASL |
| **Pattern** | Request-Response | Pub-Sub | Request-Response | RPC | Pub-Sub + Queues |
| **Best For** | Cloud webhooks | Sensor telemetry | Constrained devices | Enterprise | Banking/Messaging |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Cloud Storage Durability Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Given:** AWS S3 Standard claims durability of $99.999999999\%$ over a year for a single object.
> **Find:** Probability of data loss over 10 years for 10 million objects.

### Step 1 вҖ” Annual Loss Probability

$$
P_{\text{loss, annual}} = 1 - 0.99999999999 = 1 \times 10^{-11}
$$

### Step 2 вҖ” 10-Year Loss Probability (assuming independence)

$$
P_{\text{loss, 10y}} \approx 10 \times 1 \times 10^{-11} = 1 \times 10^{-10}
$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Loss Probability Across 10 Million Objects

$$
P_{\text{any loss, 10M objects, 10y}} \approx 1 \times 10^{-10} \times 10^7 = 1 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
P_{\text{any loss, 10M objects, 10y}} \approx 0.001 = 0.1\%
$$

This shows that **even with eleven-9s durability, large-scale object fleets face measurable aggregate loss** вҖ” motivating cross-region replication.

---

## 3.5 API Authentication Derivation: JWT (JSON Web Token)

A JWT has three **Base64URL-encoded** parts:

$$
\text{JWT} = \underbrace{H}_{\text{Header}} . \underbrace{P}_{\text{Payload}} . \underbrace{S}_{\text{Signature}}
$$

### Step 1 вҖ” Header

```json
{"alg": "HS256", "typ": "JWT"}
$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Payload (Claims)

$$
\text{Claims} = \{ \text{sub}, \text{iat}, \text{exp}, \text{scope} \}
$$

Example:

```json
{"sub": "device:D042", "iat": 1737465600, "exp": 1737469200, "scope": "telemetry:write"}
```

### Step 3 вҖ” Signature

$$
S = \text{HMAC-SHA256}(\text{base64}(H) \; . \; \text{base64}(P), \; \text{secret})
$$

### Step 4 вҖ” Verification

The server independently computes $S'$ and compares:

$$
\text{Valid} \iff S = S' \quad \land \quad \text{now} < \text{exp}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 вҖ” Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied
All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `n1`, `step1`, `cloudBucket`). No reserved keywords are used. Labels are uppercase alphanumeric only, no bold/italic markers inside quoted labels.

---

## 4.2 Cloud Storage Architecture for IIoT

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[SENSOR LAYER PLC RTU SCADA] --> G1[EDGE GATEWAY]
    S2[SENSOR LAYER MOTOR VIBRATION TEMPERATURE] --> G1
    S3[SENSOR LAYER RFID BARCODE VISION] --> G1
    G1 -->|MQTT TLS| BR[MQTT BROKER EMQX MOSQUITTO]
    BR -->|RULE ENGINE| RT[REAL TIME PROCESSING STREAM ANALYTICS]
    RT -->|HOT TIER| DB1[STRUCTURED DATABASE DYNAMODB POSTGRES]
    RT -->|WARM TIER| DB2[OBJECT STORAGE AWS S3 AZURE BLOB]
    DB1 --> DASH[OPERATOR DASHBOARD REST API]
    DB2 --> DASH
    DB2 -->|COLD ARCHIVE| GL[GLACIER COLD STORAGE 11 NINES]
    DASH --> BI[BUSINESS INTELLIGENCE ERP SAP]
    BR -->|REST CALLBACK| EXT[THIRD PARTY WEBHOOK]
```

**Caption:** Sensor data flows from the OT layer through an MQTT broker, with hot/warm/cold tiering mapping different storage models. Hot tier = structured DB, warm tier = object storage, cold tier = archival blob.

---

## 4.3 REST API Request Response Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as IOT DEVICE CLIENT
    participant T as TLS HANDSHAKE
    participant A as AUTH VERIFIER OAUTH JWT
    participant S as REST API SERVER
    participant D as DATABASE STORAGE
    C->>T: CLIENT HELLO TLS 1.3
    T-->>C: SERVER HELLO FINISHED 0 RTT
    C->>A: HTTPS POST BEARER TOKEN JSON
    A-->>C: TOKEN VALIDATED 200
    C->>S: PROCESSED REQUEST ROUTE
    S->>D: QUERY OR WRITE
    D-->>S: RESULT SET
    S-->>C: HTTP 201 CREATED JSON RESPONSE
```

**Caption:** Six-stage sequence showing TLS establishment, JWT authentication, server-side processing, persistence, and structured response.

---

## 4.4 MQTT Pub Sub Broker Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PUBLISHER SENSOR 1] -->|PUBLISH TOPIC A| BR[(MQTT BROKER)]
    P2[PUBLISHER SENSOR 2] -->|PUBLISH TOPIC B| BR
    P3[PUBLISHER SENSOR 3] -->|PUBLISH TOPIC C| BR
    BR -->|MATCHED TOPIC| S1[SUBSCRIBER ACTUATOR]
    BR -->|MATCHED TOPIC| S2[SUBSCRIBER DASHBOARD]
    BR -->|MATCHED TOPIC| S3[SUBSCRIBER CLOUD ARCHIVE]
    BR -.->|WILL MESSAGE| W[LAST WILL AND TESTAMENT]
```

**Caption:** Brokers decouple publishers from subscribers using topics. The Last Will and Testament (LWT) is a pre-registered message sent automatically if a client disconnects unexpectedly вҖ” critical for IIoT fault detection.

---

## 4.5 Cloud Storage Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{DATA TYPE STRUCTURED OR UNSTRUCTURED} -->|STRUCTURED| Q2{NEED COMPLEX QUERIES}
    Q1 -->|UNSTRUCTURED| O1[USE OBJECT STORAGE S3 BLOB]
    Q2 -->|YES| SQL[USE SQL DATABASE POSTGRES MYSQL]
    Q2 -->|NO| KV[USE NOSQL KEY VALUE DYNAMODB REDIS]
    Q1 -->|BINARY LARGE| O1
    O1 --> Q3{ACCESS FREQUENCY}
    Q3 -->|HOT DAILY| S3W[STANDARD STORAGE S3 STANDARD]
    Q3 -->|WARM MONTHLY| S3I[INFREQUENT ACCESS S3 IA]
    Q3 -->|COLD YEARLY| S3G[GLACIER ARCHIVE]
```

**Caption:** Decision tree mapping data characteristics to the appropriate cloud storage class вҖ” directly relevant to KTU scenario questions on storage model selection.

---

## 4.6 API Protocol Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP_LAYER [APPLICATION LAYER IIOT STACK]
        R1[REST API WEBHOOKS]
        S1[SOAP ENTERPRISE RPC]
        M1[MQTT PUB SUB TELEMETRY]
        C1[COAP CONSTRAINED UDP]
        A1[AMQP QUEUE BASED]
        D1[DDS REAL TIME DISTRIBUTED]
    end
    subgraph TRANS_LAYER [TRANSPORT LAYER]
        T1[TCP RELIABLE]
        T2[UDP FAST]
    end
    R1 --> T1
    S1 --> T1
    M1 --> T1
    A1 --> T1
    C1 --> T2
    D1 --> T2
    D1 --> T1
```

**Caption:** IIoT application-layer protocols and their underlying transport bindings, illustrating why MQTT/CoAP dominate while SOAP recedes in modern IIoT.
