# SECTION 5 вҖ” KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Explain the difference between Object Storage and Block Storage in cloud computing. Give one IIoT use case for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Object Storage | Block Storage |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Unit** | Object (data + metadata + ID) | Fixed-size block (4вҖ“16 KB) |
| **Access Protocol** | HTTP REST (PUT/GET) | iSCSI / Fibre Channel / cloud API |
| **Hierarchy** | Flat namespace (bucket) | Logical Volume / LUN |
| **Scalability** | Virtually unlimited | Limited by volume size |
| **Performance** | Higher latency, high throughput | Low latency, high IOPS |
| **Best For** | Unstructured (images, video, logs) | Databases, VM disks |

**IIoT Use Cases:**
- **Object Storage:** Storing raw CCTV footage, firmware OTA binaries, sensor log archives.
- **Block Storage:** Hosting time-series databases (InfluxDB) or running MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems) on cloud VMs. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*List any three Quality of Service (QoS) levels in MQTT and explain the delivery guarantee of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| QoS | Name | Guarantee | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **QoS 0** | At most once | No acknowledgment, possible loss | Single PUBLISH packet |
| **QoS 1** | At least once | Acknowledged, possible duplicates | PUBLISH + PUBACK |
| **QoS 2** | Exactly once | Guaranteed single delivery | PUBLISH вҶ’ PUBREC вҶ’ PUBREL вҶ’ PUBCOMP (4-step handshake) |

**Engineering Note:** QoS 2 is preferred for billing or safety-critical commands (e.g., emergency shutdown), while QoS 0 is used for high-frequency non-critical telemetry. **(3 Marks)**

---

## PART B вҖ” 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE pattern: Each Module offers an internal choice (either-or). You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each has sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]**
**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of cloud-based data storage for an Industrial IoT system. Discuss the role of hot, warm, and cold storage tiers.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Compare REST and SOAP communication APIs in detail. Justify which is more suitable for a low-bandwidth IIoT edge gateway.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) вҖ” 7 Marks**

```
Block Diagram (to be drawn in exam):

  [Sensors/PLC] -> [Edge Gateway] -> [MQTT Broker] 
                                            |
                                  +---------+---------+
                                  |         |         |
                            [HOT DB]  [WARM S3]  [COLD GLACIER]
                              ^         ^            ^
                              |         |            |
                        [Dashboard] [Analytics] [Compliance Archive]
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Sensors/PLCs** generate raw industrial data (vibration, temperature, pressure).
2. **Edge Gateway** performs local filtering, aggregation, and protocol translation.
3. **MQTT Broker** (e.g., EMQX, Mosquitto) brokers messages to multiple subscribers.
4. **Storage Tiering:**
   - **Hot Tier:** Real-time data stored in low-latency databases (DynamoDB, InfluxDB) for live dashboards. Access latency **<10 ms**.
   - **Warm Tier:** Recent historical data in object storage (S3 Standard, Azure Blob Hot) for analytics. Access latency **~50 ms**.
   - **Cold Tier:** Long-term archival in glacier-class storage for compliance, audits, ML retraining. Access latency **minutes to hours**, but cost is **~10x cheaper**.
5. **Consumers:** Dashboards, analytics engines, regulatory archives all tap into the appropriate tier. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Part (b) вҖ” 7 Marks**

| Parameter | REST | SOAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Acronym** | REpresentational State Transfer | Simple Object Access Protocol |
| **Data Format** | JSON (preferred), XML | XML only |
| **Transport** | HTTP/HTTPS | HTTP, SMTP, TCP, JMS |
| **State** | Stateless | Stateful or stateless |
| **Contract** | OpenAPI (optional) | WSDL (mandatory) |
| **Payload Size** | Small (JSON) | Large (XML envelope) |
| **Security** | TLS + OAuth/JWT | WS-Security + TLS |
| **Caching** | HTTP-native | Difficult |
| **Tooling** | Lightweight, browser-friendly | Heavy, enterprise-only |

**Justification for IIoT Edge Gateway:**

For a **low-bandwidth, resource-constrained edge gateway**, **REST with JSON is more suitable** because:

1. **JSON payloads are 30вҖ“60% smaller** than equivalent SOAP/XML envelopes, conserving uplink bandwidth.
2. **REST is stateless**, fitting the typical request-response nature of sensor data uploads.
3. **Easier integration** with lightweight clients (Python `requests`, Node.js, embedded C via libcurl).
4. **No WSDL dependency** вҖ” the gateway only needs to know the endpoint URL.

SOAP remains preferred only in **enterprise banking, healthcare, or SAP-integrated scenarios** where transactional integrity, WS-Security, and ACID-level contracts outweigh payload overhead. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**(a)** *Describe the four logical data storage models used in cloud platforms with suitable examples. How does each apply to a Smart Factory use case?* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the MQTT protocol stack. With a sequence diagram description, illustrate the QoS Level 2 (Exactly Once) message exchange.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) вҖ” 7 Marks**

The **four logical data storage models** in cloud platforms are:

1. **File Storage**
   - Organized as folders and files in a hierarchy.
   - Accessed via NFS, SMB.
   - **Smart Factory Example:** Storing engineering drawings, machine configuration files, operator manuals on a shared drive accessible to multiple shop-floor terminals.

2. **Block Storage**
   - Splits data into fixed-size blocks (4вҖ“16 KB).
   - Accessed via iSCSI/Fibre Channel or cloud-native APIs (AWS EBS, Azure Disk).
   - **Smart Factory Example:** Boot disk of the MES server running on a cloud VM; underlying storage for the PostgreSQL database holding real-time OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) data.

3. **Object Storage**
   - Flat namespace with buckets; each object has payload + metadata + unique key.
   - Accessed via REST API.
   - **Smart Factory Example:** Archiving terabytes of CCTV footage from production lines, or storing firmware binaries for OTA updates to PLCs.

4. **Database Storage**
   - SQL (relational) or NoSQL (document, key-value, column, graph).
   - **Smart Factory Example:**
     - **SQL:** Storing BOM (Bill of Materials), work orders, inventory.
     - **NoSQL (Time-Series DB):** Storing high-frequency sensor telemetry from hundreds of machines.

**Summary Table:**

| Storage Model | Format | Access | Smart Factory Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| File | Files/Directories | NFS/SMB | Manuals, drawings |
| Block | Blocks | iSCSI/API | MES databases |
| Object | Objects + Metadata | REST | Video, firmware |
| Database | Tables/Documents | SQL/NoSQL | OEE, telemetry | **(7 Marks)**

---

**Part (b) вҖ” 7 Marks**

**MQTT Protocol Stack:**

```
+--------------------------------+
|     APPLICATION (Publishers/   |
|        Subscribers)            |
+--------------------------------+
|     MQTT CONTROL PACKETS       |
|  (CONNECT, PUBLISH, SUBSCRIBE) |
+--------------------------------+
|     TRANSPORT (TCP)            |
+--------------------------------+
|     NETWORK (IP)               |
+--------------------------------+
|     LINK (Ethernet/Wi-Fi/5G)   |
+--------------------------------+
```

**Key Features:**
- Header is only **2 bytes** (vs HTTP's variable large header).
- Runs over **TCP port 1883** (plain) or **8883** (TLS).
- Uses a **broker** (e.g., Mosquitto, EMQX, HiveMQ) to mediate between clients.
- Supports **wildcards**: `+` (single-level), `#` (multi-level).

**QoS Level 2 вҖ” Exactly-Once Sequence:**

```
Publisher                 Broker
    |                       |
    |---- PUBLISH --------->|
    |                       |
    |<--- PUBREC -----------|
    |                       |
    |---- PUBREL ---------->|
    |                       |
    |<--- PUBCOMP ----------|
    |                       |
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation:**

1. **PUBLISH** вҖ” Publisher sends message with QoS=2, message gets a unique Packet Identifier (PID).
2. **PUBREC** вҖ” Broker acknowledges receipt and takes **responsibility** for the message; the publisher's copy is marked for deletion.
3. **PUBREL** вҖ” Publisher releases the message, confirming it has given up its copy.
4. **PUBCOMP** вҖ” Broker confirms completion, finalizing the exactly-once contract.

This four-step handshake ensures **no message is lost AND no duplicate is delivered**, which is mandatory for billing-grade or safety-critical IIoT commands. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning вҖ” Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing Object Storage with File Storage.** Object storage uses a **flat bucket** вҖ” there are no real "folders". Saying "object storage has directories" will cost you 1вҖ“2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up REST and SOAP.** REST is **not a protocol**; it is an **architectural style**. SOAP is an actual **protocol**. This distinction is worth 2 marks in 14-mark answers.
> 3. **Forgetting to draw the block diagram.** KTU explicitly mandates a "neat block diagram" in 7-mark sub-parts вҖ” omitting it typically incurs a **1 to 2-mark deduction** even if the text is correct.
> 4. **Skipping QoS 2 four-step handshake.** Many students stop at PUBLISH вҶ’ PUBACK. KTU examiners expect the **full PUBREC вҶ’ PUBREL вҶ’ PUBCOMP** sequence for full 7 marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning TLS/Port numbers.** Always specify that MQTT uses **port 8883 (TLS)** and **port 1883 (plain)** вҖ” examiners reward this engineering specificity.
> 6. **Confusing API Gateway with API.** A **gateway** is a runtime proxy; an **API** is the contract. Do not interchange them.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- вҳ‘ **Cloud Storage Models** = logical abstractions: File / Block / Object / Database.
- вҳ‘ **Service Architectures** = Public, Private, Hybrid (and Community).
- вҳ‘ **Service Models** = IaaS / PaaS / SaaS вҖ” remember the responsibility split.
- вҳ‘ **Durability of S3 = 11 nines** ($99.999999999\%$). Availability is usually **99.9%** to **99.99%**.
- вҳ‘ **Object Storage** has **flat namespace + metadata + REST API**; **no real folders**.
- вҳ‘ **Block Storage** is for **databases and VM disks** вҖ” sub-ms latency.
- вҳ‘ **REST** = architectural style + HTTP + JSON + stateless; **Level 2** (URIs + HTTP verbs) is "true REST".
- вҳ‘ **SOAP** = protocol + XML only + WSDL required + WS-Security.
- вҳ‘ **MQTT** = 2-byte header, TCP, **pub/sub**, three QoS levels (0, 1, 2).
- вҳ‘ **QoS 2 four-step handshake**: PUBLISH вҶ’ PUBREC вҶ’ PUBREL вҶ’ PUBCOMP.
- вҳ‘ **MQTT Ports**: **1883** (plain), **8883** (TLS).
- вҳ‘ **MQTT Wildcards**: `+` (single level), `#` (multi level).
- вҳ‘ **CoAP** = UDP-based, **4-byte header**, for constrained devices.
- вҳ‘ **AMQP** = TCP, enterprise-grade, queue-based.
- вҳ‘ **DDS** = real-time, supports both UDP and TCP.
- вҳ‘ **HTTP Status Codes**: 2xx success, 4xx client error, 5xx server error вҖ” remember **200, 201, 204, 400, 401, 404, 500, 503**.
- вҳ‘ **JWT** = `Header.Payload.Signature` (Base64URL encoded).
- вҳ‘ **Hot/Warm/Cold tiering** is the industry-standard IIoT storage pattern.
- вҳ‘ **Last Will and Testament (LWT)** is MQTT's fault-detection mechanism.
- вҳ‘ Always draw a **block diagram** in 7-mark sub-parts.
- вҳ‘ Always **state assumptions** (e.g., "assuming independence of failures") in derivations.
- вҳ‘ In comparisons, use **tables with 6+ rows** вҖ” KTU examiners reward depth.

---

**END OF MODULE 3 NOTES вҖ” INTRODUCTION TO CLOUD STORAGE MODELS AND COMMUNICATION APIs**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Application Framework
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT525 - INDUSTRY 4.0 AND INDUSTRIAL INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Web Application Framework

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Web Application Framework (WAF):** A software framework designed to support the development of web applications including web services, web resources, and web APIs. It provides a standard way to build and deploy web applications on the World Wide Web, automating the overhead associated with common activities performed in web development.

In the context of **Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)** under KTU 2024 Scheme, a Web Application Framework serves as the **middleware layer** that bridges raw sensor/actuator data (from the edge/perception layer) with end-user visualization, control interfaces, and business intelligence dashboards.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Restaurant Kitchen" Analogy рҹҚҪпёҸ**

Imagine an IIoT factory floor where sensors are constantly producing raw data (temperature, pressure, vibration, humidity). This data is like **raw ingredients** arriving at a busy restaurant kitchen. A Web Application Framework is the **structured kitchen system** вҖ” it provides:

| Layer in Restaurant | Equivalent in Web Framework | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Waiter (Takes Order) | **Routing Layer** | Directs incoming HTTP requests to the correct handler |
| Chef (Prepares Food) | **Controller/Logic Layer** | Processes business rules, executes decisions |
| Recipe Book | **Model Layer** | Defines data structures and database interaction |
| Plated Dish | **View/Template Layer** | Renders final HTML/JSON response for user |
| Kitchen Equipment | **Middleware** | Authentication, logging, session management, error handling |

Without a framework, you would have to manually build every "kitchen tool" from scratch вҖ” reinventing authentication, database connections, and routing every single time.

### 1.3 Core Terminology for KTU 2024 Syllabus

- **Framework vs. Library:** A *library* is called by your code; a *framework* calls your code (Inversion of Control).
- **MVC Pattern:** Model-View-Controller architectural separation.
- **REST API:** Representational State Transfer вҖ” stateless communication protocol.
- **WebSockets:** Full-duplex communication channel over a single TCP connection.
- **Middleware:** Software that processes requests before they reach the application logic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry 4.0 Relevance:** In KTU's PEEIT525 syllabus, web application frameworks are the **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** layer. They convert raw MQTT/CoAP sensor data streams into graphical dashboards used by plant managers to monitor machinery in real-time.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Components of a Web Application Framework

A typical IIoT-enabled web framework operates across **four conceptual pillars**:

#### Pillar 1: Frontend (Client-Side)
- **Technologies:** HTML5, CSS3, JavaScript (ES6+), TypeScript
- **Modern Frameworks:** React.js, Angular, Vue.js
- **Role in IIoT:** Renders real-time dashboards (e.g., Chart.js, D3.js for sensor trends)
- **Communication Protocol:** WebSockets (`ws://`), Server-Sent Events (SSE), AJAX

#### Pillar 2: Backend (Server-Side)
- **Languages:** Python, JavaScript (Node.js), Java, Ruby, Go, C#
- **Popular Frameworks:**
  - **Django** (Python) вҖ” Batteries-included, ORM built-in
  - **Express.js** (Node.js) вҖ” Minimalist, fast, ideal for IIoT microservices
  - **Flask** (Python) вҖ” Lightweight, microframework
  - **Spring Boot** (Java) вҖ” Enterprise-grade
- **Role in IIoT:** Handles REST API endpoints, MQTT broker integration, device authentication

#### Pillar 3: Database Layer
- **Relational:** PostgreSQL, MySQL (for structured sensor metadata)
- **NoSQL:** MongoDB, InfluxDB (for time-series sensor data)
- **Time-Series Optimized:** InfluxDB, TimescaleDB (specifically for IIoT telemetry)
- **In-Memory:** Redis (for caching real-time alerts)

#### Pillar 4: Communication & Integration Layer
- **MQTT over WebSockets:** Port 9001 (browser-friendly MQTT)
- **REST/HTTP:** Port 80/443 (most common API style)
- **gRPC:** High-performance RPC for inter-service communication
- **OPC-UA:** Industrial protocol bridge for legacy SCADA systems

### 2.2 The MVC (Model-View-Controller) Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MVC is the foundational architectural pattern tested in KTU exams.**

```
Request Flow: User вҶ’ View вҶ’ Controller вҶ’ Model вҶ’ Database вҶ’ Model вҶ’ View вҶ’ Response
```

- **Model:** Encapsulates data and business logic. Example: A `SensorReading` model containing temperature, timestamp, device_id.
- **View:** Presentation layer. Renders the final user interface.
- **Controller:** Receives input, processes it (via the Model), and returns the appropriate View.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| HTTP Request Latency | $T_{total} = T_{network} + T_{processing} + T_{database}$ | Milliseconds (ms) |
| Throughput (Requests/sec) | $\theta = \frac{N_{requests}}{T_{window}}$ | Requests per second (RPS) |
| Response Time SLA | $T_{p99} \leq T_{SLA}$ | 99th percentile latency must meet SLA |
| Concurrent Connections | $C_{max} = \frac{Memory_{server}}{M_{per\_connection}}$ | Depends on runtime (Node.js ~10MB) |
| WebSocket Frame Size | $S_{frame} \leq 2^{63} - 1$ bytes | Per RFC 6455 |
| MQTT QoS Levels | 0 (At most once), 1 (At least once), 2 (Exactly once) | Reliability guarantees |
| Database Index Lookup | $T_{lookup} = O(\log n)$ for B-Tree | Indexed queries |
| Full Table Scan | $T_{scan} = O(n)$ | Avoid in real-time IIoT |
| Cache Hit Ratio | $H = \frac{Hits}{Hits + Misses}$ | Target: $H \geq 0.90$ |
| Polling Interval vs Push | $E_{polling} = \frac{D_{frame}}{T_{interval}} \times N_{clients}$ | WebSockets reduce overhead by ~95% |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in IIoT

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application:** In a smart manufacturing plant, a Django backend subscribes to MQTT topics published by PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers). When a vibration sensor on a CNC machine exceeds threshold, the controller triggers a Model update, which pushes a WebSocket notification to all connected dashboards within **< 200ms**, enabling predictive maintenance.

### 2.5 Key Design Principles (GRASP / SOLID for Frameworks)

- **DRY:** Don't Repeat Yourself
- **KISS:** Keep It Simple, Stupid
- **YAGNI:** You Aren't Gonna Need It
- **Separation of Concerns:** Each module has one responsibility
- **Statelessness:** REST APIs must be stateless per REST constraints (Roy Fielding, 2000)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Request-Response Lifecycle in an IIoT Web Application

Let us derive the **end-to-end flow** of an HTTP request in an Industry 4.0 context вҖ” from the moment a sensor publishes data to the moment it appears on a plant manager's dashboard.

**Step 1: Sensor Data Acquisition (Edge Layer)**

An IIoT sensor (e.g., DHT22 temperature sensor) publishes a JSON payload via MQTT to a broker:

$$
Payload = \{ "device\_id": "CNC\_01", "temp": 78.4, "ts": 1719842400 \}
$$

**Step 2: Broker-to-Backend Bridge**

The web framework (e.g., Node.js with Express.js) runs an MQTT subscriber that listens on topic `factory/cnc/temperature`.

**Step 3: Controller Invocation**

When a message arrives, the framework invokes the controller function:

$$
f_{controller}(payload) \rightarrow Model.update(payload)
$$

**Step 4: Model Layer (Database Persistence)**

$$
T_{persistence} = T_{insert} + T_{index\_update}
$$

For InfluxDB (time-series DB):

$$
T_{insert} \approx 1-5 \text{ ms per point}
$$

**Step 5: Cache Invalidation**

If the value is "fresh" (within last 5 seconds), update Redis cache:

$$
\text{Redis Key: } K = "device:CNC\_01:temp"
$$

$$
\text{Value: } V = \{ "temp": 78.4, "ts": 1719842400 \}
$$

**Step 6: WebSocket Broadcast**

The framework pushes the new value to all connected dashboard clients:

$$
N_{broadcast} = N_{connected\_clients} \times S_{frame}
$$

For 50 clients with 100-byte frames:

$$
N_{broadcast} = 50 \times 100 = 5000 \text{ bytes per update}
$$

**Step 7: Client-Side Render**

The frontend JavaScript (e.g., React) receives the WebSocket message and updates the DOM:

$$
\Delta t_{render} \approx 16 \text{ ms (60 FPS target)}
$$

**Step 8: Total End-to-End Latency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{end-to-end} &= T_{MQTT} + T_{controller} + T_{persistence} + T_{websocket} + T_{render} \\
&= 5 + 2 + 4 + 3 + 16 \\
&= 30 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is well within the **< 100 ms** threshold required for real-time IIoT monitoring.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Framework Selection for IIoT

| Criterion | Django | Express.js | Flask | Spring Boot |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Language | Python | JavaScript | Python | Java |
| Learning Curve | Moderate | Easy | Easy | Steep |
| Async Support | Partial (ASGI) | Native | Limited | Native |
| IIoT Suitability | High (admin panel) | **Very High** (MQTT.js) | Medium | High (enterprise) |
| WebSocket Support | Channels | ws library | Flask-SocketIO | Spring WebSocket |
| Built-in Admin | Yes | No | No | No |
| Cold Start Time | ~800 ms | ~50 ms | ~200 ms | ~2500 ms |
| Memory Footprint | ~150 MB | ~80 MB | ~60 MB | ~300 MB |
| KTU Exam Relevance | High | Very High | High | Medium |

### 3.3 Derivation: Polling vs. WebSocket Overhead Comparison

**Polling Approach (Client requests every T seconds):**

$$
\text{Requests per hour} = \frac{3600}{T} \text{ requests/hour/client}
$$

$$
\text{Server load} = \frac{3600}{T} \times N_{clients} \text{ requests/hour}
$$

For $T = 5$ seconds and $N_{clients} = 100$:

$$
\text{Server load} = \frac{3600}{5} \times 100 = 72000 \text{ requests/hour}
$$

**WebSocket Approach (Persistent connection):**

$$
\text{Server load} = 1 \text{ connection/client} + N_{pushes}
$$

For 100 clients and 1 push/second average:

$$
\text{Server load} = 100 \text{ connections} + 3600 \text{ pushes/hour} = 3600 \text{ messages/hour}
$$

**Efficiency Gain:**

$$
\text{Reduction Factor} = \frac{72000}{3600} = 20\times
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 20x reduction is **why KTU examiners emphasize WebSockets over polling** in IIoT dashboard design questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Three-Tier IIoT Web Application Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph edge["EDGE LAYER - Sensors and Actuators"]
        S1["Temperature Sensor"]
        S2["Vibration Sensor"]

    subgraph broker["COMMUNICATION LAYER - MQTT Broker"]

        TP2["Topic factory/vibration"]

    subgraph backend["BACKEND LAYER - Web Application Framework"]
        R["Express.js Router"]
        C1["Temperature Controller"]
        C2["Vibration Controller"]
        C3["Pressure Controller"]
        M1["SensorReading Model"]
        DB["InfluxDB Time Series DB"]

    end

    subgraph frontend["FRONTEND LAYER - Dashboard"]
        UI1["React Dashboard"]
        UI2["Mobile PWA"]
        UI3["Control Room Monitor"]

    end

    S1 --> TP1
    S2 --> TP2
    S3 --> TP3
    S4 --> TP1

    TP1 --> MQ
    TP2 --> MQ
    TP3 --> MQ

    MQ --> R

    R --> C2
    R --> C3

    C1 --> M1
    C2 --> M1

    M1 --> WS

    API --> DB

```

### 4.2 MVC Pattern Flow Inside the Backend

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Browser
    participant V as View Template
    participant CT as Controller
    participant M as Model
    participant DB as Database

    U->>CT: HTTP GET /devices/CNC_01/temperature
    Note over CT: Controller receives HTTP request
    CT->>M: device.getTemperature(CNC_01)
    M->>DB: SELECT * FROM readings WHERE device_id = CNC_01
    DB-->>M: Returns sensor data rows
    M-->>CT: Returns SensorReading object
    CT->>V: render(dashboard.html, data)
    V-->>U: HTTP 200 OK with HTML page
    Note over U: Dashboard displayed with real-time data
```

### 4.3 Web Application Framework Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Choose Web Framework for IIoT Project"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question A1:** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define Web Application Framework. List any four popular frameworks with their primary languages.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Web Application Framework (WAF)** is a software framework that provides a standardized structure for developing, deploying, and managing web applications, web services, and web APIs. It abstracts common low-level tasks (HTTP handling, session management, database access) so developers focus on business logic.

**Four Popular Frameworks:**

| Framework | Language | Primary Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Django | Python | Full-stack with admin panel |
| Express.js | JavaScript (Node.js) | Lightweight REST APIs |
| Spring Boot | Java | Enterprise applications |
| Ruby on Rails | Ruby | Rapid prototyping |

*(1 mark definition + 0.5 per framework row = 3 marks)*

---

**Question A2:** **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Differentiate between REST API and WebSocket communication in the context of IIoT.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | REST API | WebSocket |
|---|---|---|
| Communication Pattern | Request-Response (Half-duplex) | Full-duplex, bidirectional |
| Connection | New TCP per request | Persistent single connection |
| Server Push | Not native (requires polling) | Native push support |
| Overhead | High (HTTP headers per request) | Low (frames after handshake) |
| IIoT Use Case | Device configuration, reporting | Real-time sensor dashboards |
| Protocol | HTTP/HTTPS | ws:// or wss:// |

**Verdict:** For real-time IIoT monitoring, **WebSocket is preferred**; for one-off CRUD operations, **REST API is preferred**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain the MVC (Model-View-Controller) architectural pattern in detail. With a neat diagram, describe how it processes an HTTP request in an IIoT dashboard application. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast polling-based and WebSocket-based real-time data delivery mechanisms in IIoT applications. Derive the server load for both approaches and justify which is more suitable for a smart factory monitoring system with 100 concurrent users. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” Alternative Choice:**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the typical three-tier architecture of an IIoT web application. Identify and explain the role of at least five components in each tier, with reference to a specific web framework of your choice. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sequence/flow diagram, explain the complete request-response lifecycle when a temperature sensor value is displayed on a plant manager's dashboard. Include all intermediate layers (MQTT broker, controller, model, database, WebSocket). **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

#### **Question A Solution:**

**(a) MVC Pattern Explanation (7 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> MVC is a software architectural pattern that separates an application into three interconnected components.

**Step 1 (1 Mark) вҖ” Model Layer:**
- Encapsulates application data and business logic.
- In IIoT: `SensorReading` model with fields `device_id`, `temperature`, `timestamp`.
- Interacts with database (PostgreSQL/InfluxDB).

**Step 2 (1 Mark) вҖ” View Layer:**
- Presentation logic. Renders HTML, JSON, or XML.
- In IIoT: Dashboard with Chart.js graphs, real-time gauges.
- Modern views use templating engines (Jinja2, EJS) or JSX (React).

**Step 3 (1 Mark) вҖ” Controller Layer:**
- Acts as intermediary between Model and View.
- Receives HTTP request, invokes appropriate Model method, selects View.
- In IIoT: `GET /api/v1/devices/CNC_01/temperature` вҶ’ `TemperatureController.get()`.

**Step 4 (2 Marks) вҖ” Request Flow Diagram:**

```
Browser вҶ’ View вҶ’ Controller вҶ’ Model вҶ’ Database вҶ’ Model вҶ’ View вҶ’ Browser
```

**Step 5 (2 Marks) вҖ” IIoT Example:**
- User opens dashboard for CNC machine 01.
- View requests current temperature from Controller.
- Controller calls `SensorModel.getLatest("CNC_01")`.
- Model queries InfluxDB, returns reading.
- Controller passes data to View template.
- View renders gauge widget with current temperature.

---

**(b) Polling vs WebSocket Comparison (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark) вҖ” Polling Definition:**
- Client repeatedly sends HTTP GET requests at fixed intervals (e.g., every 5 seconds) to fetch new data.

**Step 2 (1 Mark) вҖ” WebSocket Definition:**
- Single persistent TCP connection established via HTTP upgrade handshake; server can push data anytime.

**Step 3 (2 Marks) вҖ” Server Load Derivation:**

For Polling at interval $T = 5$ seconds with $N = 100$ clients:

$$
\text{Requests/hour} = \frac{3600}{5} \times 100 = 72000 \text{ requests/hour}
$$

For WebSocket:

$$
\text{Connections} = 100, \text{Messages/hour} = 3600 \text{ (1 push/sec avg)}
$$

$$
\text{Total load} = 100 \text{ connections} + 3600 \text{ pushes} = 3700 \text{ messages/hour}
$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks) вҖ” Efficiency Ratio:**

$$
\text{Reduction Factor} = \frac{72000}{3700} \approx 19.46\times
$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark) вҖ” Justification:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a smart factory monitoring system with 100 concurrent users requiring **real-time** updates, **WebSocket is the clear choice**. It reduces server load by ~20x, provides sub-second latency, and enables bidirectional communication for remote actuator control.

---

#### **Question B Solution:**

**(a) Three-Tier Architecture (7 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> Three-tier architecture separates concerns into presentation, application logic, and data storage layers.

**Step 1 (2 Marks) вҖ” Presentation Tier:**
- User Interface Layer
- Components: HTML5, CSS3, JavaScript, React.js framework, Chart.js library, WebSocket client
- Example: Operator console showing live factory floor status

**Step 2 (2 Marks) вҖ” Application Tier (Logic Layer):**
- Web framework backend (e.g., Django + Django REST Framework)
- Components: REST API endpoints, MQTT subscriber, authentication middleware, business logic services, WebSocket server (Django Channels)
- Example: Python service receiving MQTT messages and orchestrating responses

**Step 3 (2 Marks) вҖ” Data Tier:**
- Persistent storage and caching
- Components: InfluxDB (time-series sensor data), PostgreSQL (device metadata), Redis (real-time cache), MQTT broker (Mosquitto)
- Example: Historical temperature data stored with 1-year retention policy

**Step 4 (1 Mark) вҖ” Integration:**
The three tiers communicate via well-defined protocols: HTTP/REST (presentation вҶ” application), TCP/MQTT (application вҶ” broker), SQL/NoSQL queries (application вҶ” data).

---

**(b) Request-Response Lifecycle (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark) вҖ” Sensor Publishes Data:**
A DHT22 sensor on CNC machine reads temperature 78.4В°C and publishes JSON to MQTT topic `factory/cnc/01/temperature`.

**Step 2 (1 Mark) вҖ” MQTT Broker Forwards:**
Mosquitto broker receives the message and forwards to all subscribers, including the web framework's MQTT client.

**Step 3 (1 Mark) вҖ” Backend Controller Receives:**
Express.js controller `mqttSubscriber.on('message', handler)` is triggered. The handler validates payload schema.

**Step 4 (1 Mark) вҖ” Model Layer Persists:**
`SensorReading.create({device_id, temperature, timestamp})` inserts row into InfluxDB.

**Step 5 (1 Mark) вҖ” WebSocket Broadcast:**
The server emits a WebSocket event `sensor:update` to all connected dashboard clients with the new reading.

**Step 6 (1 Mark) вҖ” Frontend Receives and Renders:**
React component receives event, updates state, Chart.js redraws the gauge in < 16ms.

**Step 7 (1 Mark) вҖ” Total Latency Calculation:**

$$
T_{total} = T_{mqtt} + T_{controller} + T_{db} + T_{ws} + T_{render} \approx 30 \text{ ms}
$$

**Sequence Diagram:**

```
Sensor вҶ’ MQTT Broker вҶ’ Controller вҶ’ Model вҶ’ DB
                                      вҶ“
                            WebSocket Server вҶ’ React UI
```

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Framework with Library:** A library is *called by* your code; a framework *calls your code* (Inversion of Control). Students often lose 1 mark by omitting this distinction.
>
> 2. **Skipping Units in Numerical Answers:** For latency calculations like $T_{total} = 30 \text{ ms}$, forgetting the unit loses 0.5вҖ“1 mark.
>
> 3. **Not Drawing Diagrams:** KTU board examiners explicitly allocate 2вҖ“3 marks for "neat diagrams." Skipping the MVC flow diagram or sequence diagram costs heavily.
>
> 4. **Forgetting IIoT Context:** Generic web framework answers without IIoT examples (sensors, MQTT, dashboards) score lower. Always tie back to Industry 4.0.
>
> 5. **Wrong Formula for Throughput:** Many students write $\theta = N/T$ without units. Always state **requests/second**.
>
> 6. **Ignoring Security:** Authentication, CORS, JWT, and HTTPS are frequently tested as 1-mark sub-points. Do not omit.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Web Application Frameworks (Module 3):**

- вң… Web Application Framework = structured toolkit for building web apps with **Inversion of Control**.
- вң… Three-Tier Architecture: **Presentation вҶ’ Application Logic вҶ’ Data**.
- вң… MVC Pattern: **Model (data) вҶ’ View (UI) вҶ’ Controller (logic)** вҖ” request flows through all three.
- вң… Popular Frameworks: Django (Python), Express.js (Node.js), Flask, Spring Boot (Java).
- вң… **REST API** = stateless, request-response, best for CRUD operations.
- вң… **WebSocket** = persistent, full-duplex, best for **real-time IIoT dashboards**.
- вң… MQTT over WebSockets (port 9001) bridges MQTT devices to browser dashboards.
- вң… Time-series databases (InfluxDB) are preferred over RDBMS for sensor data.
- вң… Polling load = $72000$ req/hour vs WebSocket load = $3700$ msg/hour вҶ’ **WebSocket is 20x more efficient**.
- вң… End-to-end IIoT latency target: **< 100 ms** for real-time monitoring.
- вң… DRY, KISS, SOLID principles apply to web framework design.
- вң… Statelessness is a core REST constraint (Roy Fielding, 2000).
- вң… Middleware handles cross-cutting concerns: auth, logging, CORS, rate-limiting.
- вң… Web Application Frameworks form the **HMI layer** of the IIoT Reference Architecture (RAMI 4.0).
- вң… Cache hit ratio target: $H \geq 0.90$ for optimal performance.
- вң… Always include **units, diagrams, and IIoT context** in KTU exam answers.

---

**End of Notes вҖ” Module 3, Topic: Web Application Framework**
**Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | PEEIT525 | Industry 4.0 and IIoT**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT525 - INDUSTRY 4.0 AND INDUSTRIAL INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Designing a Web API

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Web API (Application Programming Interface)** is a programmatic interface that allows software applications to communicate with each other over the Internet using standardized web protocols, primarily the **HTTP/HTTPS** protocol. In the context of **Industry 4.0 and the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)**, a Web API acts as the **middleware abstraction layer** that enables heterogeneous industrial devices (PLCs, sensors, actuators, SCADA systems, MES platforms) to expose their telemetry, control signals, and configuration parameters to higher-level applications such as cloud dashboards, analytics engines, and digital twin systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition**: A Web API in IIoT is a **RESTful (Representational State Transfer)** or **HTTP-based service contract** that defines a set of endpoints, request/response schemas, authentication mechanisms, and operational semantics enabling machine-to-machine (M2M) communication across the **EdgeвҖ“FogвҖ“Cloud continuum**.

### Key Web API Architectural Styles

| Style | Full Form | Data Format | State |
|---|---|---|---|
| **REST** | Representational State Transfer | JSON / XML | Stateless |
| **SOAP** | Simple Object Access Protocol | XML | Stateless |
| **GraphQL** | Graph Query Language | JSON | Stateless |
| **gRPC** | gRPC Remote Procedure Call | Protobuf | Stateless |
| **WebSocket** | Full-Duplex Communication | JSON / Binary | Stateful / Stateful |
| **MQTT** (over WebSockets) | Message Queuing Telemetry Transport | Binary | Session-based |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU examinations, **REST (RESTful Web API)** is the dominant style, and the syllabus emphasizes it for IIoT integration.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Plain English Analogy вҖ” The Restaurant Menu Analogy:**

Imagine you walk into a restaurant. You (the **client application** or **sensor dashboard**) want food (the **data** or **control command**). You don't go directly into the kitchen and start cooking вҖ” that would be chaos. Instead:

- рҹҚҪпёҸ **The Menu** = The **Web API Documentation** (it lists what is available and how to order it)
- рҹ‘ЁвҖҚрҹҚі **The Waiter** = The **API Endpoint** (it takes your request and returns a response)
- рҹҚІ **The Dish** = The **Resource (Data)** (e.g., temperature reading, motor status, machine telemetry)
- рҹ“қ **Your Order Slip** = The **HTTP Request** (with method: GET, POST, etc.)
- рҹҚӣ **The Plate Returned** = The **HTTP Response** (with status code + data payload)

In an **IIoT factory**, thousands of sensors continuously generate data. The Web API is the "waiter" that serves this data to any authorized client вҖ” be it a **cloud analytics platform**, a **mobile operator dashboard**, or a **machine-learning inference engine** вҖ” without the client needing to know the internal sensor hardware, firmware, or proprietary protocol.

### Core Design Constraints to Remember

- **HTTP/HTTPS Protocol** is the transport.
- **Statelessness** вҖ” Every request is independent; the server does not remember previous client state.
- **Resource-Oriented** вҖ” URLs identify *resources* (nouns), not actions (verbs).
- **Standard Status Codes** вҖ” `200 OK`, `404 Not Found`, `500 Internal Server Error`, etc.
- **Content Negotiation** вҖ” Client requests format via `Accept` header (e.g., `application/json`).

> [!TIP]
> **IIoT Context**: In an IIoT deployment, a sensor publishing temperature every 5 seconds to `https://api.factory.com/v1/sensors/sensor-001/temperature` is a textbook Web API interaction. The endpoint design follows REST principles.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The REST Architectural Style вҖ” Fielding Constraints

Dr. **Roy Fielding** (2000 dissertation) defined REST through **6 architectural constraints**. A Web API claiming to be "RESTful" must satisfy all six:

1. **ClientвҖ“Server Separation** вҖ” UI and data storage concerns are separated.
2. **Statelessness** вҖ” Each request contains all information needed to be processed; no client session is stored on the server.
3. **Cacheability** вҖ” Responses must define themselves as cacheable or non-cacheable.
4. **Uniform Interface** вҖ” Standardized resource identification, manipulation via representations, self-descriptive messages, and **HATEOAS** (Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State).
5. **Layered System** вҖ” Client cannot tell if it's connected directly to the end server or via an intermediary (gateway, load balancer, firewall).
6. **Code-On-Demand (Optional)** вҖ” Server can transfer executable code (e.g., JavaScript) to client.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU, the **Stateless**, **ClientвҖ“Server**, **Uniform Interface**, and **Layered System** constraints are the most-tested. **HATEOAS** is frequently asked as a 3-mark question.

---

## 2.2 HTTP Methods вҖ” The Verb Set for Resources

HTTP methods are **idempotent** or **non-idempotent** operations. This is a **highly tested KTU concept**.

| HTTP Method | Operation | Idempotent? | Safe? | Typical Use Case in IIoT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GET** | Retrieve resource representation | вң… Yes | вң… Yes | Read sensor value, fetch device list |
| **POST** | Create new resource / trigger action | вқҢ No | вқҢ No | Register new sensor, log an alarm event |
| **PUT** | Replace entire resource | вң… Yes | вқҢ No | Update full device configuration |
| **PATCH** | Partial update of resource | вқҢ No (generally) | вқҢ No | Update only the threshold value |
| **DELETE** | Remove resource | вң… Yes | вқҢ No | Decommission a sensor, clear log |
| **HEAD** | Same as GET but no body | вң… Yes | вң… Yes | Check resource existence / headers |
| **OPTIONS** | Describe communication options | вң… Yes | вң… Yes | CORS pre-flight, capability discovery |

> [!NOTE]
> **Idempotency** means applying the same operation multiple times produces the **same result** as applying it once. This is critical in IIoT where **network retransmissions** are common over unreliable industrial wireless links.

### Mathematical Notation of Idempotency

For a method $M$ applied to a resource $R$:

$$M(R) = M(M(R)) = M(M(M(R)))$$

Whereas for non-idempotent methods:

$$M(R) \neq M(M(R)) \quad \text{(in general)}$$

A `POST /api/v1/sensors` call creates a *new* sensor with a unique ID each time, hence is **non-idempotent**.

---

## 2.3 URI / URL Design вҖ” Resource Modeling

A **Uniform Resource Identifier (URI)** uniquely identifies a resource. The structure follows:

$$\text{URI} = \text{scheme} : \text{authority} \ \text{/} \ \text{path} \ [ \text{?} \text{query} ] \ [ \ \# \text{fragment} ]$$

**Example for IIoT:**

```
https://api.factory.com/v1/sensors/sensor-001/readings?from=2025-01-01&limit=100
```

| Component | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Scheme | `https` | Secure HTTP |
| Authority | `api.factory.com` | Domain of API gateway |
| Path | `/v1/sensors/sensor-001/readings` | Resource hierarchy |
| Query | `?from=2025-01-01&limit=100` | Filters & pagination |
| Fragment | (none here) | Client-side anchor |

### Resource Modeling Rules (KTU-High-Yield)

- вң… Use **plural nouns** for collections: `/sensors`, `/devices`, `/actuators`
- вң… Use **hierarchical paths** for relationships: `/sensors/{id}/readings`
- вң… Use **kebab-case** for multi-word resources: `/motor-controllers`
- вқҢ **Never** use verbs in URIs: `/getSensorData` вқҢ
- вқҢ **Never** expose database internals: `/table_sensors/row_42` вқҢ
- вң… **Version the API** in the URL: `/v1/`, `/v2/`

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all key metrics, equations, and design rules for Web API design as expected in KTU 2024 exams.

| **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| HTTP Request line | `Method SP Request-URI SP HTTP-Version CRLF` | RFC 7230 |
| Standard Port (HTTP) | **Port 80** | TCP |
| Standard Port (HTTPS) | **Port 443** | TCP (TLS encrypted) |
| Idempotent Methods | $\{ \text{GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE} \}$ | Safe-to-retry |
| Safe Methods (no side effect) | $\{ \text{GET, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE} \}$ | Read-only |
| Total Status Codes | $\mathbf{1xx \cup 2xx \cup 3xx \cup 4xx \cup 5xx}$ | 5 classes |
| 2xx Range | $200 \leq c \leq 299$ | Success |
| 4xx Range | $400 \leq c \leq 499$ | Client error |
| 5xx Range | $500 \leq c \leq 599$ | Server error |
| Throughput (API) | $\text{RPS} = \dfrac{\text{Total Requests}}{\text{Time Window (s)}}$ | Requests Per Second |
| Latency Budget | $L_{total} = L_{network} + L_{server} + L_{queue}$ | milliseconds |
| Rate Limit (Token Bucket) | $\text{Allowed} = \min(\text{burst}, \text{tokens})$ where tokens refill at rate $r$ | tokens/sec |
| Availability (SLA) | $A = \dfrac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |
| Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Operational Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | hours |
| JSON Payload Size | $\vert P_{json} \vert = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{len}(k_i) + \text{len}(v_i) + \text{metadata}$ | bytes |

> **Critical Note on Pipes**: All absolute-value / magnitude notations use `\vert` style to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Web APIs are the **backbone of Industry 4.0 ecosystems** for:

- рҹ”§ **Predictive Maintenance** вҖ” `GET /machines/{id}/vibration-data` streams sensor data to ML models
- рҹ“Ҡ **Digital Twins** вҖ” APIs synchronize physical asset state with virtual replicas
- рҹҸӯ **MES Integration** вҖ” Manufacturing Execution Systems consume API events for order tracking
- рҹҡҡ **Supply Chain Visibility** вҖ” APIs enable ERP-to-WMS-to-IoT platform communication
- рҹ”җ **Secure Device Management** вҖ” APIs handle over-the-air (OTA) firmware updates with auth tokens

---

## 2.6 Status Codes вҖ” Quick Reference

| Code | Name | When to Use (IIoT Context) |
|---|---|---|
| **200 OK** | Success | Reading sensor data successfully |
| **201 Created** | Resource created | New sensor registered |
| **204 No Content** | Success, no body | Sensor deleted, no payload |
| **301 Moved Permanently** | Endpoint moved | API version migration |
| **400 Bad Request** | Malformed request | Invalid JSON body sent |
| **401 Unauthorized** | Auth missing/failed | Invalid API key |
| **403 Forbidden** | Auth valid but no permission | Writing to read-only sensor |
| **404 Not Found** | Resource missing | Sensor ID doesn't exist |
| **429 Too Many Requests** | Rate limited | Exceeded IIoT gateway quota |
| **500 Internal Server Error** | Generic server failure | Database connection lost |
| **503 Service Unavailable** | Server overloaded / maintenance | Gateway temporarily down |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Designing an IIoT Web API вҖ” End-to-End Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> While Web API design is not a *mathematical derivation* in the classical sense, it follows a rigorous **step-by-step engineering methodology** which KTU examiners award marks for. Below is the analytical matrix in derivation form.

### Step 1 вҖ” Identify Domain Entities (Resources)

In an IIoT factory, extract the **nouns** from the requirement specification:

- **Nouns identified**:
  - Sensor (e.g., temperature, pressure, vibration)
  - Actuator (e.g., motor, valve, relay)
  - Machine / Asset
  - Reading / Measurement
  - Alert / Event
  - Gateway

### Step 2 вҖ” Define Resource Relationships (Cardinality)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{One Machine} &\longleftrightarrow \text{Many Sensors} \quad (1:N) \\
\text{One Sensor} &\longleftrightarrow \text{Many Readings} \quad (1:N) \\
\text{One Sensor} &\longleftrightarrow \text{Zero or Many Alerts} \quad (1:0..N) \\
\text{One Gateway} &\longleftrightarrow \text{Many Sensors} \quad (1:N)
\end{aligned}
$$

This means: For every machine, there can be multiple sensors. For every sensor, there can be many historical readings. This cardinality directly drives URI design.

### Step 3 вҖ” Map Resources to URI Endpoints

Using the relationship analysis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Collection URI} &\rightarrow \texttt{/v1/machines} \\
\text{Single Resource} &\rightarrow \texttt{/v1/machines/\{machineId\}} \\
\text{Nested Collection} &\rightarrow \texttt{/v1/machines/\{machineId\}/sensors} \\
\text{Deeply Nested} &\rightarrow \texttt{/v1/sensors/\{sensorId\}/readings} \\
\text{Action Endpoint} &\rightarrow \texttt{/v1/sensors/\{sensorId\}/calibrate} \quad \text{(POST only)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Students often nest too deeply (e.g., `/machines/{id}/sensors/{id}/readings/{id}/analysis/{id}`). The rule is **max 2-3 levels of nesting**. Beyond that, use **query parameters** or **separate top-level resources** like `/readings?sensorId=...`.

### Step 4 вҖ” Assign HTTP Methods (CRUD Mapping)

The **CRUD-to-HTTP mapping** is a **direct derivation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Read (C-)} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{GET} \\
\text{R-} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{GET} \\
\text{U-} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{PUT (full)} \ \text{or} \ \text{PATCH (partial)} \\
\text{D-} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{DELETE}
\end{aligned}
$$

The missing "C" (Create) is **POST** since creation is not idempotent.

> [!NOTE]
> Although `PUT` *can* be used for creation when the client knows the ID upfront (idempotent), `POST /collection` is the dominant pattern for new resource creation.

### Step 5 вҖ” Design Request/Response Schemas

**Request example (POST to create a sensor):**

```json
{
  "sensorId": "T-001",
  "type": "temperature",
  "unit": "celsius",
  "location": "Boiler-3",
  "samplingRate": 5
}
```

**Response example (201 Created):**

```json
{
  "id": "5f8a7b2c-9d1e-4a3b-b6c7-2e8f9a0b1c2d",
  "sensorId": "T-001",
  "type": "temperature",
  "unit": "celsius",
  "location": "Boiler-3",
  "samplingRate": 5,
  "status": "active",
  "createdAt": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z",
  "_links": {
    "self": "/v1/sensors/5f8a7b2c-9d1e-4a3b-b6c7-2e8f9a0b1c2d",
    "readings": "/v1/sensors/5f8a7b2c-9d1e-4a3b-b6c7-2e8f9a0b1c2d/readings",
    "alerts": "/v1/sensors/5f8a7b2c-9d1e-4a3b-b6c7-2e8f9a0b1c2d/alerts"
  }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `_links` object implements **HATEOAS** вҖ” the API response itself tells the client what related actions are possible. This is a *direct* REST constraint derived by Fielding.

### Step 6 вҖ” Design Error Response Schema

A consistent error structure is a **mandatory** design element:

```json
{
  "error": {
    "code": "SENSOR_NOT_FOUND",
    "message": "The requested sensor does not exist.",
    "details": {
      "sensorId": "T-999",
      "timestamp": "2025-01-15T10:35:22Z",
      "traceId": "abc-123-xyz"
    },
    "_links": {
      "documentation": "/docs/errors/SENSOR_NOT_FOUND"
    }
  }
}
```

### Step 7 вҖ” Authentication & Authorization Design

For IIoT, choose the appropriate mechanism based on deployment:

| Method | Use Case | Complexity | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **API Key** | Simple device-to-cloud | Low | High |
| **OAuth 2.0** | Third-party app access | High | High |
| **JWT (JSON Web Token)** | Stateless session token | Medium | High |
| **mTLS** | High-security device auth | High | Medium |
| **Basic Auth** | Internal testing only | Low | Low |

> [!WARNING]
> **Basic Auth over plain HTTP is NEVER acceptable in production IIoT.** Always use **HTTPS + JWT/OAuth 2.0 + mTLS** for industrial systems.

### Step 8 вҖ” Rate Limiting & Throttling (Derivation)

The **Token Bucket algorithm** is the standard rate-limiting derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } r &= \text{refill rate (tokens per second)} \\
\text{Let } B &= \text{burst capacity (max tokens)} \\
\text{Let } t_{\text{last}} &= \text{timestamp of last refill} \\
\\
\text{At time } t, \quad \text{tokens available} &= \min\left(B, \text{tokens}_{\text{prev}} + r \cdot (t - t_{\text{last}})\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

If `tokens < 1`, the request is **rejected with HTTP 429 Too Many Requests**.

### Step 9 вҖ” Versioning Strategy

Three accepted approaches, in order of KTU preference:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{URI Versioning} &\rightarrow \texttt{/api/v1/sensors} \quad \text{(most common)} \\
\text{Header Versioning} &\rightarrow \texttt{Accept: application/vnd.factoryapi.v2+json} \\
\text{Query Parameter} &\rightarrow \texttt{/api/sensors?version=2} \quad \text{(least recommended)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 10 вҖ” Documentation Specification (OpenAPI / Swagger)

Use **OpenAPI 3.0** specification for machine-readable API contracts. The OpenAPI document is the **single source of truth** for:

- Endpoint paths and methods
- Request/response schemas
- Authentication mechanisms
- Status codes and error formats
- Example payloads

This document is derived *after* completing Steps 1-9.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Design Checklist Matrix

| # | Design Decision | Recommendation | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transport Protocol | HTTPS (TLS 1.3) | Encryption mandatory in Industry 4.0 |
| 2 | Data Format | JSON (primary), XML (legacy) | JSON is lighter, IIoT-friendly |
| 3 | Resource Naming | Plural nouns, kebab-case | REST convention, readability |
| 4 | Maximum Nesting | вүӨ 3 levels | Avoids URI explosion |
| 5 | Authentication | OAuth 2.0 + JWT | Industry standard |
| 6 | Versioning | URI-based (`/v1/`) | Clear and cacheable |
| 7 | Pagination | Cursor-based or page+limit | Scales for large sensor datasets |
| 8 | Error Format | RFC 7807 (Problem Details) | Standardized errors |
| 9 | Documentation | OpenAPI 3.0 | Industry standard, KTU-recommended |
| 10 | Rate Limiting | Token bucket per API key | Protects gateway from overload |

---

## 3.3 Performance & SLA Derivations

### Availability Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{monthly}} &= \dfrac{\text{Uptime}_{\text{hours}}}{\text{Uptime}_{\text{hours}} + \text{Downtime}_{\text{hours}}} \times 100\% \\
\\
\text{If SLA} &= 99.9\% \ (\text{"three nines"}) \\
\text{Downtime allowed} &= (1 - 0.999) \times 30 \times 24 = 0.72 \text{ hours/month} = 43.2 \text{ minutes/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Latency Budget for IIoT Pipeline

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{total}} &= L_{\text{sensor}} + L_{\text{gateway}} + L_{\text{network}} + L_{\text{cloud}} + L_{\text{client}} \\
\\
\text{Typical budget:} \quad L_{\text{total}} &\leq 500 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(real-time monitoring)} \\
L_{\text{total}} &\leq 5 \text{ s} \quad \text{(batch analytics acceptable)}
\end{aligned}
$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## рҹ“қ PART A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024]**
**Define a RESTful Web API. List any FOUR HTTP methods used in REST API design with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **RESTful Web API** is an application programming interface that conforms to the architectural constraints of **REpresentational State Transfer (REST)** as defined by Roy Fielding in his 2000 doctoral dissertation. It uses HTTP as the transport protocol, identifies resources via URIs, and operates statelessly, returning representations (typically JSON) of resources in response to client requests.

**Four HTTP methods with examples:**

1. **GET** вҖ” `GET /v1/sensors/sensor-001` вҶ’ Retrieves the details of sensor with ID `sensor-001`.
2. **POST** вҖ” `POST /v1/sensors` вҶ’ Creates a new sensor record in the system.
3. **PUT** вҖ” `PUT /v1/sensors/sensor-001` вҶ’ Replaces the entire configuration of sensor `sensor-001`.
4. **DELETE** вҖ” `DELETE /v1/sensors/sensor-001` вҶ’ Removes sensor `sensor-001` from the registry.

> [!TIP]
> Mentioning the **idempotency** property of these methods (GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent; POST is not) earns extra marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023]**
**Explain the concept of HATEOAS in the context of RESTful Web APIs. How does it benefit API consumers?**

**Model Answer:**

**HATEOAS (Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State)** is a constraint of the REST architectural style. In a HATEOAS-compliant API, the server returns not just data in its response, but also **hypermedia links** that tell the client what related actions it can take next.

**Example response:**

```json
{
  "sensorId": "T-001",
  "temperature": 78.5,
  "_links": {
    "self": "/v1/sensors/T-001",
    "readings": "/v1/sensors/T-001/readings",
    "alerts": "/v1/sensors/T-001/alerts",
    "calibrate": "/v1/sensors/T-001/calibrate"
  }
}
```

**Benefits to API consumers:**

1. **Discoverability** вҖ” Clients can dynamically explore available actions without hardcoding URIs.
2. **Decoupling** вҖ” Server can change URI structures without breaking clients.
3. **Self-documenting** вҖ” The API teaches the client how to use it through responses.
4. **Stateful interactions over stateless protocol** вҖ” The client "navigates" through resource states via links.

---

## рҹ“қ PART B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the **six architectural constraints** of REST as defined by Roy Fielding. Explain how each constraint contributes to scalable and reliable Web API design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **RESTful Web API** for an **IIoT-based Smart Factory** that manages machines, sensors, and their telemetry data. Provide the **complete resource model**, **URI design** with HTTP methods, **JSON request/response schemas** for at least two endpoints, and explain the **authentication mechanism** you would recommend. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution вҖ” Question A**

#### Part (a) вҖ” Six REST Constraints

The REST architectural style, formalized by **Roy Fielding** in his 2000 dissertation, defines six constraints. A Web API that satisfies all six is considered "truly RESTful."

**1. ClientвҖ“Server Constraint**

The client (UI layer) and the server (data storage layer) are **decoupled**. They evolve independently. In an IIoT context, a mobile dashboard (client) and a sensor data API (server) can be updated separately.

**Why it matters:** Improves **portability** of client UI and **scalability** of server-side data.

**2. Statelessness**

Each request from the client must contain **all the information** the server needs to process it. The server does not store any client session context between requests.

$$\text{Server State}_n = f(\text{Request}_n) \quad \text{only, no dependence on } \text{Request}_{n-1}, \text{Request}_{n-2}, \ldots$$

**Why it matters:** Enhances **reliability** (no session loss on server crash), **scalability** (any server can handle any request), and **visibility** (each request is self-contained for logging).

**3. Cacheability**

Responses must explicitly indicate whether they are **cacheable** or **non-cacheable** using HTTP headers like `Cache-Control`, `ETag`, and `Expires`.

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Cache-Control: max-age=60
ETag: "abc123"
```

**Why it matters:** Reduces **latency** and **network bandwidth** вҖ” critical for IIoT gateways handling thousands of sensor readings.

**4. Uniform Interface**

This is the **central feature** of REST, comprising four sub-constraints:

- **Identification of resources** вҖ” Each resource has a unique URI.
- **Manipulation through representations** вҖ” Client interacts with representations (JSON), not the resource itself.
- **Self-descriptive messages** вҖ” Each message contains enough info (media type, status) for processing.
- **HATEOAS** вҖ” Hypermedia links guide the client's next actions.

**5. Layered System**

The architecture is composed of hierarchical layers (client вҶ’ load balancer вҶ’ API gateway вҶ’ service вҶ’ database). The client cannot tell whether it is communicating with the end server or an intermediary.

**Why it matters:** Enables **load balancing**, **caching proxies**, **security firewalls**, and **legacy system encapsulation**.

**6. Code-On-Demand (Optional)**

Servers can temporarily extend client functionality by transferring executable code (e.g., JavaScript applets, scripts). This is the **only optional** constraint and is rarely used in modern IIoT APIs.

---

#### Part (b) вҖ” IIoT Smart Factory Web API Design

**Resource Model:**

| Resource | URI Template | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Machine | `/v1/machines` | Production machine in factory |
| Single Machine | `/v1/machines/{machineId}` | Specific machine instance |
| Sensor | `/v1/machines/{machineId}/sensors` | Sensors attached to a machine |
| Reading | `/v1/sensors/{sensorId}/readings` | Telemetry data from a sensor |
| Alert | `/v1/sensors/{sensorId}/alerts` | Alert rules for a sensor |

**URI Design with HTTP Methods:**

| Method | URI | Action |
|---|---|---|
| `GET` | `/v1/machines` | List all machines |
| `GET` | `/v1/machines/{machineId}` | Get one machine |
| `POST` | `/v1/machines` | Register a new machine |
| `PUT` | `/v1/machines/{machineId}` | Update machine details |
| `DELETE` | `/v1/machines/{machineId}` | Decommission a machine |
| `GET` | `/v1/machines/{machineId}/sensors` | List sensors of a machine |
| `POST` | `/v1/machines/{machineId}/sensors` | Add a new sensor |
| `GET` | `/v1/sensors/{sensorId}/readings` | Fetch sensor readings |
| `POST` | `/v1/sensors/{sensorId}/alerts` | Create alert rule |

**JSON Request Schema вҖ” Register a New Machine (POST /v1/machines):**

```json
{
  "machineId": "M-001",
  "name": "CNC Lathe A1",
  "type": "CNC",
  "location": "Shop-Floor-2",
  "manufacturer": "Siemens",
  "commissionDate": "2024-12-01"
}
```

**JSON Response Schema вҖ” Successful Creation (201 Created):**

```json
{
  "id": "7a3b9c2e-1f4d-4a8b-9e6c-5d2a8f7b3c1e",
  "machineId": "M-001",
  "name": "CNC Lathe A1",
  "type": "CNC",
  "location": "Shop-Floor-2",
  "manufacturer": "Siemens",
  "commissionDate": "2024-12-01",
  "status": "operational",
  "_links": {
    "self": "/v1/machines/M-001",
    "sensors": "/v1/machines/M-001/sensors",
    "maintenance": "/v1/machines/M-001/maintenance"
  }
}
```

**Authentication Recommendation:**

For an IIoT Smart Factory, I recommend **OAuth 2.0 with JWT (JSON Web Tokens)** combined with **mTLS for device-to-gateway communication**.

**Rationale:**

- **OAuth 2.0** provides delegated, scoped access вҖ” operators get `read` scope, admins get `write` scope.
- **JWT** is stateless and self-contained, reducing server load (aligned with REST statelessness constraint).
- **mTLS** ensures both client (device) and server (gateway) authenticate each other with X.509 certificates, preventing rogue device injection.
- All communications over **HTTPS (TLS 1.3)** on **port 443**.

**Token Example:**

```
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...
```

**Scope-based authorization:**

```json
{
  "sub": "operator-john",
  "scope": "sensors:read machines:read",
  "exp": 1736899200,
  "iss": "https://auth.factory.com"
}
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare **REST, SOAP, and GraphQL** API architectures in detail. Discuss their **advantages, disadvantages, and ideal use cases** in an Industry 4.0 environment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **principles of good URI design** for RESTful Web APIs. Design a **paginated, filtered, and versioned** API endpoint for retrieving historical sensor readings from an IIoT system. Provide the **request structure, response structure, and error handling mechanism** with proper HTTP status codes. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution вҖ” Question B**

#### Part (a) вҖ” Comparison of REST, SOAP, and GraphQL

| Feature | REST | SOAP | GraphQL |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Representational State Transfer | Simple Object Access Protocol | Graph Query Language |
| **Data Format** | JSON, XML, YAML, plain text | XML only | JSON only |
| **Transport** | HTTP/HTTPS | HTTP, SMTP, TCP, JMS | HTTP/HTTPS |
| **State** | Stateless | Stateless | Stateless |
| **Standard** | Architectural style (Fielding) | W3C protocol | Facebook (open spec) |
| **Contract** | OpenAPI (optional) | WSDL (mandatory) | SDL Schema |
| **Error Handling** | HTTP status codes | SOAP Fault element | GraphQL errors array |
| **Caching** | Native HTTP caching | Complex | Difficult |
| **Learning Curve** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Tooling** | Excellent (Swagger, Postman) | Mature | Good (Apollo, Relay) |

**REST вҖ” Advantages:**

- Simple, leverages existing HTTP semantics
- Lightweight (JSON is terse)
- Easy to cache, scales horizontally
- Widely understood in industry

**REST вҖ” Disadvantages:**

- Over-fetching (returns more data than needed)
- Under-fetching (requires multiple round-trips for related data)
- No formal contract (mitigated by OpenAPI)

**SOAP вҖ” Advantages:**

- Strong typing via WSDL and XSD
- Built-in error handling (SOAP Fault)
- WS-Security standard for enterprise-grade security
- Protocol-independent (works over SMTP, TCP)

**SOAP вҖ” Disadvantages:**

- Verbose XML increases payload size
- Slower parsing, higher bandwidth
- Steep learning curve
- Not cacheable natively

**GraphQL вҖ” Advantages:**

- Client specifies **exactly** what fields it needs (no over/under-fetching)
- Single endpoint for all queries
- Strongly typed schema

**GraphQL вҖ” Disadvantages:**

- Complex caching (no URL-based caching)
- File uploads are awkward
- Query depth/complexity attacks possible
- Harder to monitor and rate-limit

**Ideal Use Cases in Industry 4.0:**

| Architecture | Best Suited For |
|---|---|
| **REST** | Public APIs, mobile apps, simple CRUD over sensor data |
| **SOAP** | Legacy enterprise integration, banking, high-security |
| **GraphQL** | Complex dashboards needing aggregated data from many sensors |

---

#### Part (b) вҖ” URI Design Principles & Paginated API

**Principles of Good URI Design:**

1. **Use nouns, not verbs** вҖ” `/sensors` вң… vs `/getSensors` вқҢ
2. **Use plural nouns for collections** вҖ” `/sensors` вң… vs `/sensor` вқҢ
3. **Use hierarchical paths for relationships** вҖ” `/machines/{id}/sensors`
4. **Use kebab-case for multi-word resources** вҖ” `/motor-controllers`
5. **Keep nesting shallow** вҖ” Maximum 2вҖ“3 levels
6. **Version the API in the URI** вҖ” `/v1/`, `/v2/`
7. **Use query parameters for filtering, not path** вҖ” `?type=temperature`
8. **Avoid exposing implementation details** вҖ” Don't use `/db_table_sensors/row_5`

**Designed API Endpoint:**

**Endpoint:** `GET /v1/sensors/{sensorId}/readings`

**Query Parameters for Filtering & Pagination:**

| Parameter | Type | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `from` | ISO 8601 datetime | Start of time range | `2025-01-01T00:00:00Z` |
| `to` | ISO 8601 datetime | End of time range | `2025-01-31T23:59:59Z` |
| `limit` | Integer | Max records per page | `50` |
| `cursor` | Opaque string | Pagination cursor | `eyJpZCI6MTAwfQ==` |
| `sort` | String | Sort order | `timestamp:desc` |
| `aggregation` | String | Time-bucketing | `avg`, `min`, `max`, `sum` |

**Full Request URL Example:**

```
GET /v1/sensors/sensor-001/readings?from=2025-01-01T00:00:00Z&to=2025-01-31T23:59:59Z&limit=50&cursor=eyJpZCI6MTAwfQ==&aggregation=avg
```

**Successful Response (200 OK):**

```json
{
  "data": [
    {
      "timestamp": "2025-01-15T10:00:00Z",
      "value": 75.4,
      "unit": "celsius",
      "quality": "good"
    },
    {
      "timestamp": "2025-01-15T10:05:00Z",
      "value": 76.1,
      "unit": "celsius",
      "quality": "good"
    }
  ],
  "pagination": {
    "limit": 50,
    "returned": 2,
    "nextCursor": "eyJpZCI6MTAyfQ==",
    "hasMore": true
  },
  "_links": {
    "self": "/v1/sensors/sensor-001/readings?from=2025-01-01T00:00:00Z&limit=50&cursor=eyJpZCI6MTAwfQ==",
    "next": "/v1/sensors/sensor-001/readings?from=2025-01-01T00:00:00Z&limit=50&cursor=eyJpZCI6MTAyfQ==",
    "sensor": "/v1/sensors/sensor-001"
  }
}
```

**Error Handling with HTTP Status Codes:**

| Status Code | When Returned | Response Body |
|---|---|---|
| **400 Bad Request** | Invalid `from` date format | `{ "error": { "code": "INVALID_DATE_FORMAT", "message": "..." } }` |
| **401 Unauthorized** | Missing/expired JWT token | `{ "error": { "code": "AUTH_REQUIRED", "message": "..." } }` |
| **403 Forbidden** | User lacks read permission for this sensor | `{ "error": { "code": "FORBIDDEN", "message": "..." } }` |
| **404 Not Found** | Sensor ID does not exist | `{ "error": { "code": "SENSOR_NOT_FOUND", "message": "..." } }` |
| **429 Too Many Requests** | Rate limit exceeded | `{ "error": { "code": "RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED", "retryAfter": 60 } }` |
| **500 Internal Server Error** | Database connection lost | `{ "error": { "code": "INTERNAL_ERROR", "traceId": "..." } }` |
| **503 Service Unavailable** | Gateway maintenance window | `{ "error": { "code": "SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE" } }` |

**Error Response Body Example (404):**

```json
{
  "error": {
    "code": "SENSOR_NOT_FOUND",
    "message": "The sensor with ID 'sensor-999' does not exist or has been decommissioned.",
    "details": {
      "sensorId": "sensor-999",
      "timestamp": "2025-01-15T10:35:22Z",
      "traceId": "abc-123-xyz-789"
    },
    "_links": {
      "documentation": "https://docs.factory.com/errors/SENSOR_NOT_FOUND",
      "support": "https://support.factory.com/tickets/new"
    }
  }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to version the API** вҖ” `/sensors` without `/v1/` prefix loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Using verbs in URIs** вҖ” `/getSensorReadings` is wrong; use nouns.
> 3. **Mixing up PUT and PATCH** вҖ” PUT replaces *entire* resource; PATCH modifies *partial* fields.
> 4. **Forgetting HATEOAS `_links`** вҖ” Modern REST responses should include hypermedia links.
> 5. **Returning wrong status codes** вҖ” `200 OK` for a creation is wrong; use `201 Created`.
> 6. **Not using `Accept` header for content negotiation** вҖ” Always specify `application/json`.
> 7. **Deep nesting beyond 3 levels** вҖ” Causes URI explosion; use query parameters instead.
> 8. **Skipping error response schema design** вҖ” A consistent error format is mandatory for KTU full marks.

---

## рҹ”Ғ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

### рҹ”‘ Core Definitions
- **Web API**: Programmatic interface for M2M communication over HTTP/HTTPS.
- **REST**: Architectural style defined by Fielding (6 constraints).
- **RESTful API**: API that satisfies REST constraints.
- **HATEOAS**: Hypermedia links drive client navigation.

### рҹӣ пёҸ HTTP Methods (Memorize the Table)
- **GET** = Read, idempotent, safe
- **POST** = Create, non-idempotent
- **PUT** = Full update, idempotent
- **PATCH** = Partial update, generally non-idempotent
- **DELETE** = Remove, idempotent

### рҹ“җ URI Design Rules
- вң… Plural nouns (`/sensors`)
- вң… Hierarchical paths (`/machines/{id}/sensors`)
- вң… Kebab-case (`/motor-controllers`)
- вң… Versioning (`/v1/`)
- вқҢ No verbs in URIs
- вқҢ No deep nesting (вүӨ 3 levels)

### рҹ“Ҡ HTTP Status Code Classes
- **1xx** вҖ” Informational
- **2xx** вҖ” Success (200, 201, 204)
- **3xx** вҖ” Redirection (301, 304)
- **4xx** вҖ” Client error (400, 401, 403, 404, 429)
- **5xx** вҖ” Server error (500, 503)

### рҹ”җ Authentication Options
- **API Key** вҖ” Simple, for devices
- **OAuth 2.0 + JWT** вҖ” Multi-tenant, for apps
- **mTLS** вҖ” High security, for industrial

### рҹҸӯ IIoT-Specific Considerations
- Always use **HTTPS** (never plain HTTP in production)
- Use **cursor-based pagination** for large telemetry datasets
- Implement **rate limiting** via token bucket
- Document via **OpenAPI 3.0**
- Apply **HATEOAS** for self-discoverable APIs
- Use **semantic versioning** (`/v1/`, `/v2/`)
- Apply **content negotiation** (`Accept: application/json`)

### рҹ§® Key Formulas to Remember
$$\text{RPS} = \frac{\text{Total Requests}}{\text{Time Window (s)}}$$

$$L_{total} = L_{network} + L_{server} + L_{queue}$$

$$A = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Token bucket: } \text{Allowed} = \min(B, \text{tokens}_{prev} + r \cdot \Delta t)$$

### вҡ пёҸ Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid
1. Confusing idempotent vs safe methods.
2. Forgetting to include `Content-Type` and `Accept` headers.
3. Designing non-RESTful APIs with verbs in URIs.
4. Not providing error response schemas.
5. Omitting versioning strategy.
6. Using SOAP-style envelopes in REST APIs.
7. Not explaining HATEOAS when discussing REST.

### рҹҺҜ High-Yield Topics for KTU 2024
- REST 6 constraints (especially **statelessness**, **uniform interface**, **HATEOAS**)
- HTTP method semantics + idempotency
- URI design best practices
- Status code selection
- JSON vs XML trade-offs
- Authentication: OAuth 2.0 + JWT workflow
- OpenAPI specification role
- Web API in IIoT context (sensor/actuator/machine management)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEIT525 - INDUSTRY 4.0 AND INDUSTRIAL INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Web Services # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Web Services (Industry 4.0 Definition)** > A **Web Service** is a standardized, interoperable, machine-to-machine (M2M) communication paradigm that uses a standardized XML/JSON-based messaging protocol over Internet-based networking infrastructure to enable the exchange of data, information, and computational logic between heterogeneous industrial devices, enterprise applications, and cloud platforms. In the context of **Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)**, web services are the **digital glue** that connects: - Field-level sensors and actuators (Edge Layer) - Plant-level SCADA/MES systems (Operations Layer) - Enterprise-level ERP and cloud analytics (Enterprise/Cloud Layer) They are designed following the principles of **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)**, where every industrial capability (e.g., "ReadTemperature", "OpenValve", "GetProductionReport") is exposed as an independent, network-callable function. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy: The Industrial Restaurant Waiter** > > Imagine a smart factory where machines (machines, robots, ERP software) all speak **different languages** (Siemens S7, Modbus, Java, Python, SAP). They cannot directly talk to each other. > > A **Web Service acts like a universal waiter** in a restaurant: > 1. The **Customer** (Client Application) writes down their order in a **standard menu format** (XML/JSON request). > 2. The **Waiter** (Web Service) carries the order to the **Kitchen** (Server/Industrial System). > 3. The Kitchen prepares the food and hands it back to the Waiter in a **standard plate** (XML/JSON response). > 4. The Waiter delivers the response to the customer. > > Both the customer and the kitchen speak different internal languages, but the **waiter enforces a common protocol** (SOAP or REST) so communication succeeds. ## 1.3 The Three Foundational Pillars of Web Services For any web service to exist in the IIoT stack, three standardized building blocks are mandatory: | Pillar | Full Form | Role in IIoT | |---|---|---| | **WSDL** | Web Services Description Language | The "service menu" - formally describes what the service does, what inputs it needs, and what outputs it returns. Written in **XML**. | | **SOAP** | Simple Object Access Protocol | The "messenger protocol" - rules for formatting and transporting the message. | | **UDDI** | Universal Description, Discovery and Integration | The "service phonebook" - a directory where services are registered and discovered. | ## 1.4 Standards & Metrics - **Default Transport Protocol:** HTTP/HTTPS on **port 80/443** - **Default Messaging Format:** XML (for SOAP) or JSON (for REST) - **W3C Standardization Body:** World Wide Web Consortium (W3C) - **OASIS Standardization Body:** Organization for the Advancement of Structured Information Standards - **Typical IIoT Latency Tolerance:** **100 ms вҖ“ 1 second** (depending on the layer) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 Focus):** > In KTU 2024 Scheme, "Web Services" within Industry 4.0 is taught as the **integration backbone** of CPS (Cyber-Physical Systems). You must understand how SOAP vs REST differ, the role of WSDL, and how web services enable **cloud-edge interoperability**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Layering of a Web Service Call Every web service interaction follows a strict **layered stack model**, similar to the OSI model: ### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown: 1. **Discovery Layer (UDDI):** Client looks up "where is the temperature service?" вҖ” analogous to Googling a phone number. 2. **Description Layer (WSDL):** Client downloads the "service contract" вҖ” what arguments to pass, what response to expect. 3. **Packaging Layer (SOAP Envelope / REST URI):** The request is wrapped in a standardized message envelope. 4. **Transport Layer (HTTP/HTTPS):** The message travels over the industrial network (often using MQTT-over-HTTP or CoAP bridges in real IIoT). 5. **Processing Layer (Server-side Application):** The industrial system (e.g., a PLC gateway) executes the business logic. 6. **Response Layer:** A structured XML/JSON payload is returned to the client. 7. **Application Integration:** The client application (e.g., a dashboard) updates its UI or triggers the next action. ## 2.2 Two Major Web Service Paradigms ### A) SOAP-Based Web Services (Big Web Services) > [!NOTE] > **SOAP = Simple Object Access Protocol** > A heavyweight, strictly standardized, **XML-only** protocol. The "official W3C-certified" web service. - **Message Format:** XML envelope with mandatory **Header** and **Body** sections. - **Transport:** Primarily HTTP, but can also use SMTP, TCP, JMS. - **Service Definition:** Uses **WSDL** (mandatory). - **Discovery:** Uses **UDDI** (mandatory in classical stack). - **Statefulness:** Supports both stateless and stateful operations via WS-* extensions. - **Best For:** Mission-critical industrial systems, banking, high-security enterprise integration. ### B) RESTful Web Services (Lightweight) > [!IMPORTANT] > **REST = Representational State Transfer** > An architectural style (not a protocol) introduced by **Roy Fielding (2000)**. Lightweight, JSON-friendly, dominant in modern IIoT/Cloud. - **Message Format:** JSON, XML, YAML, plain text, HTML. - **Transport:** HTTP/HTTPS only. - **Service Definition:** **Optional WSDL** (typically WADL or OpenAPI/Swagger). - **Discovery:** No formal UDDI; uses API gateways (e.g., AWS API Gateway, Azure API Management). - **Statefulness:** Strictly **stateless** (each request is independent). - **Best For:** IIoT cloud dashboards, mobile apps, microservices. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Definition / Rule | IIoT Context | |---|---|---| | **HTTP Verbs (REST)** | `GET` (read), `POST` (create), `PUT` (update), `DELETE` (remove) | Sensor data fetch uses `GET`; alarm trigger uses `POST` | | **HTTP Status Codes** | `2xx` success, `3xx` redirect, `4xx` client error, `5xx` server error | `200 OK`, `404 Not Found`, `500 Internal Server Error` | | **SOAP Message Structure** | ` >
