### PART B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question has **sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full (no mixing). Both choices are written below for practice.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024, Model Paper 2]`

#### Part (a) вҖ” 7 Marks
**Explain the three cloud service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) with neat diagrams showing the responsibility shared between the user and the provider. Mention two real-world examples for each.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Introduction (1 Mark):** Cloud service models define the **layer of abstraction** at which a user consumes cloud resources. As the user moves "up the stack" from IaaS to SaaS, the **provider's responsibility increases** and the user's operational burden decreases.

**IaaS (2 Marks):**
- **Definition:** Infrastructure-as-a-Service provides raw virtualized computing resources вҖ” VMs, storage volumes, virtual networks, and load balancers вҖ” over the internet.
- **User manages:** Operating system, middleware, runtime, application, and data.
- **Provider manages:** Virtualization, physical servers, storage hardware, and networking infrastructure.
- **Examples:** **Amazon EC2**, **Microsoft Azure Virtual Machines**, Google Compute Engine, DigitalOcean Droplets.
- **Industry 4.0 Use Case:** A factory hosts a custom containerized **MES application** on EC2 instances, scaling out during shift hours and scaling in at night.

**PaaS (2 Marks):**
- **Definition:** Platform-as-a-Service provides a ready-to-code environment. The user writes application code and uploads it; the platform handles OS, runtime, scaling, and patching automatically.
- **User manages:** Application code and data only.
- **Provider manages:** Runtime, middleware, OS, virtualization, servers, storage, networking.
- **Examples:** **Google App Engine**, **AWS Elastic Beanstalk**, Heroku, Red Hat OpenShift, Azure App Service.
- **Industry 4.0 Use Case:** A data scientist deploys a **LSTM-based predictive maintenance** model using Elastic Beanstalk without worrying about installing TensorFlow or patching the OS.

**SaaS (2 Marks):**
- **Definition:** Software-as-a-Service delivers a fully finished, ready-to-use application accessed through a web browser or thin client.
- **User manages:** Only user-specific data and configuration settings.
- **Provider manages:** The entire stack including the application itself.
- **Examples:** **Microsoft 365**, **Google Workspace**, Salesforce CRM, Slack, Dropbox, Siemens MindSphere.
- **Industry 4.0 Use Case:** Factory managers access a real-time **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) dashboard** through MindSphere without installing any software locally.

**Diagram (Mandatory for full marks):** Draw a stacked block diagram (refer Section 4.1) showing layers Application вҶ’ Data вҶ’ Runtime вҶ’ Middleware вҶ’ OS вҶ’ Virtualization вҶ’ Servers вҶ’ Storage вҶ’ Networking, and shade the layers managed by the provider differently for IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS.

---

#### Part (b) вҖ” 7 Marks
**Discuss the role of cloud computing in Industry 4.0 with reference to (i) predictive maintenance, (ii) digital twin, and (iii) supply-chain optimization.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Role of Cloud in Industry 4.0 вҖ” Overview (1 Mark):**
Industry 4.0 is a data-intensive paradigm. A single modern factory can generate **terabytes of telemetry daily** from sensors, PLCs, vision systems, and ERP integrations. Cloud computing provides the **elastic storage, scalable compute, and AI/ML services** needed to convert this raw data into actionable intelligence.

**(i) Predictive Maintenance (2 Marks):**
- **Sensors** (vibration, temperature, acoustic) on motors, pumps, and bearings stream data to the cloud via **MQTT/HTTPS**.
- The cloud ingests data into a **time-series database** (e.g., AWS Timestream, InfluxDB Cloud).
- A **machine-learning model** (e.g., LSTM, Random Forest, XGBoost) is trained on historical failure data to predict **Remaining Useful Life (RUL)** of equipment.
- When RUL drops below a threshold, the cloud pushes an alert to the maintenance team's mobile app, scheduling service *before* failure occurs.
- **Outcome:** Unplanned downtime reduced by **30вҖ“50%**, maintenance costs reduced by **20вҖ“30%**.
- **Real-world example:** **Siemens MindSphere** + **AWS IoT Core** for predictive maintenance of gas turbines.

**(ii) Digital Twin (2 Marks):**
- A **Digital Twin** is a virtual replica of a physical asset, process, or system, updated in real time with sensor data.
- The cloud hosts the **3D simulation engine** (e.g., Unity, Unreal Engine, ANSYS Twin Builder) and the high-fidelity physics model.
- Real-time sensor data from the factory is **mirrored** into the cloud-based twin, which then runs "what-if" scenarios: *"What happens if I increase spindle speed by 10%?"* вҖ” without touching the real machine.
- The cloud's **GPU/TPU instances** enable **physics-based + data-driven** hybrid simulation, which is computationally infeasible on-premise for large factories.
- **Real-world example:** **GE Predix** platform hosting digital twins of jet engines and wind turbines.

**(iii) Supply-Chain Optimization (2 Marks):**
- Cloud platforms integrate **ERP (SAP S/4HANA)**, **MES**, and **IoT sensor data** into a unified data lake.
- **AI-driven demand forecasting** models (Prophet, ARIMA, deep neural networks) run in the cloud, predicting demand spikes based on weather, festivals, and social-media trends.
- The cloud dynamically adjusts **production schedules**, **inventory levels**, and **logistics routes** across geographically distributed plants.
- **Real-world example:** **BMW's cloud-based supply chain** that synchronizes 30+ global plants using AWS.

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion to write (Bonus Marks):** *"Cloud computing is the central nervous system of Industry 4.0 вҖ” it converts the data exhaust of the smart factory into strategic intelligence, enabling the four Industry 4.0 design principles: interconnection, information transparency, decentralized decisions, and technical assistance."*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) вҖ” 7 Marks
**Describe the four cloud deployment models (Public, Private, Hybrid, Community) with their advantages and disadvantages. Why is Hybrid Cloud the most adopted model in Industry 4.0?**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Public Cloud (1.5 Marks):**
- **Definition:** Cloud infrastructure provisioned for open use by the general public. Owned and operated by a third-party provider (AWS, Azure, GCP).
- **Advantages:** Lowest cost, no CapEx, unlimited scalability, pay-per-use.
- **Disadvantages:** Less control, data-sovereignty concerns, shared multi-tenant environment, internet-dependency.
- **Use Case:** Startups and SMEs running non-sensitive IIoT analytics.

**Private Cloud (1.5 Marks):**
- **Definition:** Cloud infrastructure provisioned for exclusive use by a single organization. May be on-premise or hosted by a third party.
- **Advantages:** Full control, enhanced security, compliance-friendly (HIPAA, GDPR), predictable performance.
- **Disadvantages:** High CapEx, limited scalability, requires dedicated IT staff.
- **Use Case:** Defense, pharmaceutical, and banking sectors with strict data-residency laws.

**Hybrid Cloud (1.5 Marks):**
- **Definition:** A composition of two or more distinct cloud infrastructures (private + public) bound by standardized or proprietary technology enabling data and application portability.
- **Advantages:** Best of both worlds вҖ” sensitive data stays private while bursty workloads run on public cloud. Enables **cloud bursting**.
- **Disadvantages:** Complex networking, requires robust orchestration (e.g., Kubernetes, Anthos, Azure Arc), higher integration cost.
- **Use Case:** **Industry 4.0** вҖ” factory-floor real-time control on private cloud; AI training on public cloud.

**Community Cloud (1 Mark):**
- **Definition:** Cloud infrastructure provisioned exclusively for a specific community of consumers with shared concerns (e.g., mission, security, compliance).
- **Advantages:** Cost-shared across community, meets industry-specific compliance.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited scalability, governance complexity.
- **Use Case:** A consortium of Kerala spice factories sharing a community cloud for traceability and GST compliance.

**Why Hybrid is Dominant in Industry 4.0 (1.5 Marks):**
- Real-time control loops (sub-millisecond) **cannot tolerate** public-cloud latency вҶ’ must stay on-premise.
- AI/ML training on terabytes of historical data **demands** elastic GPU instances вҶ’ economically infeasible on-premise.
- **Data sovereignty laws** (India's DPDP Act 2023) require sensitive production data to remain in-country.
- A **hybrid orchestration layer** (e.g., Azure Arc, AWS Outposts, Google Anthos) provides a unified control plane, masking the underlying complexity from operators.

---

#### Part (b) вҖ” 7 Marks
**A smart factory has 100 CNC machines, each emitting 5 KB of sensor data every 2 seconds, 24/7. The cloud must retain this data for 2 years with 25% redundancy overhead. Compute: (i) the total raw data per year in TB, and (ii) the total cost per month if the cloud charges $0.021 per GB-month.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $N = 100$ machines
- $D_r = 5 \text{ KB} = 5120 \text{ bytes}$ per reading
- Sampling period = 2 s вҶ’ $f = 0.5 \text{ Hz}$
- $h = 24 \text{ hours/day}$, $d = 365 \text{ days/year}$
- $R = 25\% = 0.25$
- $p = \$0.021$ per GB-month

**Step 1 вҖ” Per-machine per-second data rate (0.5 Mark):**
$$
D_{sec} = 5120 \times 0.5 = 2560 \text{ bytes/second}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Aggregate per-second data rate (0.5 Mark):**
$$
D_{agg} = 100 \times 2560 = 256{,}000 \text{ bytes/second}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Bytes per day (0.5 Mark):**
$$
D_{day} = 256{,}000 \times 86{,}400 = 22{,}118{,}400{,}000 \text{ bytes/day}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Bytes per year (0.5 Mark):**
$$
D_{year} = 22{,}118{,}400{,}000 \times 365 = 8{,}073{,}216{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Convert to TB (1 Mark):**
$$
C_{raw} = \frac{8{,}073{,}216{,}000{,}000}{1{,}099{,}511{,}627{,}776} \approx 7.34 \text{ TB/year}
$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Apply redundancy overhead (0.5 Mark):**
$$
C_{total} = 7.34 \times 1.25 \approx 9.18 \text{ TB/year}
$$

**Step 7 вҖ” Convert to GB for cost calculation (0.5 Mark):**
$$
C_{GB} = 9.18 \times 1024 \approx 9{,}400.32 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 8 вҖ” Compute monthly cost (1 Mark):**
$$
C_{month} = 9{,}400.32 \times 0.021 \approx \$197.41 \text{ per month}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion (1 Mark):** The smart factory generates approximately **7.34 TB/year** of raw IIoT data, requires **9.18 TB/year** of storage after redundancy, and incurs a monthly cloud storage cost of approximately **$197.41**. The OpEx cloud model means no upfront hardware investment вҖ” the factory only pays for the storage it uses.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning вҖ” Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):**
>
> 1. **Mixing up units:** KiloBytes vs. Kilobits. $1 \text{ KB} = 1024 \text{ bytes}$, not $1000$ bytes. $1 \text{ TB} = 1024 \text{ GB} = 2^{40}$ bytes. Examiners **will** deduct a full mark for unit errors.
> 2. **Forgetting to include the 24/7 assumption:** A common mistake is to use only 8 working hours/day. The question says *24/7* вҖ” use $h = 24$ unless explicitly stated otherwise.
> 3. **Not applying the redundancy overhead:** If the question states "with 25% redundancy", you **must** multiply by $(1 + 0.25)$. Many students forget this and lose 1вҖ“2 marks.
> 4. **Writing `|x|` in markdown tables:** If you compile a comparison table in your answer, **never** use raw vertical pipes for absolute value. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to avoid breaking the table structure.
> 5. **Confusing IaaS / PaaS / SaaS responsibilities:** Memorize the responsibility table (Section 3.3). A single swapped layer loses 1 full mark.
> 6. **Writing "Cloud Computing is storing data on internet":** This is a textbook *definition trap*. The correct definition emphasizes **on-demand provisioning, elasticity, and measured service** вҖ” not just "storing data".
> 7. **No diagram in long answers:** A 14-mark answer without at least one labeled diagram is automatically capped at 10вҖ“11 marks in the KTU 2024 valuation scheme. Always draw the service-model stack and the hybrid-cloud architecture.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Month-Before-Exam Revision:** Read this section **three times** the night before your KTU exam. Each bullet is a high-yield test point.

- [x] **NIST Definition of Cloud Computing** вҖ” quote it verbatim if possible; examiners reward exact wording.
- [x] **Five Essential Characteristics** вҖ” on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service.
- [x] **Three Service Models** вҖ” IaaS (EC2, Azure VM), PaaS (App Engine, Beanstalk), SaaS (Office 365, MindSphere). Know what the user manages vs. the provider manages.
- [x] **Four Deployment Models** вҖ” Public, Private, Hybrid, Community (plus Multi-cloud as a bonus mention).
- [x] **Hybrid Cloud is dominant in Industry 4.0** because of latency, sovereignty, and burst-scaling requirements.
- [x] **EdgeвҖ“FogвҖ“Cloud Continuum** вҖ” Edge (< 1 ms), Fog (1вҖ“10 ms), Cloud (50вҖ“500 ms latency).
- [x] **Cloud Roles in Industry 4.0** вҖ” predictive maintenance, digital twin, supply-chain optimization, quality inspection, energy management.
- [x] **Storage Sizing Formula** вҖ” $C_{total} = \sum S_i$ and convert to TB using $2^{40}$ divisor.
- [x] **Bandwidth Formula** вҖ” $B = D / T$; remember to convert units consistently.
- [x] **SLA Availability** вҖ” $A = \frac{U}{U+D} \times 100\%$; compute annual downtime in minutes.
- [x] **Cost Model** вҖ” OpEx pay-per-use; convert TB вҶ’ GB before applying $/GB-month pricing.
- [x] **Data Reduction Ratio** вҖ” $R = D_{original} / D_{compressed}$; useful for storage-optimization questions.
- [x] **Real-World Platforms to Quote** вҖ” AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT, Siemens MindSphere, GE Predix, PTC ThingWorx.
- [x] **Draw a diagram in every long answer** вҖ” service-model stack, hybrid-cloud architecture, or data-pipeline flow. No diagram = capped marks.
- [x] **Use KTU buzzwords** вҖ” "pay-as-you-use", "elasticity", "multi-tenant", "service-level agreement", "CapEx vs. OpEx".
- [x] **No `|` in markdown tables** вҖ” use $\lvert$ or $\mid$ for absolute value to avoid layout breakage.
- [x] **One blank line before and after every $$ block** вҖ” formatting matters in KTU digital valuation portals.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Networked Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT525 - INDUSTRY 4.0 AND INDUSTRIAL INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Networked Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Networked Control System (NCS)** is a distributed feedback control architecture in which the system components вҖ” namely the **sensor**, **controller**, and **actuator** вҖ” are spatially distributed and exchange information (control signals, feedback data, actuation commands) over a **shared digital communication network** rather than through point-to-point hardwired connections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard Board Answer):**
> *"A Networked Control System is a closed-loop control system in which the information between the controller and the plant (sensors and actuators) is exchanged through a communication network, replacing the traditional dedicated wiring architecture."*

### Key Terminology Checklist

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Plant** | The physical process or system being controlled (motor, furnace, robotic arm) |
| **Sensor Node** | Converts physical signals (temperature, pressure) to digital packets |
| **Controller** | Computes control law (PID, MPC) based on received sensor data |
| **Actuator Node** | Receives control command and physically actuates the plant |
| **Communication Network** | Shared medium (Ethernet, CAN, Wi-Fi, 5G, TSN) carrying real-time data |
| **Packet** | Discrete unit of data transmission (header + payload) |
| **Time Delay (П„)** | Latency between signal generation and reception |
| **Packet Loss (Оі)** | Percentage of packets dropped during transmission |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The WhatsApp Group Conversation**
>
> Imagine you and your two friends (one who sees the road, one who drives the car) are trying to navigate across a city. Instead of being in the same car (traditional control), you are all in different cars communicating over a **WhatsApp group chat**:
>
> - The **friend who sees the road** = *Sensor* (observes state and sends a message)
> - **You (the navigator)** = *Controller* (thinks and sends instruction)
> - The **friend driving the car** = *Actuator* (executes command)
> - The **WhatsApp group** = *Communication Network*
>
> **Problems that can occur (just like in NCS!):**
> 1. **Message delay** вҖ” Network lag (high latency) вҶ’ late instruction вҶ’ wrong steering
> 2. **Dropped messages** вҖ” Weak internet вҶ’ instruction never reaches вҶ’ car keeps going straight
> 3. **Out-of-order messages** вҖ” Older message arrives after newer one вҶ’ driver confused
> 4. **Limited bandwidth** вҖ” Too many cars in chat group вҶ’ message queue overload
>
> In a **real NCS** (factory floor, power grid, autonomous vehicles), these are not casual inconveniences вҖ” they cause **instability, oscillation, and physical system damage**.

## 1.3 Why Networked Control Systems Matter in Industry 4.0

The transition from **Industry 3.0 (centralized wired control)** to **Industry 4.0 (distributed intelligent control)** fundamentally requires NCS. The four industrial drivers are:

1. **Modularity** вҖ” Plug-and-play addition/removal of nodes
2. **Geographic Distribution** вҖ” Control of plants spanning kilometers (oil pipelines, wind farms, smart grids)
3. **Cost Reduction** вҖ” Shared cabling reduces wiring cost by up to **60%** in automotive manufacturing
4. **Data-Driven Intelligence** вҖ” Network enables edge/cloud analytics and AI integration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The NCS is the **operational backbone** of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) and IIoT. Without NCS, there is no real-time closed-loop Industry 4.0.

## 1.4 Fundamental Architecture of an NCS

A canonical NCS consists of three primary components interconnected by a network:

**Plant вҶ’ Sensor вҶ’ Network вҶ’ Controller вҶ’ Network вҶ’ Actuator вҶ’ Plant**

This forms a **closed feedback loop** where the communication medium (network) is shared between multiple nodes, introducing **network-induced phenomena**.

## 1.5 Network-Induced Imperfections (Core NCS Problem Statement)

> [!WARNING]
> These imperfections are the **fundamental challenges** that distinguish NCS from classical control theory. KTU questions frequently test student understanding of these phenomena.

The three primary imperfections are:

- **Network-Induced Delay (П„)**: The time between source signal generation and destination reception. Comprises:
  - **П„_sc** = sensor-to-controller delay
  - **П„_ca** = controller-to-actuator delay
  - **Total delay П„ = П„_sc + П„_ca**
- **Packet Loss (Оі)**: Network congestion or link failure causes data packets to never arrive. Tolerance limit for stability is typically **Оі вүӨ 0.3** (30% loss).
- **Packet Disordering**: Packets arriving out of sequence, especially problematic for **Time-Stamped** and **Event-Triggered** control schemes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Modeling of an NCS

### 2.1.1 Continuous-Time Plant Model

The physical plant in continuous time is modeled as a linear time-invariant (LTI) system:

$$\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$$

$$y(t) = Cx(t)$$

Where:
- $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ = state vector
- $u(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ = control input
- $y(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ = measured output
- $A, B, C$ = system matrices of appropriate dimensions

### 2.1.2 Discretized Plant with Network Delay

When the controller operates digitally and the network induces delay $\tau_k$ at time-step $k$, the discrete-time dynamics become:

$$x(k+1) = \Phi x(k) + \Gamma_0(\tau_k) u(k) + \Gamma_1(\tau_k) u(k-1)$$

Where:
- $\Phi = e^{Ah}$ (discretized state transition matrix)
- $\Gamma_0(\tau_k) = \int_{0}^{h - \tau_k} e^{As} B \, ds$
- $\Gamma_1(\tau_k) = \int_{h - \tau_k}^{h} e^{As} B \, ds$
- $h$ = sampling period
- $\tau_k$ = time-varying network delay at step $k$

### 2.1.3 Augmented State-Space Form

To incorporate the delayed input as an additional state, define the augmented state vector:

$$z(k) = \begin{bmatrix} x(k) \\ u(k-1) \end{bmatrix}$$

The augmented system is:

$$z(k+1) = \begin{bmatrix} \Phi & \Gamma_1(\tau_k) \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} z(k) + \begin{bmatrix} \Gamma_0(\tau_k) \\ I \end{bmatrix} u(k)$$

This formulation enables **Lyapunov stability analysis** of the NCS.

## 2.2 Stability Conditions

### 2.2.1 Lyapunov Stability Criterion

The NCS is asymptotically stable if and only if there exists a symmetric positive-definite matrix $P \succ 0$ satisfying:

$$A_{cl}^{T} P A_{cl} - P \prec 0$$

Where $A_{cl}$ is the closed-loop matrix of the augmented NCS.

### 2.2.2 Maximum Allowable Delay Bound (MADB)

For a given sampling period $h$, the **Maximum Allowable Delay Bound (MADB)** $\bar{\tau}$ is the largest value of $\tau_k$ for which the system remains stable. It is computed as:

$$\bar{\tau} = \min \{ \tau : \text{system has a real eigenvalue} \geq 0 \}$$

### 2.2.3 Packet Loss Stability Condition

For an NCS with packet loss probability $\gamma$, asymptotic stability is guaranteed if the spectral radius satisfies:

$$\rho(\Phi) \cdot (1 - \gamma) < 1$$

Or equivalently, in terms of maximum successive packet losses $d_{max}$:

$$d_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{\ln(\rho(\Phi))}{\ln(\rho(\Phi) \cdot (1-\gamma))} \right\rfloor$$

## 2.3 NCS Scheduling Strategies

### 2.3.1 Static Scheduling (Time-Triggered)

- Each node gets a **fixed time slot** in a periodic schedule
- Deterministic latency bounds
- Examples: **TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access)**, **TTEthernet**
- Used in: Automotive CAN, avionics

### 2.3.2 Dynamic Scheduling (Event-Triggered)

- Bandwidth allocated **on-demand** when events occur
- Lower average network usage, higher jitter
- Examples: **CSMA/CD (Ethernet)**, **CAN arbitration**
- Used in: Office networks, industrial Ethernet

### 2.3.3 Hybrid Scheduling

- Combines time-triggered for critical control loops + event-triggered for non-critical
- Used in: **TSN (Time-Sensitive Networking)** вҖ” IEEE 802.1Qbv

## 2.4 Control Strategies for NCS

| Strategy | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **PID over Network** | Classical PID with delay compensation (Smith Predictor) | Slow processes (temperature, level) |
| **State Feedback** | $u(k) = -Kx(k)$ with delay-aware gain $K$ | Fast electromechanical systems |
| **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** | Solves optimization over prediction horizon | Constrained multi-variable systems |
| **Event-Triggered Control** | Updates transmitted only when state deviation exceeds threshold | Bandwidth-limited wireless NCS |
| **H-infinity Control** | Robust control minimizing worst-case gain against disturbances | Uncertain network conditions |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Expression | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A_{cl}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} \Phi - L C \Phi & \Gamma_1(\tau) \\ -L C \Phi & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | вҖ” | Closed-loop matrix of NCS with observer |
| $\Phi$ | $e^{Ah}$ | вҖ” | Discrete state transition |
| $\Gamma_0$ | $\int_{0}^{h - \tau} e^{As} B \, ds$ | вҖ” | Input matrix (current step) |
| $\Gamma_1$ | $\int_{h-\tau}^{h} e^{As} B \, ds$ | вҖ” | Input matrix (delayed step) |
| $\tau_{MADB}$ | System-dependent constant | seconds | Maximum Allowable Delay Bound |
| $\bar{d}$ | $\dfrac{\ln \alpha}{\ln(\alpha \cdot (1-\gamma))}$ | packets | Max consecutive packet losses |
| $J_{MPC}$ | $\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \left[ x_{k+i \vert k}^{T} Q x_{k+i \vert k} + u_{k+i \vert k}^{T} R u_{k+i \vert k} \right]$ | вҖ” | MPC cost function |
| $\theta_{ETM}$ | $\Vert x(t) - x(t_k) \Vert > \sigma \Vert x(t) \Vert$ | вҖ” | Event-triggered threshold |
| $R_{TO}$ | $T_{period} \cdot N_{nodes}$ | bits/s | Total network throughput |
| $\eta_{QoS}$ | $1 - \dfrac{P_{lost}}{P_{total}}$ | dimensionless | Quality of Service factor (0 to 1) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grids** вҖ” NCS enables real-time load balancing across distributed power generation
- **Autonomous Vehicles** вҖ” V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle) NCS coordinates platooning at sub-10ms latency
- **Process Industries** вҖ” Wire reduction by **30-50%** in oil refineries using Foundation Fieldbus
- **Robotics** вҖ” Cloud robotics uses NCS to offload heavy computation while maintaining real-time control
- **Healthcare** вҖ” Remote surgery (Da Vinci robot) requires **NCS with latency < 50 ms**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When answering application questions, always link the NCS concept to a specific industry 4.0 vertical (IIoT, smart manufacturing, smart grid, autonomous systems).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Discretization of Continuous Plant with Network Delay

We start with the continuous-time state equation and derive the discrete equivalent with a network-induced delay $\tau_k$.

**Step 1: Start with the continuous-time system**

$$\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$$

**Step 2: Integrate over one sampling interval $[kh, (k+1)h]$**

$$x((k+1)h) = e^{Ah} x(kh) + \int_{kh}^{(k+1)h} e^{A((k+1)h - s)} Bu(s) \, ds$$

**Step 3: Assume the control signal $u(t)$ is constant over one sampling period (zero-order hold)**

For a network-induced delay $\tau_k \in [0, h]$, the control input $u(t)$ is:
- $u(t) = u(k-1)$ for $t \in [kh, kh + \tau_k]$
- $u(t) = u(k)$ for $t \in [kh + \tau_k, (k+1)h]$

**Step 4: Split the integral at the delay boundary**

$$\int_{kh}^{(k+1)h} e^{A((k+1)h - s)} Bu(s) \, ds = \int_{kh}^{kh+\tau_k} e^{A((k+1)h - s)} B u(k-1) \, ds + \int_{kh+\tau_k}^{(k+1)h} e^{A((k+1)h - s)} B u(k) \, ds$$

**Step 5: Apply the substitution $\sigma = (k+1)h - s$ in both integrals**

For the first integral, $\sigma$ ranges from $h - \tau_k$ to $h$:
$$\int_{h-\tau_k}^{h} e^{A\sigma} B u(k-1) \, d\sigma = \Gamma_1(\tau_k) u(k-1)$$

For the second integral, $\sigma$ ranges from $0$ to $h - \tau_k$:
$$\int_{0}^{h - \tau_k} e^{A\sigma} B u(k) \, d\sigma = \Gamma_0(\tau_k) u(k)$$

**Step 6: Combine the result**

$$x(k+1) = e^{Ah} x(k) + \Gamma_0(\tau_k) u(k) + \Gamma_1(\tau_k) u(k-1)$$

**Step 7: Define the discretized matrices**

$$\Phi = e^{Ah}, \quad \Gamma_0(\tau_k) = \int_{0}^{h-\tau_k} e^{A\sigma} B \, d\sigma, \quad \Gamma_1(\tau_k) = \int_{h-\tau_k}^{h} e^{A\sigma} B \, d\sigma$$

**Final discrete NCS equation:**

$$x(k+1) = \Phi x(k) + \Gamma_0(\tau_k) u(k) + \Gamma_1(\tau_k) u(k-1)$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stability Bound on Total Network Delay

**Step 1: Assume the controller is a static state feedback** $u(k) = -Kx(k)$, with zero delay ($\tau_k = 0$).

$$x(k+1) = (\Phi - \Gamma_0 K) x(k)$$

**Step 2: Apply Jury's stability criterion** (for 2D second-order system, all roots inside unit circle):

The eigenvalues $\lambda$ of $(\Phi - \Gamma_0 K)$ must satisfy $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$.

**Step 3: Introduce total delay $\tau = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$ into the system** (using the augmented form):

$$z(k+1) = \begin{bmatrix} \Phi - \Gamma_0 K & \Gamma_1(\tau) \\ -K & 0 \end{bmatrix} z(k)$$

**Step 4: Compute the characteristic polynomial:**

$$\det \begin{bmatrix} \lambda I - (\Phi - \Gamma_0 K) & -\Gamma_1(\tau) \\ K & \lambda I \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

**Step 5: Apply the block determinant identity:**

$$\det(\lambda I - (\Phi - \Gamma_0 K)) \cdot \det\left( \lambda I - K [\lambda I - (\Phi - \Gamma_0 K)]^{-1} \Gamma_1(\tau) \right) = 0$$

**Step 6: Use the matrix inversion lemma to simplify and obtain the delay-dependent stability condition:**

For small delays (first-order Taylor approximation $\Gamma_1(\tau) \approx B\tau$):

$$\bar{\tau} = \dfrac{1 - \rho(\Phi - \Gamma_0 K)}{\Vert B K \Vert_2}$$

**Step 7: Conclude with the practical engineering interpretation:**

The MADB decreases as the control gain $K$ increases, indicating a **trade-off** between fast control response and network delay tolerance.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Packet Loss Stability Margin

**Step 1: Define the state update rule with packet loss:**

$$x(k+1) = \begin{cases} \Phi x(k) + \Gamma_0 K x(k) & \text{if packet received} \\ \Phi x(k) & \text{if packet lost} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Define the indicator random variable:**

$$\delta_k = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{with probability } (1 - \gamma) \\ 0 & \text{with probability } \gamma \end{cases}$$

**Step 3: Express the closed-loop dynamics as a stochastic system:**

$$x(k+1) = \Phi x(k) + \delta_k \Gamma_0 K x(k) = [\Phi + \delta_k \Gamma_0 K] x(k)$$

**Step 4: Compute the expected Lyapunov drift:**

$$\mathbb{E}[\Vert x(k+1) \Vert^2] = \mathbb{E}\left[ x(k)^T (\Phi + \delta_k \Gamma_0 K)^T (\Phi + \delta_k \Gamma_0 K) x(k) \right]$$

**Step 5: Apply the law of total expectation:**

$$\mathbb{E}[\Vert x(k+1) \Vert^2] = (1-\gamma) \Vert (\Phi + \Gamma_0 K) x(k) \Vert^2 + \gamma \Vert \Phi x(k) \Vert^2$$

**Step 6: Bound using the spectral radius $\rho$:**

$$\mathbb{E}[\Vert x(k+1) \Vert^2] \leq \left[ (1-\gamma) \rho(\Phi + \Gamma_0 K)^2 + \gamma \rho(\Phi)^2 \right] \Vert x(k) \Vert^2$$

**Step 7: The mean-square stability condition requires:**

$$(1-\gamma) \rho(\Phi + \Gamma_0 K)^2 + \gamma \rho(\Phi)^2 < 1$$

**Step 8: Rearranging for the maximum allowable loss probability $\gamma_{max}$:**

$$\gamma_{max} < \dfrac{1 - \rho(\Phi + \Gamma_0 K)^2}{\rho(\Phi)^2 - \rho(\Phi + \Gamma_0 K)^2}$$

This is a **critical KTU result** вҖ” it quantifies how reliable the network must be for the closed-loop system to remain stable.

## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU-Style Computation)

**Given:** A scalar NCS with $\Phi = 0.9$, $\Gamma_0 = 0.5$, $K = 1.0$

**Find:** (a) MADB, (b) Maximum allowable packet loss $\gamma_{max}$

**Part (a) Calculation:**

Closed-loop matrix (no delay): $A_{cl} = 0.9 - 0.5(1.0) = 0.4$

$$\bar{\tau} = \dfrac{1 - \rho(A_{cl})}{\Vert B K \Vert_2} = \dfrac{1 - 0.4}{0.5 \times 1.0} = \dfrac{0.6}{0.5} = 1.2 \text{ (in normalized time units)}$$

**Part (b) Calculation:**

$$\rho(\Phi + \Gamma_0 K) = \vert 0.9 + 0.5(1.0) \vert = 1.4$$

Since $\rho(\Phi + \Gamma_0 K) = 1.4 > 1$, the nominal closed-loop is **unstable** with $K = 1.0$. This indicates a poorly designed gain.

**Redesign with $K = 0.5$:**

$$\rho(\Phi + \Gamma_0 K) = \vert 0.9 + 0.5(0.5) \vert = 1.15$$

Still unstable. Need $K < -1.8$ for stability.

**With $K = -2.0$:**

$$\rho(\Phi + \Gamma_0 K) = \vert 0.9 - 1.0 \vert = 0.1$$

$$\gamma_{max} < \dfrac{1 - 0.01}{0.81 - 0.01} = \dfrac{0.99}{0.80} = 1.2375$$

Since the bound exceeds 1, **any packet loss probability is acceptable** for this design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical NCS Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR[Reference Input r t] --> nodeS[Summing Junction]
    nodeY[Output y t] --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeC[Controller u t]
    nodeC --> nodeN1[Network Delay tau_ca]
    nodeN1 --> nodeA[Actuator]
    nodeA --> nodeP[Plant G s]
    nodeP --> nodeY
    nodeY --> nodeSen[Sensor]
    nodeSen --> nodeN2[Network Delay tau_sc]
    nodeN2 --> nodeS
```

## 4.2 Detailed Functional Architecture of an IIoT-NCS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PHY["PHYSICAL LAYER (Field Level)"]
        s1[Temperature Sensor] 
        s2[Pressure Sensor]
        s3[Flow Sensor]
        a1[Heater Actuator]
        a2[Valve Actuator]
        m1[Industrial Motor]
    end
    subgraph EDGE["EDGE LAYER (Network + Computation)"]
        n1[Ethernet TSN Switch]
        n2[Wireless Gateway 5G]
        e1[Edge Controller]
    end
    subgraph CLOUD["CLOUD LAYER (Supervisory)"]
        c1[MPC Optimizer]
        c2[Data Historian]
        c3[Dashboard HMI]
    end
    s1 --> n1
    s2 --> n1
    s3 --> n2
    n1 --> e1
    n2 --> e1
    e1 --> n1
    n1 --> a1
    n1 --> a2
    n2 --> m1
    e1 --> c1
    c1 --> e1
    e1 --> c2
    c2 --> c3
```

## 4.3 Time-Triggered vs Event-Triggered Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[Start Sampling Loop] --> check{Trigger Condition Met}
    check -- YES Event Triggered --> trans[Transmit State to Controller]
    check -- NO Periodic Timer --> time[Wait until Period T expires]
    time --> trans
    trans --> ctrl[Controller Computes u k]
    ctrl --> act[Actuator Receives u k]
    act --> plant[Plant Updates]
    plant --> check
```

## 4.4 Network Imperfection Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    imper[Network Imperfections] --> d1[Delay]
    imper --> d2[Packet Loss]
    imper --> d3[Disordering]
    imper --> d4[Jitter]
    d1 --> d1a[Sensor to Controller tau_sc]
    d1 --> d1b[Controller to Actuator tau_ca]
    d2 --> d2a[Single Loss]
    d2 --> d2b[Burst Loss]
    d2 --> d2c[Probabilistic Loss]
    d3 --> d3a[Out of Sequence]
    d3 --> d3b[Duplication]
```

## 4.5 Closed-Loop Signal Flow with Discrete-Time Sampler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sref[Reference r k] --> sumj[Summing Junction e k]
    sout[y k] --> sumj
    sumj --> ctrl[Digital Controller K z]
    ctrl --> zoh[Zero Order Hold]
    zoh --> gplant[Plant G s]
    gplant --> sout
    sout --> samp[Sampler with Period h]
    samp --> sumj
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** When asked to "draw the NCS block diagram," always show the network block between sensor-controller and controller-actuator, and label the delays $\tau_{sc}$ and $\tau_{ca}$. Examiners allocate **1-2 marks** specifically for this labeling.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q: Define a Networked Control System. List any two advantages and two challenges of NCS over traditional point-to-point control systems.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
A Networked Control System is a closed-loop control system in which the information exchange between the controller and the spatially distributed plant components (sensors and actuators) occurs over a shared digital communication network rather than dedicated point-to-point wiring.

**Advantages (1 Mark вҖ” any two):**
1. Reduced wiring complexity and installation cost (up to 60% savings in automotive)
2. Easier maintenance, diagnostics, and modular addition of new nodes
3. Enables remote control and geographically distributed control

**Challenges (1 Mark вҖ” any two):**
1. Network-induced time delays degrade control performance and may cause instability
2. Packet loss and disordering can lead to incorrect control actions
3. Limited bandwidth shared among multiple nodes creates scheduling conflicts

---

### Question 2: Network Imperfection
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q: Differentiate between network-induced delay and packet loss in an NCS. How does each affect closed-loop stability?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Network-Induced Delay | Packet Loss |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Time between signal generation and reception | Complete failure of packet to arrive |
| Effect on stability | Causes phase lag, can destabilize if $\tau > \tau_{MADB}$ | Acts as intermittent open-loop operation |
| Quantitative measure | Bounded by $\tau_{MADB}$ | Bounded by $\gamma_{max}$ probability |
| Compensated by | Smith Predictor, delay-aware $K$ | Hold-input strategy, retransmission |

**Key Insight (1 Mark):** Delay shifts system phase margin, whereas packet loss reduces effective loop gain intermittently. Both reduce the **Maximum Allowable Transfer Interval (MATI)** for stability.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each вҖ” Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1): Comprehensive NCS Analysis

**[KTU University Exam - July 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Networked Control System. Identify the two types of network-induced delays and explain how they affect system stability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A scalar NCS is described by the discrete equation $x(k+1) = 0.8 x(k) + 0.3 u(k) + 0.1 u(k-1)$. The controller is $u(k) = -K x(k)$. Determine:
- (i) The closed-loop pole location in terms of $K$ **(2 Marks)**
- (ii) The range of $K$ for asymptotic stability **(3 Marks)**
- (iii) The maximum allowable network delay $\bar{\tau}$ if $\Gamma_0 = 0.3$ and $\Gamma_1 = 0.1$ **(2 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) вҖ” Architecture & Stability (7 Marks)**

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):**

The NCS architecture consists of: **Plant вҶ’ Sensor вҶ’ Network вҶ’ Controller вҶ’ Network вҶ’ Actuator вҶ’ Plant** with two network blocks indicating $\tau_{sc}$ (sensor-to-controller delay) and $\tau_{ca}$ (controller-to-actuator delay).

**Two Types of Delays (2 Marks):**
1. **$\tau_{sc}$**: Sensor-to-Controller delay вҖ” from when sensor samples plant to when controller receives the data
2. **$\tau_{ca}$**: Controller-to-Actuator delay вҖ” from when controller sends $u(k)$ to when actuator applies it
3. **Total Delay:** $\tau = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$

**Stability Effect (2 Marks):**
- The delay introduces phase lag equivalent to $e^{-\tau s}$ in continuous time
- This reduces the **phase margin** of the closed-loop system
- Stability is preserved only if $\tau < \tau_{MADB}$ (Maximum Allowable Delay Bound)
- For the augmented discrete system, asymptotic stability requires eigenvalues of the augmented closed-loop matrix to lie strictly inside the unit circle

**Part (b) вҖ” Numerical Analysis (7 Marks)**

**(i) Closed-Loop Pole (2 Marks):**

Setting $u(k) = -K x(k)$ and $u(k-1) = -K x(k-1)$ in the augmented form, the closed-loop characteristic equation for the no-delay case ($\tau = 0$):

$$x(k+1) = (0.8 - 0.3K) x(k)$$

The closed-loop pole is: $\lambda = 0.8 - 0.3K$

**(ii) Stability Range (3 Marks):**

For asymptotic stability, $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$:

$$\vert 0.8 - 0.3K \vert < 1$$

$$-1 < 0.8 - 0.3K < 1$$

**Left inequality:** $0.8 - 0.3K > -1 \Rightarrow 0.3K < 1.8 \Rightarrow K < 6$

**Right inequality:** $0.8 - 0.3K < 1 \Rightarrow -0.3K < 0.2 \Rightarrow K > -0.667$

**Stability Range:** $-0.667 < K < 6$

**(iii) Maximum Allowable Delay (2 Marks):**

$$\bar{\tau} = \dfrac{1 - \rho(\Phi - \Gamma_0 K)}{\Vert \Gamma_1 K \Vert_2}$$

Using the stabilizing gain $K = 2$ (mid-range):

$$\rho(\Phi - \Gamma_0 K) = \vert 0.8 - 0.3(2) \vert = 0.2$$

$$\Vert \Gamma_1 K \Vert_2 = 0.1 \times 2 = 0.2$$

$$\bar{\tau} = \dfrac{1 - 0.2}{0.2} = \dfrac{0.8}{0.2} = 4 \text{ (normalized time units)}$$

---

### Question B (Option 2): Scheduling & Control Strategies

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q: (a)** Compare the static (time-triggered) and dynamic (event-triggered) scheduling strategies in NCS. Under what conditions is each preferred? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An industrial NCS has closed-loop matrix eigenvalues at $\lambda_1 = 0.7$ and $\lambda_2 = 0.5$. The packet loss probability on the network is $\gamma = 0.2$.
- (i) Calculate the maximum eigenvalue $\rho(\Phi)$ and check if the system is stable **(2 Marks)**
- (ii) Derive the maximum allowable packet loss probability $\gamma_{max}$ for mean-square stability **(3 Marks)**
- (iii) Comment on whether the current design is robust to packet loss **(2 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) вҖ” Scheduling Comparison (7 Marks)**

| Aspect | Static (Time-Triggered) | Dynamic (Event-Triggered) |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Periodic clock | State deviation threshold |
| Determinism | Highly deterministic | Stochastic latency |
| Bandwidth usage | Fixed (may waste) | Adaptive (efficient) |
| Jitter | Zero | Variable |
| Implementation | TDMA, TTEthernet | CSMA/CD, CAN |
| Industrial use | Automotive, avionics | Process control, wireless |

**Static Preferred When:** **(2 Marks)**
- Hard real-time deadlines must be guaranteed (e.g., anti-lock braking)
- Determinism is critical (aerospace, medical devices)
- Number of nodes is fixed and known

**Dynamic Preferred When:** **(2 Marks)**
- Network bandwidth is limited (wireless sensor networks)
- Traffic is bursty and unpredictable
- Energy efficiency is required (battery-powered nodes)

**Hybrid TSN Approach (1 Mark):** Modern Industry 4.0 uses IEEE 802.1Qbv TSN to combine both вҖ” time-triggered for critical loops and event-triggered for best-effort traffic.

**Part (b) вҖ” Packet Loss Analysis (7 Marks)**

**(i) Stability Check (2 Marks):**

$$\rho(\Phi) = \max(\vert 0.7 \vert, \vert 0.5 \vert) = 0.7$$

Since $\rho(\Phi) = 0.7 < 1$, the system is **asymptotically stable** in the no-loss case.

**(ii) Maximum Allowable Loss (3 Marks):**

The mean-square stability condition is:

$$(1 - \gamma) \rho(\Phi_{cl})^2 + \gamma \rho(\Phi)^2 < 1$$

Assuming $\rho(\Phi_{cl}) \approx 0$ (well-designed controller, output regulator):

$$(1 - \gamma) \cdot 0 + \gamma (0.7)^2 < 1$$

$$0.49 \gamma < 1$$

$$\gamma < 2.04$$

Since $\gamma$ is bounded in $[0, 1]$, any $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ is theoretically tolerated.

For a more realistic closed-loop with $\rho(\Phi_{cl}) = 0.3$:

$$(1-\gamma)(0.09) + \gamma(0.49) < 1$$

$$0.09 - 0.09\gamma + 0.49\gamma < 1$$

$$0.40\gamma < 0.91$$

$$\gamma_{max} = 2.275$$

Again, bounded by physical limits, so the system tolerates **any** packet loss.

**(iii) Robustness Comment (2 Marks):**

The system is **highly robust to packet loss** because:
- Open-loop eigenvalues are well within the unit circle ($\rho = 0.7$)
- Strong damping means loss of correction has minimal cumulative effect
- For practical IIoT deployment, this design can tolerate noisy industrial networks (e.g., 30-40% loss) without controller redesign

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include the augmented state** when delay is present вҖ” losing 2-3 marks
> 2. **Sign errors in $A_{cl}$** вҖ” students often write $A_{cl} = \Phi + \Gamma_0 K$ instead of $\Phi - \Gamma_0 K$
> 3. **Confusing $\tau_{sc}$ and $\tau_{ca}$** in diagrams вҖ” examiners specifically check this labeling
> 4. **Not stating the stability region** as a strict inequality вҖ” must use $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$, not $\leq$
> 5. **Ignoring units** in MADB calculations вҖ” always specify "normalized time units" or "seconds"

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for NCS**

### Core Concepts (Must Know)
- [x] NCS = Closed-loop control with network-mediated feedback
- [x] Two network delays: $\tau_{sc}$ (sensor-to-controller) and $\tau_{ca}$ (controller-to-actuator)
- [x] Three network imperfections: delay, packet loss, disordering
- [x] Augmented state-space model is the standard analysis framework
- [x] MADB = Maximum Allowable Delay Bound (system-specific constant)

### Critical Formulas (Must Memorize)
- [x] $\Phi = e^{Ah}$ вҖ” discretized state matrix
- [x] $\Gamma_0(\tau) = \int_{0}^{h-\tau} e^{As} B \, ds$
- [x] $\Gamma_1(\tau) = \int_{h-\tau}^{h} e^{As} B \, ds$
- [x] Stability: $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$ for all closed-loop eigenvalues
- [x] Packet loss bound: $\gamma_{max} < \dfrac{1 - \rho(\Phi_{cl})^2}{\rho(\Phi)^2 - \rho(\Phi_{cl})^2}$

### Industrial Standards (Must Recognize)
- [x] **TSN (IEEE 802.1Qbv)** вҖ” Time-Sensitive Networking for Industry 4.0
- [x] **OPC UA** вҖ” Industrial M2M communication protocol
- [x] **EtherCAT** вҖ” Real-time Ethernet for NCS
- [x] **CAN bus** вҖ” Automotive NCS standard
- [x] **5G URLLC** вҖ” Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication for wireless NCS

### Real-World IIoT Applications
- [x] Smart manufacturing (Industry 4.0 closed-loop)
- [x] Autonomous vehicle platooning (V2V NCS)
- [x] Smart grid load-frequency control
- [x] Remote robotic surgery
- [x] Distributed wind farm pitch control

### Common KTU 14-Mark Question Patterns
- [x] Derive discrete NCS model from continuous plant (with delay)
- [x] Compute MADB for given system matrices
- [x] Find stability range of controller gain $K$
- [x] Compare scheduling strategies (static vs dynamic)
- [x] Analyze packet loss tolerance

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When answering "Explain NCS" questions, always use the **three-part structure**: (1) Architecture with block diagram, (2) Network-induced challenges, (3) Stability/control solutions. This structure maps directly to KTU's marking scheme and ensures full marks allocation across the rubric.
