# SECTION 5 вҖ” KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam вҖ” May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define process analytics. List and briefly explain its four pillars.**

**Model Answer:**

Process analytics is the application of statistical, computational, and ML techniques on industrial process data to derive actionable insights.

Four pillars:

1. **Descriptive Analytics** вҖ” Tells *what happened* using dashboards, KPIs, historical trend charts. *Example:* OEE dashboard on a packaging line.
2. **Diagnostic Analytics** вҖ” Tells *why it happened* via root-cause analysis and correlation. *Example:* Splunk dashboard correlating alarm frequency with ambient temperature.
3. **Predictive Analytics** вҖ” Tells *what will happen* via ML forecasts. *Example:* LSTM model predicting bearing RUL.
4. **Prescriptive Analytics** вҖ” Tells *what to do* via optimization and recommendations. *Example:* Closed-loop recipe adjustment in a mixing tank.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students list only **3 pillars** (omitting *Prescriptive*) вҖ” that costs 1 full mark. Always write all four with a one-line industrial example for each.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023]**
**Compare open-source and commercial analytics tools along three dimensions relevant to Industry 4.0.**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | Open Source | Commercial |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| **Licence Cost** | Free / community edition; pay only for support | Per-seat or per-sensor licence fees |
| **Time-to-Value** | Longer вҖ” needs integration, customization | Shorter вҖ” pre-integrated, ready-to-deploy |
| **Support** | Community forums; paid 3rd-party support (e.g., Confluent) | Guaranteed 24/7 enterprise SLAs |
| **Customization** | Full source access вҶ’ highly customizable | Vendor-locked; limited customization |
| **Compliance** | Self-managed hardening required | Pre-certified (SOC2, FedRAMP, HIPAA) |

---

## 5.2 Part B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** вҖ” *[KTU University Exam вҖ” Sample Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **end-to-end architecture of a process analytics pipeline** in an Industry 4.0 environment. Identify one open-source tool for each layer with justification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Apache Kafka** as an open-source streaming tool for IIoT. Explain its architecture, key components, and a use-case in process analytics. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The end-to-end process analytics pipeline in Industry 4.0 consists of **five layers**:

1. **Edge / Data Acquisition Layer** вҖ” Sensors, PLCs, SCADA, DCS, OPC-UA / Modbus / MQTT devices. *OSS Tool:* **Node-RED / Eclipse Kura** for protocol bridging and edge analytics.
2. **Ingestion Layer** вҖ” Streaming broker that buffers high-velocity data. *OSS Tool:* **Apache Kafka** вҖ” distributed, fault-tolerant, horizontal-scalable commit-log.
3. **Storage Layer** вҖ” Time-series DB + data lake for raw + curated data. *OSS Tool:* **InfluxDB** for TSDB + **Hadoop HDFS** for archival.
4. **Processing Layer** вҖ” Distributed compute for batch + stream. *OSS Tool:* **Apache Spark** with Structured Streaming.
5. **Analytics & Visualization Layer** вҖ” ML modelling + dashboards. *OSS Tools:* **Python (Scikit-learn, TensorFlow)** for ML + **Grafana** for dashboards.

**Justification example:** Apache Kafka was chosen over RabbitMQ because Kafka persists messages on disk (RabbitMQ deletes on consume), enabling **replay for batch ML training** вҖ” a critical need for predictive-maintenance models.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Apache Kafka** is a distributed streaming platform originally developed at LinkedIn and now under the Apache Foundation.

**Architecture components:**

- **Broker** вҖ” A Kafka server hosting one or more partitions.
- **Topic** вҖ” A named stream of records; partitioned for parallelism.
- **Partition** вҖ” Ordered, append-only sequence; the unit of parallelism.
- **Producer** вҖ” Publishes records to topics.
- **Consumer** вҖ” Subscribes to topics via *consumer groups*.
- **ZooKeeper / KRaft** вҖ” Coordination service for cluster metadata (KRaft replaces ZooKeeper in Kafka 3.x+).

**Key features:**

- **High throughput** вүҲ millions of messages/sec on commodity hardware.
- **Durability** вҖ” messages persisted on disk with replication factor вүҘ 3.
- **Horizontal scalability** вҖ” add brokers to scale throughput.
- **Stream processing** вҖ” via **Kafka Streams** library.

**Use-case in process analytics:**
A bottling plant with 10 000 sensors streams vibration + temperature to Kafka topic `machine_telemetry`. A Spark Streaming consumer subscribes to the topic, computes rolling RMS vibration every 5 seconds, and triggers an isolation-forest anomaly detector that publishes predictions to topic `predictions`. Grafana subscribes to `predictions` to show real-time anomaly heatmaps on a factory-floor TV.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse Kafka with a **message queue** like RabbitMQ. KTU expects the answer to mention: *(i) Kafka is a distributed log, not a queue*, *(ii) topics are partitioned*, *(iii) messages are persisted and replayable*. Skipping these three points loses up to 3 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** вҖ” *[KTU University Exam вҖ” Model Question]*

**(a)** Discuss any **three commercial process analytics tools** with their key features and industrial use-cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A medium-scale food-processing industry with **strict FSSAI traceability requirements** wishes to deploy process analytics. Recommend an appropriate commercial stack and justify your selection over an open-source alternative. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Splunk Enterprise**
- *Key features:* Index-based log search; SPL query language; pre-built dashboards for OT/IT logs; MLTK (Machine Learning Toolkit).
- *Use-case:* Diagnostic analytics on SCADA alarm logs to identify recurring failure modes on a packaging line.

**2. IBM Watson Studio**
- *Key features:* AutoML, governed model deployment, fairness & explainability, integration with Cognos BI.
- *Use-case:* Predictive quality analytics in a pharmaceutical batch process вҖ” predict batch-yield deviation from raw-material properties.

**3. Siemens MindSphere**
- *Key features:* Native integration with Siemens PLCs (S7-1500), asset model based on RAMI 4.0, closed-loop write-back to PLC.
- *Use-case:* Fleet-level energy optimization on a Siemens-Native discrete plant вҖ” analytics runs on MindSphere and writes back set-points to PLCs.

**4. (Bonus) Tableau / Power BI** for visualization-only commercial BI layer.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Recommended commercial stack:** **Splunk Enterprise (ingestion + diagnostic) вҶ’ Snowflake (data warehouse) вҶ’ IBM Watson Studio (predictive ML) вҶ’ Power BI (visualization)**, with **FSSAI traceability hooks**.

**Justification over open-source alternative:**

1. **Regulatory Compliance** вҖ” FSSAI traceability requires **tamper-proof audit logs** and **validated versions**. Commercial tools ship with pre-validated **21 CFR Part 11 / GxP modules** which open-source stacks lack out-of-the-box. Self-validation of an OSS stack would take 6вҖ“12 months and incur certification risk.

2. **SLA & Reliability** вҖ” Food-processing lines run 24Г—7; downtime costs вӮ№ 5вҖ“10 lakh/hour. Commercial tools give **99.95 % uptime SLAs** with on-call vendor support.

3. **Time-to-Value** вҖ” Pre-built connectors for ERP (SAP, Oracle), PLCs, and LIMS mean faster deployment (вүҲ 4вҖ“6 weeks) vs. an OSS build (вүҲ 16вҖ“24 weeks).

4. **Long-Term Maintainability** вҖ” Vendor handles upgrades, security patches, and version compatibility вҖ” critical when internal IT bandwidth is limited.

5. **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** вҖ” Although licence cost is higher, the avoided cost of *downtime + audit failure + delayed product launch* justifies the spend.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write only "commercial is better because it has support." KTU expects **regulatory + SLA + TCO reasoning specifically tied to FSSAI**. Use the word **"traceability" and "audit trail"** explicitly to score full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap вҖ” Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist вҖ” KTU Module 4: Process Analytics Tools**

- вң… **Process Analytics = Descriptive + Diagnostic + Predictive + Prescriptive.** Always quote all **four pillars**.
- вң… **Open Source вү  Free in total cost.** Account for **ops + skill + training** in TCO.
- вң… **Five-layer pipeline:** *Edge вҶ’ Ingest вҶ’ Store вҶ’ Process вҶ’ ML вҶ’ Viz* (Memorize tool-for-each-layer mapping).
- вң… **Memorize at least 3 OSS tools:** Apache **Kafka** (ingest), Apache **Spark** (process), **InfluxDB / Grafana** (store/viz), **Python + Scikit-learn** (ML), **KNIME** (visual analytics).
- вң… **Memorize at least 3 Commercial tools:** **Splunk** (log analytics), **IBM Watson Studio** (AutoML), **Power BI / Tableau** (BI), **Snowflake** (warehouse), **MindSphere / ThingWorx** (IIoT platforms).
- вң… **Kafka vocabulary:** Broker, Topic, Partition, Producer, Consumer, Consumer Group, ZooKeeper / KRaft.
- вң… **Selection criteria:** Cost, Scalability, Time-to-Value, Support/SLA, Customization, Compliance.
- вң… **Weighted-score formula:** $\text{Score} = \sum w_i \cdot s_i$ вҖ” practice numerical computation.
- вң… **Latency formula:** $L_{e2e} = L_{ingest} + L_{persist} + L_{process} + L_{viz}$ вҖ” required for closed-loop control.
- вң… **Throughput sizing:** $\Theta = P \cdot B \cdot \theta_{partition}$; partition count derived from sensor fan-out.
- вң… **Hybrid stacks are realistic:** Most enterprises use OSS for ingestion + Commercial for BI/Governance.
- вң… **For regulated industries** (pharma, food, aero), prefer Commercial tools for **audit + traceability**.
- вң… **For SMEs / cost-sensitive plants**, prefer OSS stacks like **ThingsBoard + InfluxDB + Grafana + Python**.
- вң… **Avoid pitfalls:** Don't call Kafka a "queue", don't list only 3 pillars, don't say OSS is "always cheaper".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Data Management in the Cloud
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT525 - INDUSTRY 4.0 AND INDUSTRIAL INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process Data Management in the Cloud

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Data Management in the Cloud** refers to the end-to-end framework of acquiring, transmitting, storing, processing, governing, and retrieving industrial process data using cloud computing infrastructure. In the context of **Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)** and **Industry 4.0**, it encompasses the cloud-side architecture responsible for ingesting high-velocity telemetry from shop-floor sensors, actuators, PLCs, and SCADA systems, persisting it in distributed storage systems, transforming it through batch or stream pipelines, and exposing it to analytics, dashboards, and machine learning workloads.

The three foundational pillars of cloud-based process data management are:

1. **Data Ingestion Layer** вҖ” protocols (MQTT, AMQP, OPC UA, HTTP/REST, CoAP) that collect sensor data.
2. **Data Storage & Processing Layer** вҖ” distributed databases, data lakes, warehouses, and compute engines.
3. **Data Consumption Layer** вҖ” analytics, visualization (BI dashboards), ML/AI inference, and control feedback loops.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The "Smart Hospital" Metaphor

Imagine a massive hospital where every patient (an industrial machine on a factory floor) wears a smartwatch (a sensor). The smartwatch continuously records heart rate, oxygen level, and temperature.

- The **patient rooms** are the shop-floor equipment.
- The **smartwatch** is the IIoT sensor.
- The **wireless signal** is the MQTT/HTTP transmission protocol.
- The **central hospital database** is the **Cloud Storage Layer**.
- The **doctors analyzing the records** are the **Analytics & AI Layer**.
- The **nurse stations monitoring dashboards** are the **Visualization Layer**.

Just as the hospital does not store every heartbeat reading in a written file cabinet but in a structured Electronic Health Record (EHR) system, modern factories do not store process data in local PCs вҖ” they send it to the **cloud**, where it is indexed, time-stamped, compressed, and made queryable from anywhere in the world.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (KTU High-Yield):** The shift from **local SCADA historians** to **cloud historians** is the defining transition of Industry 4.0 data management. Cloud historians offer **elastic scalability**, **multi-site aggregation**, and **AI-readiness** вҖ” features that legacy on-premise systems cannot match.

### 1.3 Core Standard Metrics (Industry-Grade)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|---|
| Ingestion Throughput | $\lambda$ | **10,000 вҖ“ 1,000,000 events/sec** |
| End-to-End Latency | $L_{e2e}$ | **< 100 ms (real-time)** / **minutes (batch)** |
| Data Retention Period | $T_{ret}$ | **90 days (hot)** / **5+ years (cold/archive)** |
| Compression Ratio | $\rho_c$ | **5:1 to 10:1** (telemetry) |
| Availability SLA | $A$ | **99.9% вҖ“ 99.99%** |

### 1.4 Why Cloud Data Management Matters in IIoT

- **Scalability** вҖ” Petabyte-scale storage grows on demand.
- **Accessibility** вҖ” Engineers, managers, and AI models access data globally.
- **Integration** вҖ” Seamless interoperability with ERP, MES, and CRM.
- **Cost Efficiency** вҖ” Pay-as-you-go replaces heavy CapEx.
- **Advanced Analytics** вҖ” Native support for ML, anomaly detection, predictive maintenance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Stage Data Lifecycle in Cloud IIoT

**Stage 1 вҖ” Acquisition (Edge вҶ’ Gateway)**
- Sensors (temperature, pressure, vibration, flow) sample physical phenomena.
- Edge gateways (e.g., Siemens IOT2050, AWS Greengrass) perform local aggregation, filtering, and protocol translation.
- Data is buffered in ring buffers when the network is intermittent.

**Stage 2 вҖ” Ingestion (Gateway вҶ’ Cloud Broker)**
- Publish/subscribe brokers (HiveMQ, EMQX, AWS IoT Core) accept MQTT topics.
- Message queuing telemetry transport (MQTT) operates over TCP port **8883** (TLS) and **1883** (plain).
- Quality of Service (QoS) levels: **0** (at most once), **1** (at least once), **2** (exactly once).

**Stage 3 вҖ” Storage (Cloud)**
- **Time-Series Databases (TSDB):** InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, AWS Timestream вҖ” optimized for high-write, low-cardinality queries.
- **Data Lakes:** AWS S3, Azure Data Lake Gen2 вҖ” store raw, semi-structured, and unstructured data in Parquet/ORC/Avro formats.
- **Data Warehouses:** Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift вҖ” columnar storage for OLAP analytics.
- **NoSQL Stores:** Cassandra, MongoDB вҖ” for asset metadata, configuration, and documents.

**Stage 4 вҖ” Processing**
- **Batch Processing:** Apache Hadoop (MapReduce), Apache Spark вҖ” operates on historical windows.
- **Stream Processing:** Apache Flink, Apache Kafka Streams, AWS Kinesis вҖ” operates on unbounded event streams using **windowing strategies** (tumbling, sliding, session).
- **Lambda Architecture:** Combines batch + speed layer for both accuracy and low latency.
- **Kappa Architecture:** Single stream-processing pipeline; simplifies operations.

**Stage 5 вҖ” Consumption**
- BI Dashboards (Power BI, Grafana, Tableau).
- ML model serving (predictive maintenance, anomaly detection).
- Closed-loop control signals sent back to PLCs (rare but emerging).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** Distinguish between **Hot Path** (real-time stream processing) and **Cold Path** (batch processing on historical data). In a 14-mark question, examiners expect you to draw a clear architectural block diagram showing both paths.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Little's Law** (queuing) | $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | items, events/s, s | $L$ = items in system, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $W$ = avg wait time |
| 2 | **Throughput** | $\lambda = \dfrac{N}{T}$ | events/s | $N$ = total events, $T$ = observation window |
| 3 | **End-to-End Latency** | $L_{e2e} = L_{net} + L_{ing} + L_{proc} + L_{query}$ | ms | Sum of all pipeline stages |
| 4 | **Storage Capacity** | $C_{s} = \lambda \cdot s_{avg} \cdot T_{ret} \cdot \rho_{c}^{-1}$ | bytes | $s_{avg}$ = avg sample size, $\rho_c$ = compression ratio |
| 5 | **Compression Ratio** | $\rho_{c} = \dfrac{S_{orig}}{S_{comp}}$ | dimensionless | Higher = better compression |
| 6 | **Availability SLA** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \cdot 100\%$ | % | $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures, $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair |
| 7 | **Annual Downtime** | $D_{yr} = (1 - A) \cdot 525600$ | min/yr | 525,600 = minutes in a year |
| 8 | **Batch Window Throughput** | $\lambda_{batch} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}{\Delta t_{batch}}$ | records/s | $V_i$ = batch sizes |
| 9 | **Kafka Partition Throughput** | $\lambda_{partition} \leq 10$ MB/s | MB/s | Empirically bounded per partition |
| 10 | **Stream Window Aggregation** | $W_{agg} = \{(t, x) \mid t \in [t_0, t_0 + \Delta)\}$ | вҖ” | Tumbling window of size $\Delta$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip (Board Pattern):** When a question asks "design a cloud data pipeline", explicitly mention **Little's Law** to size the buffer and **$L_{e2e}$** to justify SLA-bound cloud region selection. These are favorite valuation hooks.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Predictive Maintenance (Siemens MindSphere):** Cloud-stored vibration + temperature data trains ML models that predict motor failure 7вҖ“14 days in advance.
- **Energy Optimization (GE Predix):** Wind turbine SCADA data is streamed to cloud; analytics optimize pitch angles in real time.
- **Smart Grids (AWS IoT + Timestream):** Substation telemetry aggregated across 1000+ feeders, queried in sub-second latency.
- **Pharma Batch Release:** Cloud historian ensures FDA 21 CFR Part 11 compliance with audit trails.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section demonstrates three core analytical exercises a KTU examiner may set as sub-parts of a 14-mark question. Each derivation is fully worked out, with no skipped steps.

### 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 вҖ” Sizing Cloud Storage Capacity for a Plant

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
A manufacturing plant has **500 sensors**, each sampling at **$f_s = 2$ Hz**. Each sample is encoded as a JSON payload of **$s_{avg} = 256$ bytes**. The plant wants to retain hot data for **$T_{hot} = 30$ days** and cold/archive data for **$T_{cold} = 5$ years** at a compression ratio of $\rho_c = 8$. Calculate the total storage required.

**Step 1 вҖ” Total arrival rate $\lambda$:**

$$
\lambda = N \cdot f_s = 500 \cdot 2 = 1000 \; \text{samples/s}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Raw daily volume $V_{day}$:**

$$
V_{day} = \lambda \cdot s_{avg} \cdot 86400
$$

$$
V_{day} = 1000 \cdot 256 \cdot 86400
$$

$$
V_{day} = 256000 \cdot 86400
$$

$$
V_{day} = 2.21184 \times 10^{10} \; \text{bytes/day} \approx 22.12 \; \text{GB/day}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Hot storage volume (30 days):**

$$
C_{hot} = V_{day} \cdot T_{hot} = 22.12 \cdot 30 = 663.6 \; \text{GB}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Cold storage volume (5 years = 1825 days), compressed:**

$$
C_{cold} = \dfrac{V_{day} \cdot 1825}{\rho_c}
$$

$$
C_{cold} = \dfrac{22.12 \cdot 1825}{8} = \dfrac{40369}{8} \approx 5046.1 \; \text{GB} \approx 4.93 \; \text{TB}
$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Total storage requirement:**

$$
C_{total} = C_{hot} + C_{cold} \approx 0.65 \; \text{TB} + 4.93 \; \text{TB} \approx 5.58 \; \text{TB}
$$

> **Engineering Conclusion:** The plant needs roughly **5.6 TB** of cloud storage. This justifies the choice of AWS S3 Standard-IA for hot tier and S3 Glacier for cold tier вҖ” both deliver cost-effective lifecycle management.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 вҖ” Applying Little's Law to Size an Ingestion Buffer

**Problem Statement:**
The cloud broker must buffer messages during network outages. Suppose messages arrive at $\lambda = 5000$ events/s, and the average time a message spends in the broker (waiting + processing) is $W = 80$ ms. Find the **average number of in-flight messages** $L$.

**Step 1 вҖ” State Little's Law:**

$$
L = \lambda \cdot W
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Convert $W$ to seconds (unit consistency):**

$$
W = 80 \; \text{ms} = 0.080 \; \text{s}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Substitute:**

$$
L = 5000 \cdot 0.080
$$

$$
L = 400 \; \text{messages}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Convert to memory requirement (assuming 256 B payload):**

$$
M_{buffer} = L \cdot s_{avg} = 400 \cdot 256 = 102400 \; \text{bytes} = 100 \; \text{KB}
$$

> **Engineering Conclusion:** The broker only needs a **100 KB** in-memory queue to absorb backlog вҖ” well within standard Kafka partition defaults.

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 вҖ” Availability SLA вҶ’ Annual Downtime

**Problem Statement:**
A cloud provider advertises **99.95%** availability. Compute the maximum allowable annual downtime.

**Step 1 вҖ” Apply formula:**

$$
D_{yr} = (1 - A) \cdot 525600
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Compute unavailability fraction:**

$$
1 - A = 1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Multiply by minutes in a year:**

$$
D_{yr} = 0.0005 \cdot 525600
$$

$$
D_{yr} = 262.8 \; \text{minutes/year}
$$

$$
D_{yr} \approx 4 \; \text{hours} \; 22 \; \text{minutes} \; 48 \; \text{seconds}
$$

> **Engineering Conclusion:** For a mission-critical plant, **99.99%** is preferred ($D_{yr} \approx 52.6$ min/yr). Always cite this in safety-critical justifications.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix вҖ” Choosing a Cloud Storage Tier

| Storage Tier | Access Latency | Cost/GB/month | Use Case | KTU-Mapped Stage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **In-Memory (Redis)** | < 1 ms | High | Real-time dashboards | Stage 5 (Consumption) |
| **TSDB (InfluxDB/Timestream)** | 10вҖ“100 ms | Medium | Hot sensor data | Stage 3 (Hot Storage) |
| **Object Store (S3 Standard)** | 50вҖ“200 ms | Low | Raw JSON/Parquet lake | Stage 3 (Data Lake) |
| **Warehouse (Redshift/BigQuery)** | Seconds | Medium | BI analytics | Stage 4 (Batch) |
| **Archive (S3 Glacier)** | MinutesвҖ“Hours | Very Low | Compliance retention | Stage 3 (Cold Storage) |

flowchart TB

### 4.3 Data Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Acquired
    Acquired --> Ingested : MQTT Publish
    Ingested --> Buffered : Network Down
    Buffered --> Ingested : Network Up
    Ingested --> Hot : Within 30 days
    Hot --> Warm : 30 to 90 days
    Warm --> Cold : More than 90 days
    Cold --> Purged : Retention Expired
    Purged --> [*]
    Ingested --> Anomaly : ML Flagged
    Anomaly --> Alerted : Threshold Crossed
    Alerted --> [*]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Paper 2024]**
*Define cloud-based process data management in the context of IIoT. List any three advantages of using cloud for industrial data.*

**Model Answer (Expected KTU Key):**

Cloud-based process data management is the systematic acquisition, transmission, storage, processing, and retrieval of industrial process data using cloud computing infrastructure (public, private, or hybrid). In IIoT, it serves as the centralized backbone where field telemetry from sensors, PLCs, and SCADA systems is ingested, persisted, and analyzed.

**Three Advantages:**
1. **Elastic Scalability** вҖ” Storage and compute resources scale with data growth without hardware procurement.
2. **Global Accessibility** вҖ” Engineers, data scientists, and managers can query data from any location.
3. **AI/ML Integration** вҖ” Cloud platforms provide native services (AWS SageMaker, Azure ML) for predictive analytics on industrial data.

> **Valuation Tip:** Award full 3 marks only if the student mentions *all three aspects* вҖ” **ingestion, storage, processing**. A vague answer on "cloud storage" alone fetches only 1.5 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Supplementary 2024]**
*Differentiate between Time-Series Databases (TSDB) and Data Lakes. Give one industrial use case for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Time-Series Database (TSDB) | Data Lake |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Type** | Numeric, time-stamped, structured telemetry | Raw, semi-structured, unstructured |
| **Schema** | Fixed schema, indexed by time | Schema-on-read |
| **Query** | Time-window aggregations, downsampling | Full-text search, batch SQL |
| **Examples** | InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, AWS Timestream | AWS S3, Azure Data Lake Gen2 |
| **Industrial Use Case** | Real-time vibration monitoring of rotating machinery | Long-term archival of SCADA logs and maintenance records |

> **Valuation Tip:** A table-format answer is the gold standard. Award 1.5 marks for the difference and 1.5 marks for the use cases.

### 5.2 Part B вҖ” 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often **skip the diagram**. A 14-mark question without a labeled architecture block diagram loses **at least 3 marks**.
> - Do not confuse **MQTT QoS 1** with **QoS 2** in protocol-related questions вҖ” examiners are strict.
> - Always **state units** in storage/throughput calculations. A correct number without "GB" or "events/s" may fetch only partial credit.
> - For "design a pipeline" questions, mention **at least one real cloud service** (AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT) to score top-band marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) вҖ” Cloud Pipeline Design**

**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-stage cloud data management architecture** for a smart factory. Label the ingestion protocol, storage tiers, and processing engines. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A refinery has **1200 sensors** sampling at **5 Hz**, with each sample being **512 bytes**. Hot retention is **45 days**, cold retention is **7 years**, compression ratio is **10:1**. Calculate:
- (i) Total daily raw data volume.
- (ii) Hot storage capacity.
- (iii) Cold storage capacity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five-Stage Architecture (7 Marks):**

| Stage | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Acquisition | Sensors, PLCs, SCADA | Sample physical parameters |
| 2. Ingestion | Edge gateway, MQTT broker | Publish over TLS to cloud |
| 3. Storage | TSDB (hot) + Data Lake (cold) | Persist time-series + raw |
| 4. Processing | Spark (batch) + Flink (stream) | Aggregate, transform, enrich |
| 5. Consumption | Grafana, ML models, alerts | Dashboard, prediction, notification |

A **labeled block diagram** is mandatory for full marks (see SECTION 4.1).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Total daily raw volume:**

$$
\lambda = 1200 \cdot 5 = 6000 \; \text{samples/s}
$$

$$
V_{day} = 6000 \cdot 512 \cdot 86400 = 2.654 \times 10^{11} \; \text{bytes} \approx 265.4 \; \text{GB/day}
$$

**(ii) Hot storage (45 days, no compression):**

$$
C_{hot} = 265.4 \cdot 45 = 11943 \; \text{GB} \approx 11.67 \; \text{TB}
$$

**(iii) Cold storage (7 years = 2555 days, compressed 10:1):**

$$
C_{cold} = \dfrac{265.4 \cdot 2555}{10} = 67809.7 \; \text{GB} \approx 66.22 \; \text{TB}
$$

**Total storage $\approx$ 77.9 TB.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” Streaming & Data Lifecycle**

**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Sessional 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Compare **Lambda Architecture** and **Kappa Architecture** for real-time industrial data processing. State one advantage and one limitation of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **industrial data lifecycle** from acquisition to archival, with a clear state-transition diagram. Discuss the role of MQTT QoS levels in this lifecycle. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Lambda vs Kappa (7 Marks):**

| Feature | Lambda Architecture | Kappa Architecture |
|---|---|---|
| **Pipelines** | Two (batch + speed) | One (stream only) |
| **Reprocessing** | Batch layer reprocesses from raw | Replay Kafka topic |
| **Complexity** | High (two codebases) | Lower (single codebase) |
| **Latency** | Speed layer low, batch high | Uniformly low |
| **Use Case** | Historical + real-time analytics | Pure event-driven analytics |

**Lambda вҖ” Advantage:** Robust, fault-tolerant batch layer. **Limitation:** Code duplication.
**Kappa вҖ” Advantage:** Simpler ops, single source of truth. **Limitation:** Requires durable log (Kafka).

**(b) Data Lifecycle & MQTT QoS (7 Marks):**

**Lifecycle stages** (as in SECTION 4.3): Acquired вҶ’ Ingested вҶ’ Hot вҶ’ Warm вҶ’ Cold вҶ’ Purged. Each transition must be **time-bound** and **policy-driven**.

**MQTT QoS Levels:**
- **QoS 0** вҖ” At most once. Suitable for non-critical telemetry (e.g., ambient temperature).
- **QoS 1** вҖ” At least once. Default for most industrial telemetry. Handles duplicates.
- **QoS 2** вҖ” Exactly once. Mandatory for billing-grade, safety, or regulatory data (e.g., pharmaceutical batch records).

A **state transition diagram** is required for full marks (refer SECTION 4.3).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Architecture:** Always remember the **five stages** вҖ” Acquisition, Ingestion, Storage, Processing, Consumption.
- **Protocols:** **MQTT (1883/8883)** for lightweight IIoT telemetry; **OPC UA** for industrial interoperability; **AMQP** for enterprise messaging; **CoAP** for constrained devices.
- **Storage Selection Rule:** Hot = TSDB, Warm = Object Store, Cold = Archive (Glacier).
- **Lambda vs Kappa:** Lambda = dual pipeline (batch + stream); Kappa = stream-only with log replay.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Little's Law: $L = \lambda W$
  - Daily volume: $V_{day} = \lambda \cdot s_{avg} \cdot 86400$
  - SLA downtime: $D_{yr} = (1 - A) \cdot 525600$
  - Storage sizing: $C_{s} = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot s_{avg} \cdot T_{ret}}{\rho_c}$
- **Cloud Platforms to Name:** AWS IoT Core + Timestream + S3 + Kinesis; Azure IoT Hub + Time Series Insights + Data Lake; Google Cloud IoT Core + BigQuery + Pub/Sub.
- **SLA Targets:** **99.9%** = 8.77 h/yr; **99.95%** = 4.38 h/yr; **99.99%** = 52.6 min/yr.
- **Always draw a block diagram** in 14-mark questions вҖ” it is a guaranteed 3-mark item.
- **Mention real cloud services** by name to score in the top valuation band.
- **State units** explicitly in all numerical answers.
- **Compression ratio $\rho_c$** in IIoT is typically **5:1 to 10:1** for telemetry using columnar formats (Parquet, ORC).
- **Closed-loop control** from cloud to PLC is emerging but should be used only with **edge-side safety interlocks** to avoid network-failure hazards.
